reports.title: Daily Briefing — Friday, July 3
tldr: Web-design play of the day: delete your auto-grow-textarea JavaScript — field-sizing: content just went Baseline (works in every major browser), so the input shrinks and grows to fit its text with one line of CSS, for ALL users. On Jun 16 2026 Firefox 152 shipped the property — the last of the four engines (it was already in Chrome/Edge 123+ since 2024 and Safari 26.2 since Dec 2025) — which flips it to Baseline Newly Available: safe to use without an @supports guard or a JS fallback. Set field-sizing: content on a <textarea> and it grows row-by-row as you type (no more measuring scrollHeight in a resize handler); set it on a text <input> and it hugs the text like a chip/tag field; set it on a <select> and it stops reserving width for the longest option and sizes to the chosen one. The default is fixed (today's behaviour), so nothing changes until you opt in — and because it's Baseline you get to remove the dependency (react-textarea-autosize and friends), not just progressively enhance. Run-it-today caveat: always pair it with a min- and max- size so an empty field isn't a sliver and a wall of text scrolls instead of eating the page. This is the styleable-form-control leg of the modern-CSS-primitives thread, and the FIRST one in that thread that's fully Baseline (delete-the-JS, not enhance-only). Card: card-webdesign-field-sizing.

Email play: you already watch Gmail's opinion of your sending — now watch Microsoft's. Microsoft SNDS is the free dashboard that shows how Outlook/Hotmail rate your sending IPs, and it just changed enough (Jun 2026) that it's worth (re)claiming today. SNDS (Smart Network Data Services) is Microsoft's counterpart to Gmail Postmaster Tools — but it's IP-based (you register the sending IPs you own/authorize), where Gmail is domain-based, so it's the missing half of your deliverability picture for the ~350M Outlook/Hotmail/Live inboxes. It surfaces four things per IP: a plain filter status (green = healthy, yellow = warning, red = likely junked/blocked), spam-trap hits (even one flags list-hygiene rot), your complaint rate trend, and sending volume. The Jun 2026 changes are the fresh hook: the portal moved to a new URL on Jun 8 (old bookmarks/hardcoded links now dead), there's a new REST API with OAuth 2.0 for pulling data, automated CSV links now expire after 30 days, and the JMRP complaint feed is now ARF-standardised and privacy-trimmed (selected headers only, no message body). Run-it-today: register/verify your IPs at SNDS, add the JMRP feedback loop, and check the filter status the same day you check Gmail Postmaster. Free, and it maps a red status straight to a fix. Card: card-email-microsoft-snds (the Microsoft leg of the deliverability thread, alongside the Gmail-Postmaster and DMARCbis cards).

Paid play: Performance Max stops being a total black box — Google Ads added Channel Diagnostics, so you can finally see WHY a channel (YouTube, Search, Gmail, Maps…) isn't serving and fix the input. Reported Jul 2 2026 (rolling out, not yet in every account): inside a PMax campaign under Insights & Reports → Channel performance, a new Channel Diagnostics view aggregates diagnostic data across all channels and per individual channel — flagging the fixable reasons a channel is limited or dark: missing or rejected assets, an unrealistic target (tCPA set too low / tROAS set too high), weak Ad Strength, audience-signal or conversion-tracking problems. The honest read: if a channel shows no issues but still isn't serving, that's PMax choosing higher-ROI placements in the moment — not a bug. This is a rare case of Google handing an operator a window into the automation (pairs with the same 'keep a manual hand on the wheel' lesson as the AI-Max experiment). Run-it-today: open Channel Diagnostics, fix every flagged missing/rejected asset and pull any fantasy target back toward trailing actuals — then re-judge on cost-per-outcome. (Also promoted from the paid scan: Reddit made Split Testing generally available to all advertisers Jul 3 — native user-level A/B in Ads Manager, one variable changed, a winner declared at 65% confidence over 2–6 weeks; $1,000/day minimum. Cards: card-paid-pmax-channel-diagnostics, card-paid-reddit-split-testing.)

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked): the model layer is hardening into a governed, geopolitical thing even as the hype gets a reality-check from the top. Two dated reads (US markets are closed today for Independence Day — observed Fri Jul 3, NYSE/Nasdaq and bonds shut, reopen Mon Jul 6 — so there's no fresh print, only the setup). (1) Anthropic is closing the loopholes Chinese firms used to reach Claude (FT, Jul 2–3): Ant Group ran corporate Claude accounts through a Singapore entity; ByteDance reimbursed engineers for personal subscriptions accessed over VPNs; others routed through cloud providers (incl. Azure) and 'transfer-station' relay services. It doesn't break US or Chinese law — it breaks Anthropic's terms of service (which bar China-controlled entities) — and Anthropic is now tracking user time-zones, killing relays and shutting access, all while it marches toward an IPO (confidential S-1 filed Jun 1; The Information: bankers hired Freshfields). (2) Zuckerberg's candor (Reuters exclusive, Jul 2 town hall): agentic AI 'over at least the last four months hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected,' the reorg (May's ~10% cuts, ~7,000 moved to AI teams) wasn't as 'clean' as hoped, and execs were 'super optimistic' about tools like Claude Code in Jan/Feb — even as Meta spends up to ~$145B on infra this year (he still expects bigger payoff in 3–6 months). For us this is a climate signal: (a) treat model access as a compliance surface — know WHICH model, WHERE it runs, and that your usage fits ToS/jurisdiction, and build client AI for portability so a policy shock is a config change; (b) the loudest AI booster conceding agents are slower than hoped is your license to scope agents narrowly and keep a human in the loop — don't sell autonomy you can't ship. Our open Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20] stays open and under pressure from Thursday's chip rout; we judge it at the window.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is see the machine, then decide. field-sizing lets the browser do the measuring you used to script — so you delete the JS and just cap the min/max. SNDS and Channel Diagnostics both hand you a gauge that used to be hidden — Microsoft's opinion of your mail, and PMax's reason a channel is dark — so the work shifts from guessing to reading a status and fixing the named input. And the landscape rhymes: Anthropic is instrumenting who's really using its model, and even Zuckerberg is reading his own dashboard honestly and saying 'slower than we hoped.' The edge isn't more automation — it's a clear gauge and a hand on the wheel: know which model you're on, watch every inbox's verdict, read the diagnostic, and promise only what the meter says you can ship.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: delete the auto-grow-textarea JavaScript — `field-sizing: content` reached Baseline (Firefox 152, Jun 16), so a form control sizes to its own text in one line of CSS, for every user

blocks.sub: Jun 16 2026: Firefox 152 shipped `field-sizing` — the LAST of the four engines (Chrome/Edge 123+ since 2024, Safari 26.2 since Dec 2025) — flipping it to Baseline Newly Available. `field-sizing: content` makes a <textarea> grow row-by-row as you type, a text <input> hug its text (chip/tag fields), and a <select> size to the CHOSEN option instead of reserving width for the longest one. Default is `fixed` (unchanged), so it's opt-in; because it's Baseline you DELETE the JS autosize dependency, not just enhance. Always pair with a min- and max- size so an empty field isn't a sliver and a wall of text scrolls.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: making a <textarea> grow with its content has, for years, meant JavaScript — a library like react-textarea-autosize, or a hand-rolled input handler that reads scrollHeight and re-sets the height on every keystroke (and fights it on paste, on resize, on font-load). The CSS field-sizing property deletes that whole chore. Set field-sizing: content and the browser sizes the control to what's actually in it: a textarea grows line-by-line as you type (and shrinks back when you delete); a text input shrinkwraps to its value, which is exactly what a tag/chip/inline-edit field wants; and a <select> stops reserving space for its widest option and sizes to the option you've picked. It applies to text-type inputs (text, search, url, tel, email, password, number, even file), <textarea>, and <select>; the two values are fixed (the default — today's behaviour, so nothing breaks until you opt in) and content. On Jun 16 2026 Firefox 152 became the last major engine to ship it, joining Chrome/Edge 123+ (Mar 2024) and Safari 26.2 (Dec 2025) — which moves it to Baseline Newly Available. Why that word matters for us: unlike the Chromium-only CSS primitives we've tracked (carousel, sibling-index, scroll-triggered animations), where the play is 'ship it as decorative progressive enhancement,' a Baseline feature is one you can lean on for all users — so this isn't 'enhance,' it's 'delete the dependency.' On the builds we ship — comment boxes, chat composers, support forms, inline-edit cells, tag inputs — that's a real bundle-size and bug-surface win for one line of CSS.
blocks.doIt: (1) Opt in where auto-size helps: add field-sizing: content to the specific controls that should grow — comment/message <textarea>s, tag/inline-edit text <input>s, and content-width <select>s (leave the rest on the default fixed). (2) Always cap it: pair it with sizing bounds — e.g. textarea { field-sizing: content; min-height: 3lh; max-height: 12lh; } and input { field-sizing: content; min-width: 6ch; max-width: 100%; } — so an empty field isn't a one-character sliver and a huge paste scrolls instead of pushing your layout around (note: size/rows/cols attributes are ignored once content is on, so control size via CSS). (3) Delete the JS: remove the autosize library / scrollHeight resize handler for these fields — the browser now does it natively and correctly (including on paste and font-load). (4) Sanity-check the edges: confirm your min-height covers a placeholder line, test a very long single word (add overflow-wrap: anywhere if needed), and check RTL/zoom. (5) No @supports needed for the four current-version engines (it's Baseline) — but if you must support older browser versions, wrap the opt-in in @supports (field-sizing: content) and they simply keep the normal fixed-size control. Card: card-webdesign-field-sizing.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Baseline primitive (multiple independent confirmations) — the web-features 'Baseline' dataset dates it Newly Available 2026-06-16 (Firefox 152 the last engine; Chrome/Edge 123, Safari 26.2), MDN documents the values (fixed default / content) and the applicable elements (text inputs, <textarea>, <select>), Stefan Judis's write-up shows it's 'more than textareas' (inputs + selects size too), and Can I Use/web.dev track the support state. Honest scope: Baseline Newly Available, not yet 'Widely Available' (that milestone is dated ~2028) — meaning current versions of all four engines have it, but very old versions don't, so guard behind @supports only if your traffic includes pre-2024 Chrome / pre-26.2 Safari / pre-152 Firefox. Sizing must be bounded via CSS (attributes are ignored under content). Advances thread-modern-css-primitives — and it's the FIRST leg of that thread that's fully Baseline (a delete-the-JS, not enhance-only, feature — same class as the Popover+anchor overlay trio). Logged a prediction (a mainstream UI/component library or CSS framework documents/ships field-sizing: content as its default textarea/input auto-resize, dropping a JS autosize dependency). Sources: web-features explorer (Baseline), MDN, Stefan Judis, Can I Use.

sections.h: Email — run it today: claim Microsoft SNDS, the free dashboard for how Outlook/Hotmail rate your sending IPs — the missing Microsoft half of the deliverability picture (and it changed in Jun 2026)

blocks.sub: SNDS (Smart Network Data Services) is Microsoft's free counterpart to Gmail Postmaster Tools — but IP-based (you register the sending IPs you own/authorize), where Gmail is domain-based, so it's the missing half for ~350M Outlook/Hotmail/Live inboxes. Per IP it shows a plain filter status (green healthy / yellow warning / red junked-or-blocked), spam-trap hits, complaint-rate trend, and volume. Jun 2026 changes: portal moved to a NEW URL Jun 8 (old links dead), a new REST API with OAuth 2.0, automated CSV links now expire after 30 days, and the JMRP complaint feed is ARF-standardised + privacy-trimmed (headers only, no body). Run-it-today: register/verify your IPs, add the JMRP feedback loop, and check filter status the same day you check Gmail Postmaster.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: our deliverability thread has taught the Gmail side hard — Postmaster Tools v2 and its 'Deliverability analysis' verdict (card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis), DMARCbis np=reject (card-email-dmarcbis-np), the inbox-placement seed test, and the re-engagement/sunset flow. The gap in all of that is Microsoft: Outlook.com, Hotmail and Live are hundreds of millions of inboxes (Microsoft cites 350M+), and they're graded by a separate system you can watch for free — Smart Network Data Services (SNDS). The key difference from Gmail: SNDS is IP-based, not domain-based. You register the sending IP addresses you own or are authorised to monitor (your ESP's dedicated IPs, or your own), Microsoft verifies you via the network's admin contact, and then per IP you get four plain signals: a colour-coded filter status (green = healthy / your mail is treated well; yellow = on watch; red = being junked or blocked), spam-trap hits (Microsoft-run trap addresses your list touched — even a handful means hygiene rot or a bought/scraped segment), your complaint rate over time, and sending volume. Pair it with JMRP (the Junk Mail Reporting Program — Microsoft's feedback loop) and you also get told which of your messages recipients marked as junk, so you can suppress those addresses fast. Why it matters now, and why it's a today job: Microsoft changed SNDS in June 2026 — the portal moved to a new URL on Jun 8 (any hardcoded link or old bookmark is dead), there's a new REST API with OAuth 2.0 (with date/IP filtering) for pulling the data programmatically, the automated CSV download links now expire after 30 days (so 'set it and forget it' scripts silently go stale), and the JMRP complaint reports are now ARF-standardised and privacy-trimmed — selected headers only, no message body. Net for us: if you send any real volume to consumers, half your audience likely sits behind Microsoft's filter and you've probably been flying blind there — this is a free gauge you can stand up today, and the June changes are the nudge to (re)claim access and fix whatever's pulling data.
blocks.doIt: (1) Request access at Microsoft's SNDS site (sendersupport.olc.protection.outlook.com/snds) and enter the sending IP(s) you want to monitor — your own, or ask your ESP which dedicated IPs carry your mail. (2) Verify ownership: Microsoft emails the network's administrative contact for that IP range to authorise you — coordinate with your ESP/infra owner so the approval isn't missed. (3) Turn on JMRP (the Junk Mail Reporting Program) for the same IPs so complaints flow back to you; wire those complainers straight into your suppression list. (4) Read the gauge weekly (red daily): treat red filter status as a fire (mail is being junked/blocked — pause aggressive sends, check auth + recent list changes), yellow as a warning to investigate, and any spam-trap hit as a list-hygiene alarm (prune the stale/unengaged segment behind it — this is where the re-engagement/sunset flow, card-email-reengagement-sunset, pays off). (5) Fix your June-2026 plumbing: update any dead bookmarks to the new URL, move automated pulls to the new REST API (OAuth 2.0) or account for the 30-day CSV link expiry, and update complaint-mapping that assumed a full message body (JMRP now sends headers only). (6) Check it beside Gmail: make 'read SNDS + Gmail Postmaster' one recurring deliverability ritual so you see BOTH big consumer filters' verdicts, not just Google's. Card: card-email-microsoft-snds.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3+) — Litmus ('What Is Microsoft SNDS—And Why Your Deliverability Strategy Depends on It', which details the Jun 2026 changes: new URL Jun 8, REST API/OAuth 2.0, 30-day CSV expiry, ARF-standardised/header-only JMRP), Microsoft's own SNDS site (the primary tool + IP-registration flow), and independent how-to guides (Iterable, Twilio, Mailtrap) confirming the filter-status colours, spam-trap/complaint/volume signals and the IP-based (vs Gmail's domain-based) model. Honest scope: SNDS grades IP reputation, so it's most actionable on dedicated IPs — on a shared-IP ESP plan you may not be able to register the IP yourself (ask the ESP for its SNDS view), and it complements, not replaces, domain-level signals (DMARC/Postmaster). The Jun 2026 API/URL/JMRP specifics come from Litmus + practitioner guides; verify exact endpoints against Microsoft's current SNDS/JMRP pages before scripting. Advances the deliverability thread — the Microsoft/Outlook leg, distinct from the Gmail-Postmaster verdict (card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis), the DMARCbis auth fix (card-email-dmarcbis-np), and the IPR seed test (card-email-inbox-placement-audit). Logged a prediction (an ESP/deliverability vendor ingests SNDS via the new REST API and surfaces Microsoft filter status beside Gmail's verdict as a default panel). Sources: Litmus, Microsoft SNDS, Iterable, Twilio.

sections.h: Paid — run it today: Performance Max stops being a black box — Google Ads' new Channel Diagnostics shows WHY a channel isn't serving so you fix the input (plus Reddit opens native A/B Split Testing to all advertisers)

blocks.sub: Jul 2 2026 (rolling out, not yet in every account): inside a PMax campaign under Insights & Reports → Channel performance, a new Channel Diagnostics view aggregates diagnostic data across all channels AND per channel (Search, YouTube, Display, Discover, Gmail, Maps), flagging the fixable reasons a channel is limited or dark — missing/rejected assets, an unrealistic target (tCPA too low / tROAS too high), weak Ad Strength, audience-signal or conversion-tracking issues. Honest read: a channel with NO issues that still isn't serving just means PMax is bidding elsewhere for higher ROI right now. A rare window into the automation — fix the flagged inputs, then judge on cost-per-outcome.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Performance Max's long-standing complaint is opacity — it spends across Search, YouTube, Display, Discover, Gmail and Maps and, until recently, told you almost nothing about which channel did what or why. Google has been chipping at that (the Channel performance report broke results out by channel in 2025); the fresh piece, reported Jul 2 2026, is Channel Diagnostics — a view under Insights & Reports → Channel performance that shows diagnostic data both aggregated across all channels and for a specific channel, surfacing the fixable reasons a channel is limited or not serving. The named culprits are exactly the inputs you control: missing or rejected assets (e.g. no vertical video, so YouTube/Discover can't serve; a disapproved image), an unrealistic target (a tCPA set too low or a tROAS set too high that starves delivery), weak Ad Strength from thin/low-quality assets, or audience-signal / conversion-tracking misconfiguration. Crucially, Google is explicit about the honest case: if a channel shows no issues yet isn't serving, that isn't a fault — it means PMax is currently bidding on other channels that return more, in the moment. Why it matters for us: this is a genuine window into the black box. Instead of arguing about whether PMax 'wasted' budget on Display or 'never tried' YouTube, you open the diagnostics, see the actual blocker (usually a missing asset ratio or a fantasy target), and fix a concrete input. It's the same operator stance our paid thread keeps returning to — the platform runs the auction, but you own the inputs and the guardrails — now with a real gauge attached. Note it's still rolling out (not every account sees it yet).
blocks.doIt: (1) Open the gauge: in a PMax campaign go to Insights & Reports → Channel performance and look for the Channel Diagnostics view (if it's not there yet, your account is in the rollout queue — check back). (2) Fix rejected/missing assets first: for any channel flagged, supply what's missing — most often a vertical (9:16) video and enough image ratios for YouTube/Discover/Gmail, and resolve any disapprovals; thin asset groups are the #1 reason a channel can't serve. (3) Sanity-check your targets: if diagnostics flag an unrealistic target, pull a too-low tCPA up or a too-high tROAS down toward your trailing actuals (ties into the Jun-16 bidding-recalibration play, card-paid-google-bidding-recalibration) — a fantasy target silently throttles delivery. (4) Verify the plumbing: confirm the audience signal is populated and conversion tracking is firing (a broken signal shows up here before it shows up as bad performance). (5) Read the 'no issues but dark' case correctly: don't force a channel that PMax is skipping for ROI reasons — that's the algorithm working; spend your effort on flagged inputs, then judge on cost-per-outcome, not channel-by-channel vanity. (6) Make it a weekly triage: a 60-second diagnostics scan per PMax campaign catches a disapproved asset or a starving target before it burns a week of budget. Card: card-paid-pmax-channel-diagnostics.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Search Engine Land ('Google adds Channel Diagnostics to Performance Max', Jul 2) + Search Engine Roundtable (same-day report, noting it's not fully live for everyone) + Google Ads Help (the Channel performance report + its troubleshooting docs, which enumerate the missing/rejected-asset and target/Ad-Strength causes); Google's own blog documents the underlying channel-performance reporting. Honest scope: staged rollout — many accounts won't see Channel Diagnostics yet; and it's a diagnostic surface, not new control (PMax still allocates across channels automatically — you fix inputs, not force placements). Advances the google-ads platform-AI-defaults thread on the transparency axis (Google handing back visibility, cf. the AI-Max experiment + bidding-recalibration cards). Logged a prediction (Channel Diagnostics reaches general availability / adds asset-level channel breakdowns). Sources: Search Engine Land, Search Engine Roundtable, Google Ads Help.

blocks.sub: Jul 3 2026: Reddit made Split Testing generally available to ALL advertisers (after a partner beta). It's native user-level A/B inside Reddit Ads Manager — split your audience, run two flights with exactly ONE variable changed (creative, audience, or bid), and Reddit declares a winner at 65% confidence; tests run 2–6 weeks. Supported objectives: Awareness/Reach, Traffic, Conversions, Shopping, App Installs, Video Views. Minimum $1,000/day spend. Reddit's beta data: 4 of 5 tests found a winning variant by ROAS.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Reddit rolled its Split Testing tool out to all advertisers on Jul 3 2026 (previously a limited partner beta). It's a proper controlled A/B test built into Ads Manager: it splits your available audience at the user level (so the two groups don't overlap and contaminate each other), runs two flights that differ by exactly one variable, and calls a winner at 65% confidence; a test runs between two and six weeks. It works for the objectives most brands actually run — Awareness/Reach, Traffic, Conversions, Shopping, App Installs, Video Views — with a $1,000/day minimum spend. Why it matters for us: if Reddit is (or could be) in the mix for a client, this replaces the junk-science of eyeballing two campaigns that ran at different times to different overlapping audiences. Change one thing, let the platform hold everything else constant, and get a clean read. Reddit says 4 of 5 beta tests surfaced a winning variant by ROAS — i.e. most tests were decisive. Keep the discipline: one variable per test, let it reach the confidence call, and remember 65% is a directional bar (good for creative/audience iteration, not a bet-the-quarter certainty).
blocks.doIt: (1) In Reddit Ads Manager create a Split Test and pick a supported objective. (2) Change exactly one variable between the two flights — creative or audience or bid/placement — never several at once, or you can't attribute the win. (3) Confirm you can clear the $1,000/day minimum and let it run its 2–6 week course to the 65%-confidence winner (don't peek-and-kill early). (4) Roll the winner forward into the scaled campaign, then start the next single-variable test — treat it as a standing iteration loop, not a one-off. Card: card-paid-reddit-split-testing.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Social Media Today ('Reddit rolls out split testing to all advertisers', Jul 3) + MediaPost (Jul 3, 'Reddit advertisers can compare ad variants via A/B testing') + Social Samosa, all reporting the same GA, the user-level split, one-variable/65%-confidence/2–6-week mechanics, the supported objectives, the $1,000/day minimum and the '4 of 5 found a winner' beta stat. Honest scope: the $1,000/day floor puts this out of reach for small budgets, and 65% confidence is a directional bar — treat it as iteration guidance, not statistical proof. Advances the paid experimentation thread (a native, platform-level A/B primitive beyond Meta/Google). Sources: Social Media Today, MediaPost, Social Samosa.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked): the model layer hardens into a governed, geopolitical thing (Anthropic closes the China loopholes) even as Zuckerberg concedes AI agents are slower than hoped

blocks.sub: Two dated reads (US markets CLOSED today — Independence Day observed Fri Jul 3; NYSE/Nasdaq + bonds shut, reopen Mon Jul 6 — so no fresh print). (1) Anthropic is closing the loopholes Chinese firms used to reach Claude (FT, Jul 2–3): Ant Group via a Singapore entity's corporate accounts, ByteDance reimbursing engineers for personal subs over VPNs, plus cloud providers (incl. Azure) and 'transfer-station' relays — legal, but against Anthropic's ToS (which bar China-controlled entities); Anthropic now tracks time-zones, kills relays and cuts access, all as it heads to an IPO (confidential S-1 filed Jun 1; The Information: Freshfields hired). (2) Zuckerberg (Reuters, Jul 2 town hall): agentic AI 'hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected' over 4 months, the reorg wasn't 'clean,' execs were 'super optimistic' about Claude Code in Jan/Feb — even amid ~$145B infra spend. Landscape/intake — the craft payoff is the model-portability + provenance + human-in-the-loop framing.
blocks.p: What moved (cross-checked; treat single-sourced figures as directional): the throughline is that the model layer is becoming a governed, geopolitical surface at the same moment the loudest boosters are dialing back the timeline. (1) Anthropic is closing the China access loopholes. Per the Financial Times (Jul 2–3, via Investing.com and others), Chinese firms had been reaching Claude despite Anthropic's terms by: Ant Group issuing corporate Claude accounts tied to a Singapore-based entity; ByteDance reimbursing engineers for personal Claude subscriptions used over VPNs; and others routing through cloud providers (including Microsoft Azure) and 'transfer-station' relay services. These workarounds reportedly don't violate US or Chinese law — they violate Anthropic's terms of service, which bar Chinese companies and foreign entities they control. Anthropic is responding by tracking user time-zones for suspicious patterns, targeting relay services and shutting down identified access — and it's doing all this as it marches toward a public listing (it confidentially filed an S-1 on Jun 1 2026; The Information reported its bankers hired Freshfields to advise on the IPO). (2) Zuckerberg gave the hype a public haircut. At a Jul 2 internal town hall (Reuters exclusive), he said agentic AI development 'over at least the last four months hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected,' admitted Meta's reorg — May's ~10% layoffs and ~7,000 staff moved to AI teams — wasn't as 'clean' as it could have been, and that leadership had been 'super optimistic' about tools like Claude Code when planning in Jan/Feb; he still expects bigger benefits in 3–6 months, even as Meta spends up to ~$145B on infrastructure this year. Why it matters for us — this is a climate signal, not a trade (and there's no US print today anyway; markets are shut for the holiday), and it sharpens two operating habits: (a) treat model access as a compliance surface — for client work, know which model you're on, where it runs, and that your usage fits the vendor's ToS and the client's jurisdiction; build client AI for portability (card-ai-tooling-model-portability) so a policy or availability shock is a config change, not a rebuild; (b) when the industry's biggest AI spender concedes agents are slower than hoped, take it as license to scope agents narrowly and keep a human in the loop — sell measurable, bounded automation, not autonomy you can't yet ship. On the open Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [news, made Jun 8, due ~Jul 20]: Thursday's chip rout (Samsung −9.06%, SK Hynix −14.57%) keeps the basket under pressure; with markets closed today we hold the call open and judge it at the window. No news card (the landscape is intake; the craft payoff is the framings above). Logged a new prediction (Anthropic's access-tightening extends within ~8 weeks). Sources: FT via Investing.com, Archyde (FT summary), Reuters via StreetInsider, TechCrunch, Zacks (Anthropic S-1 timeline).

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, July 2
tldr: Web-dev play of the day: stop maintaining a separate box for the one service Vercel couldn't build — drop in a Dockerfile.vercel and Vercel runs your container next to your frontend. Announced Jun 30 2026: add a file named Dockerfile.vercel, run vercel deploy (or just git push), and Vercel builds the image, stores it, deploys it and autoscales it on Fluid compute — billed on Active CPU (you pay for execution time, so idle is near-free), with a fresh preview URL per commit and built-in logs/traces/metrics. No local Docker daemon, no registry to run, no cluster to babysit. The point: the services that used to force a second always-on host — a Go/Rails/Spring Boot API, anything needing a system library like FFmpeg or Chromium, or a framework Vercel doesn't auto-detect — now live alongside the rest of the app. Run-it-today: your container must listen on $PORT, speak HTTP, and stay stateless (push session/room state to a Marketplace backing service like Redis). Joins the 'the platform absorbed the backend' web-dev thread (WebSockets, durable workflows). Card: card-webdev-vercel-dockerfile.

AI-tooling play: re-point your model router — Claude Sonnet 5 (claude-sonnet-5, shipped Jun 30 2026) lands near-Opus agentic quality at mid-tier prices, and it's already the default on Free/Pro and in Claude Code. Anthropic's pitch, cross-checked: Sonnet 5's higher-effort performance is 'close to Opus 4.8' on many agentic tasks (an independent agentic-coding read puts it at 63.2% vs Opus 4.8 69.2% and Sonnet 4.6 58.1%) at $3/$15 per M tokens standard — ~40% under Opus 4.8's $5/$25 — with an intro $2/$10 through Aug 31 2026, plus up to 90% off with prompt caching / 50% with batch. Run-it-today: don't reprice your stack on the headline — re-run your standing eval of 3–5 of your real tasks against Sonnet 5, then move the bulk/agentic/long-horizon jobs to it and reserve Opus for the hardest ~10% that fails the eval. Resolves our Jun 6 'a Sonnet refresh reprices the mid-tier' call ✅ (honest caveat: it's Sonnet 5, a full version bump, not the guessed 4.8 point release). Card: card-ai-tooling-sonnet-5-default (pairs with the model-router + two-gate-picker cards).

CRO & AOV — answering a standing team Ask ('the best ONE thing for CRO and AOV?'): let your own numbers name the leak, then pull one lever — a free-shipping threshold pegged to your modal order value, with a live progress bar. There is no universal 'one thing'; there's the one thing your data says leaks most, and you can decide it in under an hour. Hand Claude/ChatGPT your last-90-day order export + GA4 funnel and have it compute your AOV and your modal (most common) order value, then rank the biggest leak. If it's basket size, set a free-shipping threshold just above your modal order value (roughly 15–25% over current AOV) and add a dynamic 'You're $X from free shipping' progress bar — the goal-gradient nudge lifts threshold conversion ~15–25% over static messaging. If it's checkout drop-off, fix friction first: guest checkout, fewer fields, full cost shown early (unexpected extra costs drive ~48% of abandonment — Baymard). The discipline: measure AOV, conversion AND margin for 3–4 weeks — a higher AOV you fund with absorbed shipping can be a net loss. Card: card-cro-ai-diagnose-one-lever (updated).

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked): Big AI is now a political-and-regulatory entity, and the 'show-me-the-profit' selloff is back in the chips. Three dated reads. (1) Google lost its final appeal — the EU's top court (Court of Justice) upheld the €4.1B (~$4.7B) Android antitrust fine (Jul 2), ending an eight-year fight over pre-installing Search/Chrome. (2) OpenAI proposed handing the US government a ~5% stake (FT, Jul 2) — worth ~$42.6B at its ~$852B valuation — floating that Washington take 5% of each leading US lab via a sovereign-wealth vehicle (it's not clear anyone would agree). (3) The chip rout resumed: Samsung closed −9.06%, SK Hynix −14.57% as Korea's Kospi followed an overnight Nasdaq slide on AI-profit doubts. Our open Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20] is now under real pressure — we judge it at the window and treat intraday prints as directional. For us this stays a climate signal: lead pitches with cost-per-outcome + a spend ceiling, build client AI for portability, and factor policy/ownership risk into single-vendor dependence. (Adjacent: Cloudflare's new x402 Monetization Gateway — charge any API/data/MCP call in stablecoins — advances the agentic-payments thread.)

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is collapse the stack, then re-decide on evidence. Vercel collapses your extra backend into one deploy — so the only question left is 'does this container listen on $PORT and hold no state.' Sonnet 5 collapses the price gap to Opus — so don't trust the headline, re-run your eval and let the numbers move the router. The AOV lever collapses to whatever your order data says leaks most — not someone else's case study. And the market's message is that same discipline aimed at you: it stopped paying for potential and now audits the profit — so quote cost-per-outcome, cap the spend, and keep a fallback you can run yourself.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: deploy any container on Vercel with a Dockerfile.vercel — the last always-on box (FFmpeg/Chromium, a Go/Rails/Spring API, an unsupported framework) moves onto Fluid compute next to your frontend

blocks.sub: Jun 30 2026: add a file named Dockerfile.vercel, run `vercel deploy` (or git push), and Vercel builds → stores → deploys → autoscales the image on Fluid compute, billed on Active CPU (pay for execution time, idle near-free), with a preview URL per commit and built-in logs/traces/metrics — no local daemon, registry or cluster. Requirements: listen on $PORT, speak HTTP, stay stateless (externalize persistence to a Marketplace backing service). Kills the need for a second always-on host for the one service Vercel couldn't auto-build.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: until now, a Vercel app that needed a real long-running container — an API in a framework Vercel doesn't auto-detect (Go, Rails, Spring Boot), or anything that shells out to a system binary like FFmpeg (video/audio transcoding) or headless Chromium (PDF/screenshot generation) — had to live on a separate box (Render, Railway, Fly, a VM) that you provisioned, secured and paid for around the clock, then wired back to the frontend. On Jun 30 2026 Vercel closed that gap: drop a Dockerfile.vercel into the project and it treats your container like any other deploy. The CLI (vercel deploy) or a git push builds the image, stores it in your project's registry, deploys it, and autoscales it — and it runs on Fluid compute, the same platform behind Vercel's frontends, billed on Active CPU: you pay for the CPU your code actually burns, so a waiting/idle container costs next to nothing (bidirectional autoscaling, fast boot via snapshot streaming). You get the normal Vercel wrapper for free — a preview deployment per commit, plus built-in logs, traces and metrics. Why it matters for us: this deletes an entire class of infrastructure from client builds. The 'we need a tiny separate service just for thumbnails / PDFs / a legacy API' problem — the thing that quietly adds a second host, a second bill, a second thing to patch — collapses into one repo, one deploy, one platform. It's the same 'the platform absorbed the backend' move we tracked with Vercel WebSockets (realtime with no separate server) and durable Workflows, now extended to arbitrary containers.
blocks.doIt: (1) Write a normal Dockerfile for the service, but name it Dockerfile.vercel at the project root. (2) Listen on $PORT — your server must bind the port Vercel injects (defaults to 80) and speak plain HTTP; that's the one hard contract. (3) Keep the container stateless — treat the filesystem as ephemeral; move sessions, uploads, room/presence state and any persistence to a Marketplace backing service (Redis/Postgres/blob), because instances scale up and down. (4) Deploy: run vercel deploy for the first build, then let every git push rebuild the image and hand you a fresh preview URL. (5) Use it for the right jobs first: a media pipeline that needs FFmpeg, a screenshot/PDF service on Chromium, a Go/Rails/Spring API, or an app you just want to 'bring exactly as it runs' — not for work a normal Vercel Function already handles. (6) Watch the meter: Active-CPU billing rewards idle-cheap, bursty workloads; a container pinned at 100% CPU 24/7 is the case where a fixed-price box can still be cheaper — cost it against your real traffic shape. Card: card-webdev-vercel-dockerfile.
blocks.note: Corroboration: single-vendor primary (one authoritative source is enough for a shipped-product how-to) — Vercel's own blog ('Run any Dockerfile on Vercel', Jun 30) documents the Dockerfile.vercel filename, the build→store→deploy→autoscale flow, Fluid-compute + Active-CPU billing, the $PORT/HTTP/stateless contract and the FFmpeg/Chromium/unsupported-framework use cases; a Vercel Community thread flags that the intro post is light on some end-to-end steps (verify exact port/health-check behaviour against the docs before you rely on it). Honest scope: stateless only (persistence goes to a backing service), and Active-CPU pricing can lose to a fixed box for a constantly-CPU-bound workload — match it to bursty/idle-heavy services. Advances thread-platform-absorbs-backend (related: card-webdev-vercel-websockets, card-webdev-vercel-cancelable-jobs — same Fluid/Active-CPU platform). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major deploy platform ships git-push 'any Dockerfile' with scale-to-zero/per-active-CPU billing). Sources: Vercel Blog, Vercel Community.

sections.h: AI tooling — run it today: Claude Sonnet 5 lands near-Opus agentic quality at ~40% lower price (and it's your new default) — re-run your eval and re-point the router, don't reprice on the headline

blocks.sub: Jun 30 2026: Anthropic shipped Claude Sonnet 5 (claude-sonnet-5) — the most agentic Sonnet yet (plans, uses browser/terminal tools, runs autonomously). Higher-effort performance is 'close to Opus 4.8' on many tasks (independent agentic-coding read: 63.2% vs Opus 4.8 69.2%, Sonnet 4.6 58.1%) at $3/$15 per M tokens standard (~40% under Opus $5/$25), intro $2/$10 through Aug 31 2026, up to 90% off with prompt caching. It's the DEFAULT on Free/Pro and available in Claude Code + the API (also AWS, Microsoft Foundry). The play is the router move, not the headline.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: on Jun 30 2026 Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 (model id claude-sonnet-5), positioned as the most agentic Sonnet to date — it can make plans, drive tools like browsers and terminals, and run autonomously at a level that recently needed a larger, pricier model. The headline number that matters to an operator is the price-to-capability step: Anthropic says Sonnet 5's higher-effort performance is 'close to that of Opus 4.8' on many agentic tasks, and independent write-ups put its agentic-coding score at 63.2% — between Sonnet 4.6 (58.1%) and Opus 4.8 (69.2%) — at $3 in / $15 out per million tokens standard, about 40% below Opus 4.8 ($5/$25), with an introductory $2/$10 through Aug 31 2026 and the usual up-to-90%-off prompt caching / 50%-off batch. It's already the default model on Free and Pro, and available to Max/Team/Enterprise, in Claude Code, on the Claude API/Platform, plus AWS and Microsoft Foundry. Why it matters for us: this is a textbook 'the cheap tier just moved up' event, and the exact trap our durable lesson warns about — don't reprice your stack on a launch benchmark. The right response isn't 'switch everything to Sonnet 5'; it's to treat it as a new candidate in your model router and let your own eval decide. For the large middle of real work (bulk drafting, summarization, most agentic/coding loops, long-horizon jobs), near-Opus quality at mid-tier price is a genuine margin win; the hardest ~10% may still need Opus. This is the Anthropic-side companion to the open-weight router play (GLM-5.2) — same discipline, different lever.
blocks.doIt: (1) Don't trust the headline — eval it: take 3–5 of your own real tasks (a coding loop, a summarization, an extraction, an agent run) and run them against Sonnet 5 vs your current default; diff the outputs (Promptfoo makes this a one-file job). (2) Re-point the router: where Sonnet 5 passes, move the bulk/agentic/long-horizon work to it and reserve Opus 4.8 for the ~10% that fails your eval — map each recurring task to the cheapest model that clears its bar (card-ai-tooling-glm-5-2-route, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). (3) Bank the discount deliberately: the $2/$10 intro runs only through Aug 31 2026, then $3/$15 — turn on prompt caching (up to 90% off repeated context) and batch (50% off) for anything non-interactive so the saving survives the intro window. (4) Update your two gates: capability/cost now passes for far more tasks, but re-check the jurisdiction/provenance gate for regulated client work (where the calls run, what attaches). (5) Re-check monthly: rankings and prices move weekly — put a standing calendar note to re-run the eval, don't set-and-forget. Card: card-ai-tooling-sonnet-5-default.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3+) — Anthropic's own launch post ('Introducing Claude Sonnet 5', model id + pricing + all-plans/Claude-Code availability) + The New Stack, VentureBeat and IT Pro (independent launch coverage), with MarkTechPost for the head-to-head agentic-coding/pricing numbers and the Claude Platform pricing page. Honest scope: benchmark figures are directional (a launch-week agentic-coding read, not a reproduced result — the whole point of the eval step); 'close to Opus 4.8' is Anthropic's framing and holds 'on many tasks', not all — the hardest work can still justify Opus. Resolves the Jun 6 [ai-tooling] call ('a Sonnet refresh reprices the mid-tier') ✅ with an honest caveat (it's Sonnet 5, a full version bump, not the guessed 4.8 point release, and ~a week past the rough 2–4-week window). Advances thread-open-weight-price-pressure / the model-router lesson (now: GLM-5.2 for open-weight bulk, Sonnet 5 for hosted near-frontier, Opus for the hardest tail). Logged a prediction (Anthropic makes the $2/$10 intro permanent past Aug 31 OR a 2nd frontier lab cuts a comparable mid-tier agentic model to ≤~$3/$15 to match). Sources: Anthropic, The New Stack, VentureBeat, IT Pro, MarkTechPost, Claude Platform pricing.

sections.h: CRO & AOV — run it today (answering a team Ask): let AI diagnose your single biggest dollar leak from your own order data, then pull ONE lever — a free-shipping threshold pegged to your modal order value with a live progress bar

blocks.sub: A teammate's standing Ask-anything question — 'the best ONE thing to focus on for CRO and AOV?' — gets the honest answer: it's whatever YOUR numbers say leaks most, and you can decide it in under an hour. Hand AI your 90-day order export + GA4 funnel, have it compute AOV and your MODAL (most common) order value, and rank the leak. Basket size too low → free-shipping threshold pegged just above the modal value (~15–25% over AOV) + a dynamic 'You're $X away' progress bar (goal-gradient; ~15–25% conversion lift vs static). Checkout drop-off → kill friction first (guest checkout, fewer fields, full cost shown early — unexpected costs drive ~48% of abandonment). Then measure AOV, conversion AND margin.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: this is a direct answer to a question sitting in our demand backlog (the team's Ask-anything box). The trap in 'what's the ONE thing for CRO/AOV' is importing someone else's case-study tactic; the fix is to let your distribution pick the lever, and AI makes that a sub-hour diagnosis instead of an analyst project. The move: export your last 90 days of orders plus your GA4 funnel (or Shopify checkout analytics), hand it to Claude/ChatGPT, and ask it to compute two numbers — your average order value (AOV) and your modal order value (the single most common basket total, which the mean hides) — and to name where the money leaks: small baskets, or shoppers who reach checkout and bail. If the leak is basket size, the fastest profitable lever is a free-shipping threshold — but set it against the modal order value, not a round number and not the blunt 'about 30% above AOV' rule: a threshold sitting just above the value most carts already land on (research clusters the sweet spot at ~15–25% above current AOV) is close enough to feel achievable, so shoppers add one more item, and roughly 58% will add to qualify. Pair it with a dynamic progress bar ('You're $12 away from free shipping') — the goal-gradient effect (people push harder as a goal gets closer) makes dynamic messaging convert markedly better than a static banner. If the leak is checkout drop-off instead, threshold tricks are premature — fix friction first: offer guest checkout, cut form fields, and show the full cost (shipping included) early, because unexpected extra costs are the single most-cited reason for abandonment (~48%, Baymard). Why it matters for us: it turns a vague 'grow AOV' brief into one measured, reversible experiment we can run for a client this week — and it's a repeatable diagnostic, not a one-off.
blocks.doIt: (1) Export the data: pull the last 90 days of orders (order totals + line items) and your GA4/checkout funnel. (2) Diagnose with AI: ask Claude/ChatGPT to compute AOV and the modal order value, plot the order-value distribution, and rank the biggest leak (basket size vs checkout drop-off vs low repeat rate). (3) Pick ONE lever from the diagnosis — don't run three at once, you won't know what moved. (4a) If basket size: set the free-shipping threshold just above the modal order value (sanity-check it lands ~15–25% over current AOV) and add a live 'You're $X from free shipping' progress bar in cart + mini-cart. (4b) If checkout drop-off: turn on guest checkout, remove non-essential fields, and surface total cost (incl. shipping) on the first checkout step. (5) Guard the margin: measure AOV, conversion rate and contribution margin together for 3–4 weeks — a higher AOV funded by absorbed shipping can be a net loss; the profit-max threshold is where the extra basket margin clears your per-order shipping cost. (6) Only then move to the next lever. Card: card-cro-ai-diagnose-one-lever (updated).
blocks.note: Answers a standing team Ask (gaps-digest: 'best one thing to focus on when it comes to CRO and AOV', ask_no_match) — flagged so we can mark it addressed. Corroboration: authoritative + practitioner — Baymard Institute (cart-abandonment research: ~48% of abandonments cite unexpected extra costs; guest-checkout/field-count usability) + Shopify's AOV guide (AOV/modal-order-value framing, threshold pegged to modal not a round number) + Digital Applied's 2026 free-shipping-threshold playbook (the ~15–25%-above-AOV band + dynamic progress-bar conversion lift) and Capital One Shopping consumer stats. Honest scope: the conversion-lift and add-to-qualify percentages are practitioner/aggregate figures — directional; the durable, defensible move is the diagnosis-then-one-lever discipline and the margin guardrail, not any single stat. This reinforces card-cro-ai-diagnose-one-lever (bumped) and cross-links card-cro-post-purchase-upsell (a different lever if the leak is 'no second item'). Advances toward the open Jun-26 [cro] prediction (a platform/app ships an AI threshold advisor computed from the store's own order distribution). Sources: Baymard, Shopify, Digital Applied, Capital One Shopping.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked): Big AI becomes a political entity (Google's €4.1B fine upheld, OpenAI floats a US-government stake) as the 'show-me-the-profit' selloff hits the chips again

blocks.sub: Jul 2 2026, three dated reads. (1) Google lost its FINAL appeal — the EU Court of Justice upheld the €4.1B (~$4.7B) Android antitrust fine, ending an 8-year fight over pre-installing Search/Chrome. (2) OpenAI proposed handing the US government a ~5% stake (FT) — ~$42.6B at its ~$852B valuation — as part of a floated arrangement where Washington holds 5% of each leading US lab via a sovereign-wealth vehicle (unclear anyone agrees). (3) The chip rout resumed: Samsung closed −9.06%, SK Hynix −14.57% as Korea's Kospi followed an overnight Nasdaq slide on AI-profit doubts. Landscape/intake — the craft payoff is the cost-per-outcome + portability + policy-risk framing.
blocks.p: What moved (cross-checked; figures directional): the throughline is that the biggest AI stories are now as much political and regulatory as technical. (1) Google's Android fine is final. The EU's top court, the Court of Justice, dismissed Google's appeal and upheld the €4.1B (~$4.7B) antitrust penalty for using Android to entrench Search and Chrome — closing a fight that ran since the 2018 Commission decision (a lower court had trimmed the original €4.34B). (Confirmed: CNBC, Engadget, plus wire/Bloomberg coverage via Techmeme.) (2) OpenAI floated giving Washington equity. Per the Financial Times (Jul 2), Sam Altman raised handing the US government a ~5% stake — about $42.6B at OpenAI's ~$852B valuation — in early talks, as part of a broader idea where the government takes 5% of each leading US lab (Anthropic, Google, Meta named) through a sovereign-wealth-fund vehicle; the stated rationale is 'share the AI upside with the public,' and it's explicitly unclear any of the companies would agree. (Reported: FT via CNBC + Yahoo Finance/Business Standard.) (3) The 'show-me-the-profit' selloff is back in hardware. South Korea's chipmakers bore the brunt of an overnight US semiconductor slide: Samsung Electronics closed down 9.06% and SK Hynix down 14.57%, dragging the Kospi (the two now make up ~half its weight). (Confirmed: CNBC, Yahoo Finance, TradingKey.) Why it matters for us — this is a climate signal, not a trade, and it sharpens three operating habits: (a) the market's 'audit the profit' mood is the same scrutiny clients will bring, so lead with cost-per-outcome + a spend ceiling; (b) when governments start upholding record fines and floating equity in the labs, single-vendor dependence carries policy/ownership risk — build client AI for portability (card-ai-tooling-model-portability) so a regulatory or availability shock is a config change, not a rebuild; (c) the adjacent, more constructive dev signal is Cloudflare's Monetization Gateway (Jul 1) — charge any caller for any resource (API, data, an MCP tool call) in stablecoins over the x402 protocol (402 Payment Required → pay → retry with proof), which advances the agentic-payments thread even though the gateway itself is waitlisted. On the open Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [news, made Jun 8, due ~Jul 20]: today's Samsung/SK Hynix drop puts the basket clearly under pressure — we hold the call open, resolve it at the window, and treat intraday index prints as directional. No news card (the landscape is intake; the craft payoff is the framings above). Logged a new prediction (the OpenAI government-stake proposal resolves one way within ~8 weeks). Sources: CNBC (Google fine; OpenAI stake; Samsung/SK Hynix), Engadget (Google fine), Yahoo Finance (OpenAI stake), Cloudflare (Monetization Gateway / x402).

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Monday, June 29
tldr: Web-design play of the day: delete your custom JavaScript dropdown — the native <select> is finally CSS-styleable, and it just reached a 2nd browser engine. Since Chrome 135 you can opt a plain <select> into full styling with one line — appearance: base-select — and on Jun 15 2026 WebKit confirmed customizable select is coming to Safari 27 (the 2nd engine, putting it on the path to Baseline; Firefox is implementing). The win: you keep the browser's built-in keyboard support, focus handling and screen-reader behaviour for free, but now style the closed control, the open ::picker, each option, the ::checkmark and the dropdown arrow — and options can hold rich HTML (a flag icon, an avatar, an SVG), which a real <select> always stripped. Run-it-today: set appearance: base-select on both the <select> and its ::picker(select), then style the parts; wrap it in @supports (appearance: base-select) so unsupported browsers fall back to the normal native dropdown — pure progressive enhancement, no library, no a11y debt. The accessibility golden rule (WebKit): always keep real text inside each <option> (you can hide it visually) so the option stays in the accessibility tree. Joins the modern-CSS-primitives thread (carousel, anchor positioning, sibling-index). (Chromium-only as production today → ship it as enhancement, not a dependency you delete for all users — until Safari 27 + Firefox land.)

Social play: Instagram is about to let viewers tune your reach in real time — make every post unmistakably ONE topic so the 'more of this' taps land on you. On Jun 27 2026 Instagram head Adam Mosseri demoed new ways to steer 'Your Algorithm' straight from the feed: pull down on the home feed to open the topic menu, swipe up on a Reel to tune it, and tap more/less buttons under individual Reels — moving topic control from a buried setting to an in-the-moment gesture ('we want to evolve Your Algorithm from a setting to something that feels central'). Status is honest-rough: 'some of this is testing now, some is coming soon, some might not work.' Why it matters: when a viewer can tap 'more of this topic' (or 'less of yours') on the spot, a post that reads as one clear topic in the first second gets pushed toward the people who opted in — and a muddy, off-niche one gets actively suppressed. Run-it-today: open Your Algorithm on your own account to see how Instagram is labelling content like yours, then hand the model one unmistakable topic per post — same phrase in the first caption line, the on-screen text, and the first 3 seconds — and keep off-topic posts in Stories. (Confirmed ≥3: TechCrunch, Social Media Today, India TV, The Daily Star. Card: card-social-instagram-your-algorithm, updated.)

Graphic play: stop re-describing your brand look on every image — teach Recraft your visual language ONCE, then generate hundreds of on-brand assets that actually match. The slow way is prompt-roulette: you re-type 'flat, muted palette, 2px strokes…' on every generation and still get a different look each time. Recraft's Style system fixes it — upload 5–10 consistent brand images (or combine up to 5 saved styles), and Recraft encodes them into a saveable Style (a 'Style Lock'): it learns your colour treatment, line weight, composition and detail level and constrains every later generation to it. Styles save to your account and share across the team, so it behaves like brand-system management, not one-off art. Run-it-today: in Recraft open Styles → create a new style → upload reference images, name it (e.g. RedPxl-brand-illustration), then generate your asset set with that style selected so a deck's worth of spots, icons and social cards read as one family. (Pair with card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector for editable SVG output, and card-graphic-color-palette to set the tokens first. Card: card-graphic-recraft-brand-style-lock.)

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked): markets reopened with one question owning the AI trade — 'show me the profits.' The week's concrete tell came from SpaceX: less than two weeks after the largest IPO ever, it priced a $25B debut bond that drew roughly $89B of orders (~3.5x oversubscribed, among the biggest investment-grade order books on record), across five tranches due 2031–2056 at 5.35–6.65%, with proceeds earmarked for repaying bridge loans plus AI and next-gen-rocket spend — even as the company carries a ~$5B net loss and capex that more than doubled year-over-year (CNBC frames it as huge demand and a potential headache). Read it next to the week's monetization-doubt selloff in AI/memory names (Bank of America: only ~3% of its customers pay for AI, ~$20/mo median) and the picture is capex-hunger meeting ROI-scrutiny in one trade. Our open V-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20] stays open but volatile — the basket has whipsawed all week, so we treat intraday index prints as directional and judge it at the window. For us this is a climate signal, not a trade: clients will increasingly demand evidence the AI work pays — lead every pitch with cost-per-outcome and a spend ceiling (card-ai-tooling-model-portability).

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is let the platform do the heavy lifting, but make your intent unmistakable. The native <select> hands you free accessibility the moment you stop fighting it — opt in and style it. Instagram hands viewers a 'more of this' lever — so state your one topic so plainly the lever lands on you. Recraft hands you brand consistency at scale — but only after you define the visual language once. And the market's message rhymes: the money is done paying for potential and now wants proof — so frame your AI work as a measurable outcome, not a promise.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: ship the CSS-styleable native <select> (appearance: base-select) as progressive enhancement — full styling, zero a11y debt, and now landing in a 2nd engine (Safari 27)

blocks.sub: Chrome 135+ lets you opt a plain <select> into full CSS styling with appearance:base-select on BOTH the <select> and ::picker(select); then ::picker, the option, ::checkmark, ::picker-icon and selectedcontent become styleable, options can hold rich HTML (icons/SVG/images), and the browser keeps all keyboard + screen-reader behaviour for free. On Jun 15 2026 WebKit confirmed it's coming to Safari 27 — the 2nd engine (Firefox implementing) → path to Baseline. Falls back to the platform-native select via @supports, so it's pure progressive enhancement.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: the humble <select> has always been a dead end for designers — you could change a little padding and colour, but anything more (a custom open panel, an icon next to each choice, an animated dropdown) forced you to throw it away and rebuild the whole control in JavaScript, which then has to re-implement keyboard arrows, type-ahead, focus and screen-reader roles by hand (and usually gets the accessibility wrong). The customizable <select> ends that trade-off. Since Chrome 135 you opt in by setting appearance: base-select on both the <select> element and its ::picker(select) pseudo-element. That unlocks the internal parts for styling — the closed control, the ::picker(select) (the open panel, positioned with CSS anchor positioning), each <option>, the ::checkmark on the chosen option, the ::picker-icon (the arrow), and selectedcontent (a live mirror of the current choice) — and, crucially, an <option> can now contain rich HTML (a flag, an avatar, an SVG) instead of plain text. The payoff that JS dropdowns can't match: the browser still supplies all the built-in behaviour — keyboard navigation, type-to-select, focus management and the correct accessibility-tree roles — so you style the look and keep the function for free. On Jun 15 2026 the WebKit team confirmed customizable select is coming to Safari 27, the second engine to implement it (Firefox is in progress) — the step that moves a Chromium-only feature toward Baseline. Why it matters for us: on the builds we ship, dropdowns (country pickers, plan selectors, filters) are where custom components quietly accumulate JS weight and a11y bugs. This deletes a dependency and a category of bugs at once — and because unsupported browsers fall back to the perfectly-usable native control, you can adopt it today as enhancement, not a gamble.
blocks.doIt: (1) Start native: write a real <select><option> with proper text in each option — that's your accessible, works-everywhere baseline. (2) Opt in to styling: set appearance: base-select on both the select and its picker — e.g. .brand-select, .brand-select::picker(select) { appearance: base-select; } (the dual application is the switch; styling only one does nothing). (3) Style the parts: target ::picker(select) for the open panel, option for each row, ::checkmark for the selected tick, ::picker-icon for the arrow, and selectedcontent for the closed control's label; you can animate the picker open/closed. (4) Add rich options where they help — drop an <svg> or <img> inside an <option> (e.g. flags in a country picker). (5) Gate it as enhancement: wrap the upgraded styling in @supports (appearance: base-select) { … } so non-supporting browsers render the standard native dropdown — no broken UI. (6) Honour the golden rule (WebKit): every <option> must keep real text content even if you hide it visually, so the choice stays in the accessibility tree. (7) Decide the deploy tier: treat it as Chromium-only today (Chrome/Edge 135+) — ship it as progressive enhancement now; once Safari 27 + Firefox land, it becomes a JS dropdown you can delete for everyone. Card: card-webdesign-customizable-select.

sections.h: Social — run it today: Instagram is moving 'Your Algorithm' topic control into in-feed gestures (pull-down menu, swipe-up-on-Reel, more/less buttons) — so make every post unmistakably one topic the algorithm can file and viewers can opt into

blocks.sub: Jun 27 2026: Instagram head Adam Mosseri demoed new ways to steer 'Your Algorithm' directly from the feed — pull DOWN on the home feed to open the topic menu, swipe UP on a Reel to tune it, and tap more/less buttons under individual Reels — moving topic control from a buried setting to an in-the-moment gesture ('we want to evolve Your Algorithm from a setting to something that feels central'). Status is rough: 'some is testing now, some is coming soon, some might not work.' For creators: viewers now send explicit more/less-of-this-topic signals in the moment, so an instantly-on-topic post gets fed to opted-in people and a muddy one gets pushed away.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: 'Your Algorithm' — the control that turns each person's behaviour into plain-language topics they can add or remove — reached Instagram's main feed earlier in June. On Jun 27 2026 Instagram chief Adam Mosseri previewed the next step: rather than dig into Settings, users will tune their feed with in-the-moment gestures. The three being tested: pull down on the home feed to surface the Your Algorithm topic menu; swipe up from a Reel to open similar customization; and simple 'more / less like this' buttons beneath individual Reels. Mosseri framed the goal as evolving Your Algorithm 'from a setting to something that feels central to your experience,' and was candid about status — 'some of this is testing now, some is coming soon, some might not work.' Why it matters for us: this hands the audience a fast, explicit lever over distribution. Reach already depended on Instagram's model confidently filing your post under an opt-in-able topic; now viewers can actively say 'more of this topic' or 'less of this account' in one tap, in the moment. That sharpens the same play in both directions — a post that reads as one clear topic in the first second gets surfaced to the people who chose that topic and can earn an explicit 'more like this'; an off-niche or muddy post is the one that gets a 'show less' and is pushed away from the buyers you want. Consistent, unmistakable topic signalling stops being hygiene and becomes the lever.
blocks.doIt: (1) Read the labels: on your own account open Your Algorithm (Settings → the content-preferences/recommendation controls) and look at the topics Instagram has inferred — that's roughly how it's classifying content like yours. (2) Pick ONE topic per post and state it plainly in three places the model and viewers both read: the first line of the caption, the on-screen text, and the first 3 seconds of a Reel. (3) Hold a consistent niche across recent posts so your account builds a strong, opt-into-able topic association; park off-topic personal posts in Stories, not the grid. (4) Earn the explicit positive signal: give each post a clear 'send this to…' or save-worthy payoff so viewers who tap 'more like this' also share/save (pair with card-social-instagram-sends — sends-per-reach is still the strongest distribution signal). (5) Audit weekly: re-open Your Algorithm — are the inferred topics sharpening toward your niche? Scattered topics mean your signals are too mixed; tighten the first-line/on-screen/first-3s topic cue. Card: card-social-instagram-your-algorithm (updated).
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3+) — TechCrunch ('Instagram is testing more ways for users to customize Your Algorithm', Jun 27, with Mosseri's quotes + the three gesture concepts), Social Media Today ('Instagram tests more algorithm controls'), India TV and The Daily Star (independent write-ups of the same Mosseri demo). Honest scope: these are tests, not shipped features — Mosseri explicitly says some may not ship; treat the specific gestures as directional and the durable takeaway (explicit viewer-level more/less-topic control is coming to the feed) as the thing to design for now. The run-it-today actions (read Your Algorithm, signal one clear topic per post) work today regardless of which gesture ships. Advances the Instagram-ranking thread (the 'Your Algorithm' leg). The open Jun-13 prediction (a 2nd major feed ships LLM topic controls OR Instagram exposes its inferred topic classification to creators) stays open — this is Instagram deepening user controls, not yet exposing the classification to business accounts. Logged a new prediction (Instagram ships at least one of these in-feed Your Algorithm controls to general availability by end of Q3 2026). Sources: TechCrunch, Social Media Today, India TV, The Daily Star.

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: teach Recraft your brand's visual language once (a saved Style / Style Lock from reference images), then generate a whole asset set that actually reads as one family

blocks.sub: Stop re-describing your look on every generation. Recraft's Style system lets you upload 5–10 consistent brand images (or combine up to 5 saved styles) and encodes them into a saveable Style ('Style Lock') — colour treatment, line weight, composition, detail level — that constrains every later generation. Styles save to your account and share across the team, so it works like brand-system management, not one-off prompting. Generate hundreds of on-brand assets (icons, spot illustrations, social cards) that hold a consistent visual identity Midjourney/Imagen need prompt-gymnastics to fake.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: the core failure of AI image tools for brand work isn't quality, it's consistency — ask for ten assets and you get ten slightly different looks, because the 'style' lives in a prompt you re-type (and the model re-interprets) every time. Recraft's Style system makes the look a persistent, saved object instead of a sentence. You go to the Styles area, create a new style, and either pick from Recraft's named library or upload your own reference images — Recraft recommends 5–10 images that are genuinely consistent (same genre, same aesthetic direction, similar colour treatment), or you can combine up to five existing styles. Recraft's style-embedding then extracts and encodes the visual characteristics — line weight, colour application, corner treatment, rendering approach, detail level — into a reusable Style ('Style Lock') that constrains every subsequent generation to that language. Because styles save to your account and can be shared with your team and reused across projects, it's closer to running a brand system than prompting: define the visual identity once, apply it everywhere. Why it matters for us: this is the difference between 'AI made a nice picture' and 'AI made our picture, fifty times.' For a brand or campaign you can spin up a coherent set — spot illustrations, icons, social-card backgrounds, blog headers — that all read as one family, in minutes, without a human re-establishing the look on each asset. It pairs with Recraft's native SVG output so the on-brand result is also an editable vector, not a flat PNG.
blocks.doIt: (1) Gather references: pick 5–10 of your most on-brand, visually consistent existing assets (same illustration style, palette, line treatment) — quality and consistency of the set drives the result. (2) Create the style: in Recraft open Styles → create a new style → upload reference images (or combine up to 5 saved styles), let it encode the look, and name it clearly (e.g. RedPxl-brand-illustration). (3) Generate against it: with that Style selected, prompt your asset set — keep prompts about content ('a paper-plane icon', 'a hero illustration of a team shipping a launch') and let the Style carry the look. (4) Lock & share: save the Style to your account and share it with the team so everyone generates against the same identity; iterate the reference set if the look drifts. (5) Keep it editable + on-token: for logos/icons generate with a V4 Vector model to get true SVG (card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector), and set your exact brand colours first (card-graphic-color-palette) so the palette is intentional, not approximated. (6) Human-in-the-loop: pick the 2–3 that fit, clean up, and confirm on-brand + licensed before shipping. Card: card-graphic-recraft-brand-style-lock.
blocks.note: Corroboration: tool-primary technique (one official source is sufficient for a how-to card) — Recraft's own blog ('New Tools for Brand Style Consistency and Control' + 'How to Create Sets of Consistent Images with Recraft') documents the upload-5–10-references → saved-Style → consistent-generation flow; practitioner write-ups (MindStudio, whytryai) corroborate the steps and the 5–10-image guidance. Honest scope: results depend on the consistency of your reference set (mixed references = a muddy style); confirm commercial-use rights for generated assets and verify any embedded text before shipping (use a vector/Recraft V4 model for clean editable output). Distinct from card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector (that card is about native SVG output; this one is about brand-style consistency at scale) — cross-linked. Advances the structured/brand-consistent-AI-generation thread (related: card-graphic-canva-brand-kit-ai, card-graphic-firefly-brand-batch-creatives, card-graphic-color-palette). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major AI image tool ships a saveable, team-shareable brand-style-lock trained from uploaded reference images). Sources: Recraft Blog ×2, MindStudio, whytryai.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked): markets reopen with 'show me the profits' owning the AI trade — SpaceX's $25B debut bond (huge demand, real headache) is the week's capex-hunger-meets-ROI-scrutiny tell

blocks.sub: Markets reopened Mon Jun 29 with AI-monetization doubt still driving the tape. The concrete, dated tell: SpaceX — <2 weeks after the largest IPO ever — priced a $25B DEBUT bond that drew ~$89B of orders (~3.5x oversubscribed, among the biggest investment-grade order books on record), in five tranches due 2031–2056 at 5.35–6.65%, proceeds earmarked for bridge-loan repayment + AI/next-gen-rocket spend — while carrying a ~$5B net loss and capex that more than doubled YoY (CNBC: huge demand AND a potential headache). Read with the week's selloff in AI/memory names on profit doubts (BofA: only ~3% of customers pay for AI, ~$20/mo median), it's capex-hunger meeting ROI-scrutiny in one trade.
blocks.p: What moved (cross-checked; figures directional): the dominant question owning the AI trade this week is no longer 'how big is the spend' but 'will the spend pay off.' The cleanest dated illustration is SpaceX's debut bond. Less than two weeks after the largest IPO in history, SpaceX tapped the debt market for $25B (an offering it raised from a planned ~$20B on demand) and drew roughly $89B of orders — about 3.5x oversubscribed, among the largest investment-grade order books on record — pricing five tranches due 2031–2056 at 5.35%–6.65%, with proceeds earmarked for repaying bridge loans and funding general corporate purposes, with emphasis on AI and next-generation rocket development. The tension CNBC flags: the demand is enormous, yet the company is tapping debt while carrying a ~$5B net loss and capex that more than doubled year-over-year — 'huge demand, and a potential headache for investors.' (Confirmed ≥3: CNBC, The Motley Fool, TradingKey; the bond was announced ~Jun 22 and priced into the week — order-book and tranche figures are reported, treat exact basis points as directional.) Set that against the week's monetization-doubt selloff in AI and memory names: investors are now demanding evidence the unprecedented AI spend turns into profit, and a circulated Bank of America datapoint crystallised the worry — only about 3% of its customers pay for AI services, at a ~$20/month median (reported via CBS News; treat as a single survey read, directional). The prior leg of this story (the Jun 22–24 rout that hit SK Hynix/Samsung on an HBM4/Nvidia-Rubin demand-revision scare) we've already logged; the durable, dated facts this week are the SpaceX bond and the hardening 'show-me-the-ROI' narrative around it. Why it matters for us — this is a climate signal, not a trade: the same scrutiny is coming to the AI work we pitch. Clients who watched the market stop paying for 'potential' will ask the same of us — so lead with cost-per-outcome (what does this save/earn, per dollar of model spend) and a spend ceiling, and build client AI features for portability so you're never locked into one model's pricing (card-ai-tooling-model-portability). On the open V-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, made Jun 8, due ~Jul 20]: the basket (SoftBank/Samsung/SK Hynix) has whipsawed all week — rebounding hard around Jun 25 then pressured again on profit doubts — so we treat intraday/weekly index prints as directional and resolve the call at its window; it stays open, volatile, leaning held but not safe. No news card (the landscape is intake; the craft payoff is the cost-per-outcome framing + the model-portability scaffold). Logged a new prediction (a 2nd marquee AI-infrastructure company raises ≥$10B in debt within ~8 weeks, confirming the debt-over-dilution shift to fund AI capex). Sources: CNBC (SpaceX bond), The Motley Fool (bond order book / warning), TradingKey (recap + SPCX), CBS News (selloff + BofA monetization read).
