reports.title: Daily Briefing — Friday, June 26
tldr: Web-dev play of the day: make your Next.js app feel SPA-instant on navigation — without rewriting to a SPA — by flipping two flags. On Jun 25 2026 Next.js shipped 16.3 (preview) with Instant Navigations. Turn on cacheComponents: true and every route's awaited data now gives you a choice: Stream it (wrap in <Suspense> → user sees an instant loading shell), Cache it ('use cache' → user sees instant cached UI), or Block it (export const instant = false). Add partialPrefetching: true and Next prefetches one reusable shell per route instead of one request per link — killing the embarrassing prefetch-flurry in the Network tab. Bonus dev tooling: Instant Insights turns slow navigations into a dev-time error, an instant() Playwright helper guards against regressions, and a Navigation Inspector shows exactly what each shell prefetches. Run-it-today: on a branch, npm i next@preview, set both flags, open Instant Insights, and fix the one flagged route by wrapping its data in <Suspense> or 'use cache'. (Pre-production preview; flags are opt-in but slated to become defaults; use Chrome/Firefox for the dev tooling. Same-day: AI SDK 7 also landed — agents, tool approvals, durable workflows, realtime voice/video.)

Graphic play (no Photoshop round-trip): describe a texture in plain words and let Figma build it as a live, tweakable material right on the canvas. At Config 2026 (Jun 24) Figma shipped Shader fills and effects — real WebGPU shaders you make by prompting the Figma agent or dropping a reference image. A fill is a new material; an effect transforms what's already in the layer. Named looks include frosted glass, chrome, dithering, pixelation, halftone, particles, and stackable ones like Gradient map, Riso print, Luminance particles. Because each shader is parameterized by default, you get sliders on the canvas — so a brand treatment becomes a reusable, tweakable component, not a baked PNG. The play: stop hunting stock grain/glass textures and type the treatment you want, then dial it to brand. (Open beta, rolling out Jun 24 to Full seats on a paid plan; not on Starter/Education/Gov; free of AI credits for now, charged at GA; interactive shaders 'coming soon'. Distinct from yesterday's Figma Motion animation timeline and from Code Layers, which lands in July.)

CRO play (answers a teammate's Ask-anything question — 'the best ONE thing for CRO and AOV?'): the honest answer is 'whatever YOUR numbers say is leaking most' — and you can decide it in an hour with AI on your own export. Export your last-90-day orders + GA4 funnel, hand the AI the order-value distribution, and ask it to compute your AOV and your modal (most common) order value, then name the single highest-dollar leak. If it's basket size → a free-shipping threshold set just above your modal order value plus a 'You're $X from free shipping' progress bar is the single highest-leverage, no-dev AOV lever (the 'goal-gradient' effect — people push harder as the reward nears). If it's checkout drop-off → kill friction first (guest checkout, fewer fields, show full cost incl. shipping early). Then ship one change and measure AOV, conversion AND margin for 3–4 weeks. The point: stop copying someone else's playbook — let your data pick the lever.

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; market figures directional): the AI selloff has a NEW trigger today — not capex, not talent, but IPO timing. A report (NYT, via Bloomberg) that OpenAI leans toward delaying its IPO to 2027 — with Sam Altman holding firm on a target valuation of up to ~$1 trillion rather than listing sooner at a lower one, amid tech-stock volatility and SpaceX's rocky post-IPO trade — hammered AI names Jun 26. SoftBank (OpenAI's biggest backer) fell as much as ~12–13%; Japan's Nikkei −4.15%, Korea's KOSPI −5.81% (circuit breaker tripped again), Kioxia −12%. Separately (The Information): OpenAI will ship GPT-5.6 only via a limited, customer-by-customer-approved preview at the White House's request. Counter-signal the same week: SK Hynix priced a record ~$29B Nasdaq ADR listing at ~$166/ADR (255,500 won), eyeing a Jul 10 debut (HSBC sees ~+20% upside) — money still chasing the AI-memory trade. This pressures our 'dip not regime change' V-shape call again (due ~Jul 20): SoftBank is the basket's laggard and just dropped double-digits, while Samsung/SK Hynix sit well above their Jun 8 mark — still open, leaning held but volatile.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is stop translating and start declaring — then let the system do the work. Next.js lets you declare a navigation instant (Stream/Cache/Block) instead of rebuilding as a SPA; Figma lets you describe a material instead of hand-painting it in Photoshop; the CRO play lets your data declare which lever leaks most instead of you guessing. And the market just drew the same line it's been drawing all month — it has stopped paying for AI story (an eventual $1T IPO) and wants the delivery (when, and at what return). Decide from the real signal in front of you, ship one thing, measure it.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: make Next.js navigations feel SPA-instant with Instant Navigations (two flags, no SPA rewrite)

blocks.sub: Next.js 16.3 (preview, Jun 25 2026) ships Instant Navigations: enable cacheComponents and each awaited route data becomes a choice — Stream with <Suspense> (instant loading shell), Cache with 'use cache' (instant cached UI), or Block with export const instant = false — plus Partial Prefetching (partialPrefetching) that prefetches ONE reusable shell per route instead of one request per link, with Instant Insights, an instant() Playwright helper and a Navigation Inspector to keep routes honest
blocks.p: What changed: on Jun 25 2026 the Next.js team published 16.3 as a public preview, headlined by Instant Navigations — opt-in behaviors that bring single-page-app responsiveness to the server-driven App Router without giving up server rendering. The mechanism: enable cacheComponents: true in next.config.ts, and wherever a route awaits data on the server you now choose how the navigation feels — Stream it by wrapping in <Suspense> (the user instantly sees a loading shell, real UI streams in), Cache it with 'use cache' (the user instantly sees previously cached UI), or deliberately Block with export const instant = false for routes (a blog post, say) where you'd rather wait than show a shell. Separately, partialPrefetching: true rethinks prefetch: instead of firing a request per link in the viewport (the notorious Network-tab flurry), Next now prefetches one reusable shell per route and caches it for the session; opt specific links into deeper prefetch with <Link prefetch> + 'use cache', or extend to request-time content with export const prefetch = 'allow-runtime'. To keep it from regressing, Instant Insights makes a slow navigation an error in development, an instant() helper from @next/playwright asserts what must be visible without waiting for the network, and a Navigation Inspector in DevTools pauses each navigation at its shell so you can see exactly what's prefetched. Why it matters for us: 'navigation feels slow' is the single most common, most valid criticism of the Server-Components sites we ship for clients — and the usual fix is a painful rewrite toward a client SPA. This closes that gap with config + a few <Suspense> boundaries, and the per-route shell prefetch also trims wasted bandwidth on link-heavy pages (sidebars, chat lists, dashboards).
blocks.doIt: (1) On a branch, install the preview: npm install next@preview (this is pre-production — don't ship it to real users yet). (2) Turn it on: in next.config.ts set cacheComponents: true and partialPrefetching: true. (3) Run dev and read Instant Insights — it flags routes whose navigation isn't instant (a dev-time error), so you get a worklist, not a guess. (4) Fix the flagged route: wrap its slow awaited data in <Suspense> (Stream) or mark the function 'use cache' (Cache); if a route genuinely should wait, opt out with export const instant = false. (5) Inspect the shell: open the Navigation Inspector in DevTools, pause at the shell, confirm the instant part is what you expect; add <Link prefetch> + 'use cache' only where you want more than the shell to pop in. (6) Lock it in: add an instant() Playwright test (import {'{ instant }'} from '@next/playwright') so a future refactor can't silently make the route slow again. (7) Use the official Cache Components adoption Skill (linked from the post) to have your coding agent walk an existing app through adoption. Use Chrome or Firefox for the dev tooling — Safari has known preview issues. Card: card-webdev-nextjs-instant-navigations.
blocks.note: Grounding: primary, single-vendor — the Next.js blog ('Next.js 16.3: Instant Navigations', Andrew Clark / Josh Story, Jun 25 2026) plus the preview Instant Navigations docs and the GitHub discussion thread; vendor-confirmed feature, not a multi-source claim, so no corroboration count. Honest scope: this is a pre-production preview published under the @preview npm tag — Next says a 'polished stable release' is coming 'in the coming weeks' and that changes are likely before stable, so prototype on it, don't deploy to users; the behaviors are gated behind opt-in flags (cacheComponents, partialPrefetching) that Next plans to make defaults in a future major; known preview bugs include a params-in-shell blocking case that isn't reported by Instant Insights and broken Instant Insights tooling in Safari (use Chrome/Firefox in dev); actual prefetching only runs in production. Joins the Next/Vercel platform thread. Logged a prediction (Next.js 16.3 reaches stable GA with Instant Navigations and/or cacheComponents becomes a default). Scan-line (not today's card): AI SDK 7 shipped the same day (Jun 25) — agents with tool approvals, durable workflows, sandboxes, observability, and realtime voice/video generation; pnpm add ai@latest + an npx @ai-sdk/codemod v7 migration — worth a look if you build AI features. Sources: Next.js Blog, Next.js preview docs, Vercel Blog (AI SDK 7).

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: prompt Figma to build a custom WebGPU shader (frosted glass, chrome, halftone, grain) as a live, tweakable material — no Photoshop round-trip

blocks.sub: Figma shipped Shader fills and effects at Config 2026 (Jun 24 2026): real WebGPU shaders you create by describing the look to the Figma agent or dropping a reference image — a 'fill' is a new material, an 'effect' transforms what's already in the layer; named looks include frosted glass, chrome, dithering, pixelation, halftone and particles, with stackable effects like Gradient map, Riso print and Luminance particles, all parameterized by default so you get adjustable controls on the canvas
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: at Config 2026 on Jun 24 2026 Figma made shader creation a first-class, no-code part of the canvas with Shader fills and effects (WebGPU). Instead of writing GLSL or round-tripping to Photoshop/After Effects for a texture, you describe the look in plain words to the Figma agent (or hand it a reference image) and it builds the shader for you. The distinction: a Shader fill acts as a new material on the layer, while a Shader effect transforms what's already there. Figma names looks like frosted glass, chrome, dithering, pixelation, halftone and particles, and shows stackable effects such as Gradient map, Riso print and Luminance particles. Crucially, every generated shader is parameterized by default — Figma exposes the knobs as native-feeling controls directly on the canvas, so the result isn't a frozen image but a tweakable, shareable treatment. Why it matters for us: custom surface treatments — glassmorphism panels, duotone/halftone hero art, film grain, chrome/metal type, dithered backgrounds — show up constantly in brand and UI work, and today they mean either buying stock textures or a Photoshop detour that bakes the effect into pixels nobody can adjust later. Prompt-to-shader keeps the treatment live and on-brand: a designer types the look, dials the on-canvas sliders to the brand palette, and the team can re-skin it without rebuilding. It turns 'find a grain texture' into 'describe the grain I want.'
blocks.doIt: (1) Check access: you need a Full seat on a paid Figma plan (Pro/Org/Enterprise) — it's an open beta rolling out from Jun 24 2026, not available on Starter/Education/Gov. (2) Pick the right verb: select a shape/frame and add a Shader fill when you want a new material (a glass surface, a gradient-mapped backdrop), or a Shader effect when you want to transform existing art (halftone a photo, dither an illustration). (3) Prompt the Figma agent: describe the look — e.g. 'frosted-glass card surface with subtle noise', 'halftone duotone in our brand red and ink', 'brushed-chrome metal for this headline' — or drop a reference image for it to match. (4) Dial it to brand: use the on-canvas controls Figma exposes (it parameterizes the shader automatically) to set palette, scale, intensity; stack effects (e.g. Gradient map + Luminance particles) for a layered treatment. (5) Make it reusable: keep the parameters tidy so teammates can re-skin the same shader for variants instead of regenerating from scratch. (6) Sanity-check delivery: WebGPU renders in-app — when you export to flat assets or hand to dev, confirm the look survives at the sizes/formats you ship. Card: card-graphic-figma-shaders.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Figma's own Config 2026 recap blog + Figma Learn 'What's new from Config 2026' help article (primary/vendor, same domain) and TechTimes (independent press on the Config 2026 shader/Code-Layers news). Honest scope: open beta, gradually rolling out from Jun 24 2026 and limited to Full seats on paid plans (excluded: Starter, Education, Government); shaders do not consume AI credits today but will at general availability, so factor cost in before standardizing a batch workflow; interactive shaders are 'coming soon' (not shipped); and this is distinct from two other Config 2026 items — Figma Motion (yesterday's on-canvas animation timeline, card-webdesign-figma-motion) and Code Layers (early access in July, not run-it-today). WebGPU support varies by browser/GPU — verify exported/handed-off fidelity. Advances the design-tooling thread (related: card-webdesign-figma-motion, card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector, card-graphic-figma-capture-layers). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships prompt-to-GPU-shader generation, OR Figma's shaders exit open beta to GA). Sources: Figma Blog (Config 2026 recap), Figma Learn (What's new from Config 2026), TechTimes.

sections.h: CRO & AOV — run it today: stop guessing the 'one thing' — let AI read your own order + funnel data and name the single highest-dollar lever (answers a team Ask-anything gap)

blocks.sub: A teammate asked the Ask box 'what is the best ONE thing to focus on for CRO and AOV?' — the honest answer is it depends on YOUR funnel, and you can decide it in under an hour: export last-90-day orders + GA4 funnel, have Claude/ChatGPT compute your AOV and modal (most common) order value and name the biggest leak, then ship ONE lever — a modal-pegged free-shipping threshold + progress bar if it's basket size, or checkout-friction removal if it's drop-off
blocks.p: What this answers: this is a direct reply to an open Ask-anything question in the team's demand backlog — 'the best one thing to focus on for CRO and AOV.' The trap is treating it as a universal answer; the leverage is decidable from your own numbers. There is no single 'best thing' that's true for every store — a store with a healthy AOV but leaky checkout needs the opposite fix from one with a tidy checkout and tiny baskets. The run-it-today move is to let AI read your real distribution and tell you which lever leaks the most money, then pull exactly one. The default answers, if you must pick blind: for AOV, the single highest-leverage, no-dev lever is a free-shipping threshold set just above your most common (modal) order value, paired with a live 'You're $X from free shipping' progress bar — it works via the 'goal-gradient' effect (shoppers push harder as a reward gets closer) and moves the whole basket distribution without buying any new traffic. For conversion, the single highest-leverage lever is removing checkout friction — guest checkout, fewer form fields, and showing the full price (including shipping) early, since surprise costs are the #1 documented reason carts get abandoned (Baymard). Why it matters for us: teams burn weeks debating CRO tactics copied from blog posts; an hour with the store's own export replaces that debate with a ranked, dollar-weighted worklist — and tying the threshold to the modal order value (not a round number, and not the blunt 'set it ~30% above AOV' rule of thumb) is what makes free shipping feel attainable to the most shoppers.
blocks.doIt: (1) Pull the data: in Shopify Admin → Orders → Export (or your platform's order export) grab the last 90 days; from GA4 note where the funnel drops (product → cart → checkout → purchase). (2) Ask the AI to diagnose: paste the order-value summary into Claude/ChatGPT (use its data-analysis mode for the raw CSV) and prompt: 'From this order-value distribution, compute my AOV and my modal (most common) order value, show the distribution shape, and tell me whether I lose more money to small baskets or to checkout drop-off given this funnel.' (3) If the leak is basket size (AOV): set a free-shipping threshold just above your modal order value and add a cart/mini-cart progress bar ('You're $X from free shipping') with one relevant low-cost add-on beside it — the bar is the lift (see card-cro-free-shipping-threshold for the margin math). (4) If the leak is checkout drop-off: fix friction first — enable guest checkout (card-cro-guest-checkout), cut form fields (card-cro-minimise-form-fields), and show the full cost incl. shipping early (card-cro-show-total-cost-early). (5) Bundle to the goal: ask the AI to mine co-purchase pairs from your export and propose 2–3 bundles priced to nudge orders over the threshold (card-cro-cross-sell-bundles). (6) Ship ONE change and measure AOV, conversion AND margin for 3–4 weeks before the next — a higher AOV that you fund with absorbed shipping can be a net loss. Card: card-cro-ai-diagnose-one-lever.
blocks.note: Grounding: technique built on primary/authoritative sources — Shopify's AOV guide (the AOV = revenue ÷ orders formula, the free-shipping-threshold guidance, and the caution to peg the threshold to the modal order value so it feels attainable) and Baymard Institute (checkout/cart-abandonment research: surprise/extra cost is the top abandonment reason). Honest scope: the percentage rules of thumb (e.g. 'threshold ~30% above AOV', upsell/bundle lifts in the ~15–25% range) are well-supported starting points to TEST, not guarantees — the whole point of the AI-diagnosis step is to replace folklore with your own distribution; and the AI's read is only as good as the export you give it, so sanity-check its AOV/modal numbers against your platform's own analytics. Covers the open CRO/AOV demand gap from the team's Ask-anything backlog (gaps-digest). Related: card-cro-free-shipping-threshold, card-cro-cross-sell-bundles, card-cro-post-purchase-upsell, card-cro-guest-checkout, card-cro-show-total-cost-early. Logged a prediction (a major commerce platform or top AOV app ships an AI threshold-advisor that recommends the number from a store's own order distribution). Sources: Shopify — Average Order Value guide, Baymard Institute — Cart Abandonment.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, intraday vs close vary by source)

blocks.sub: The AI selloff gets a NEW trigger — IPO timing, not capex or talent: a report (NYT, via Bloomberg) that OpenAI leans toward delaying its IPO to 2027 (Altman holding firm on a ~$1T valuation rather than listing sooner/lower, amid tech-stock volatility + SpaceX's rocky debut) slammed AI names Jun 26 — SoftBank ~−12–13%, Nikkei −4.15%, KOSPI −5.81% (circuit breaker again), Kioxia −12% — while SK Hynix priced a record ~$29B Nasdaq ADR at ~$166 (Jul 10 target), money still chasing AI memory; this PRESSURES our 'dip not regime change' V-shape call again (due ~Jul 20)
blocks.p: What moved: the month-long AI-equity repricing took a new shape on Jun 26 — this time the trigger isn't capex fear (Jun 22–23) or talent flight, it's IPO timing and valuation discipline. A report (originating with the New York Times, carried by Bloomberg) said OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO to 2027: its bankers laid out two paths — list sooner at a lower number, or wait and push for a valuation of up to ~$1 trillion (up from a ~$730B last private mark) — and CEO Sam Altman is holding firm on the higher target, with advisers warning that tech-stock volatility and SpaceX's rocky post-IPO trade would dampen retail enthusiasm now. Markets read a delayed IPO as delayed returns for OpenAI's backers: SoftBank (its largest investor) fell as much as ~12–13%; Japan's Nikkei 225 −4.15%, Korea's KOSPI −5.81% (tripping a circuit breaker again), and Japanese NAND maker Kioxia −12% as AI-memory names sold off. Separately, per The Information, Altman told staff OpenAI will release GPT-5.6 only through a limited preview approved customer-by-customer at the White House's request (the Office of the National Cyber Director / OSTP) — dovetailing with the Jun 25 'White House asks OpenAI to slow-roll its new model' thread. The counter-signal the same week: SK Hynix priced a record ~$29B Nasdaq ADR offering at ~$166 per ADR (255,500 won), targeting a Jul 10 debut, with HSBC seeing ~+20% upside — i.e. capital is still actively chasing the AI-memory trade even as sentiment whipsaws. Why it matters for us: this is a climate signal, not a trade — the market just split 'is AI real?' from 'when does it pay off, and at what return?' Clients will increasingly ask about time-to-ROI and whether the AI vendors you build on are durable through a delayed-liquidity stretch. Lead pitches with near-term, measurable payoffs you can ship now, not a vendor's eventual blockbuster IPO. Thread impact: a fresh leg of the AI-trade-repricing storyline that PRESSURES our open 'dip not regime change' V-shape call [news, due ~Jul 20]: SoftBank is the basket's laggard and just dropped double-digits again, though Samsung and SK Hynix sit well above their Jun 8 reference (SK Hynix near a record on the ADR plan) — so the basket as a whole is likely still above Jun 8 and the call stays open, leaning held but volatile; the Apple call [due ~Jul 21] remains open. Corroboration: Confirmed (≥3) — Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance, TradingKey, CNBC (+ NYT as the originating report; The Information on GPT-5.6). All percentages directional, intraday vs close vary by source. Sources: Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance, TradingKey, CNBC.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, June 25
tldr: Web-design play of the day: animate a design on the Figma canvas — then copy the real CSS/React easing straight out of Dev Mode, no After Effects round-trip. At Config 2026 (Jun 24) Figma shipped Figma Motion: a real animation timeline as a fourth canvas mode next to Design / Draw / Dev. You keyframe position, scale, rotation, opacity, drop fade/move/scale presets, define custom easing as reusable Motion variables, scrub to preview, and export MP4/WebM/GIF/SVG. The handoff win: in Dev Mode → Motion a dev copies the exact timing + easing curve as CSS, JSON, or framework-ready React (motion.dev) — deleting the 'what easing did the designer mean?' guesswork. Run-it-today: switch one frame to Motion, keyframe a single button-hover or modal entrance, copy the CSS. (Open beta, all plans; Starter exports are limited; validate the exported curve against your front-end. Code Layers — code on the canvas — is separate and only rolls out in July.)

Social play (organic, free): stop guessing why a Reel popped — ask the model that has your numbers. Meta's Creator Assistant — a free conversational AI built into the Facebook creator dashboard (live since Jun 4; Meta doubled down Jun 24 with a standalone Creator Studio app built on it) — answers questions against your own account data and brainstorms ideas off live Facebook trends. The play: ask 'why did my top Reel outperform?', pull out the repeatable variable (hook/length/audio), then ask 'give me 3 angles off what's trending in my niche' and turn that into the week's posts. It beats generic best-time charts and a blank ChatGPT prompt because it sees your real performance. (Geo-gated: US/Canada/India for now; Facebook dashboard only.)

Paid play [meta-ads]: stop building one ad per format — pack ONE ad with up to 10 mixed image + video assets across aspect ratios and let Advantage+ pick the winner per placement. Meta's refreshed multimedia-ads guidance (reported Jun 24, from Meta's own help doc) is explicit: upload all your assets into a single ad and let delivery optimize — up to 10 images and videos, including multiple aspect ratios (9:16 + 1:1/4:5), in one ad. Splitting creative per placement just splinters the learning signal. The operator edge moves back to creative volume + variety: five genuinely different angles (UGC clip, demo, testimonial, text-led explainer, lifestyle) beat fifty near-duplicates. Keep Advantage+ creative on; disclose any AI-generated asset. (Per-ad cap is 10; that's distinct from total ad-set asset volume. Figure traces to Meta's doc as quoted by Social Media Today — Reported, not yet a 3-source claim.)

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; market figures directional): this is an 'AI capex is paying off' rebound week that flips the Jun 22–23 fear. Micron blew out fiscal Q3 — revenue ~$41.5B (vs ~$35.7B est) and record 84.9% gross margin, guiding Q4 to ~$50B; HBM (high-bandwidth AI memory) is sold out through 2027 — stock jumped ~+16% premarket Jun 25. OpenAI + Broadcom unveiled 'Jalapeño' (Jun 24), OpenAI's first custom inference chip (TSMC 3nm, 8 HBM stacks, deploys end-2026). Qualcomm is buying AI-compiler company Modular (~$3.9B, all-stock) to chip at Nvidia's CUDA moat. And Morgan Stanley (Jun 24) framed the selloff as a crowded dip, not broken ROI — which resolves our Jun 23 prediction ✅ (an AI-megacap/sell-side defends capex ROI within ~2 weeks). Counter-notes: Cerebras sank on a soft margin guide; Oracle shed ~21,000 roles over the year (its 10-K ties cuts to AI); and Anthropic accused Alibaba's Qwen lab of an 'illicit' model-distillation campaign (~28.8M interactions via ~25k fake accounts), sending Alibaba's HK shares ~-4.5%. For us this is a climate signal: client scrutiny is shifting from 'is AI spend justified?' to 'show me the ROI, the unit economics, and the IP provenance.'

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is stop translating between the tool that designs and the tool that ships. Figma Motion deletes the After-Effects-to-CSS hop; Creator Assistant deletes the 'pull analytics, guess the why' hop; Meta's one-ad-many-assets deletes the build-an-ad-per-placement hop. Each move collapses a handoff and hands the optimizing to the system that already has the data — then you aim the reclaimed hour at the next lever. And the market just repriced that same instinct: it has stopped paying for AI spend on faith and now wants the outcome.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: animate on the Figma canvas with Figma Motion, then copy the CSS/React easing from Dev Mode (no After Effects)

blocks.sub: Figma shipped Figma Motion at Config 2026 (Jun 24 2026): a real animation TIMELINE as a fourth canvas mode (Design / Draw / Dev / Motion) — keyframe position/scale/rotation/opacity, fade/move/scale presets, custom easing as reusable Motion variables, scrub-to-preview, export MP4/WebM/GIF/SVG — and a Dev Mode → Motion tab that copies the exact timing + easing as CSS, JSON, or framework-ready React (motion.dev)
blocks.p: What changed: at Config 2026 on Jun 24 2026 Figma shipped Figma Motion, a real animation timeline that lives on the design canvas as a fourth mode alongside Design, Draw and Dev. You keyframe position, scale, rotation and opacity independently, drag layers to retime them, and either set keyframes by hand on the playhead or let auto-keyframing record changes as you scrub. For a fast start there are fade / move / scale presets you stack to play together or sequence; custom easing curves can be saved as Motion variables so every animation that references one updates at once. You scrub the timeline to preview and can leave time-based comments on a specific moment. It exports to MP4, WebM, GIF and (animated) SVG. The handoff payoff: in Dev Mode a Motion tab surfaces every timing value, easing curve and keyframe and lets a developer copy the animation as CSS, JSON, or framework-ready React (motion.dev). Why it matters for us: micro-interaction polish — button hovers, modal entrances, page transitions — is on nearly every site we ship, and today it means rebuilding the design in After Effects/Principle/Framer and then hand-translating the result into code, with the classic 'what easing did the designer actually mean?' loss at handoff. Motion lets the designer animate where they already design, and the dev copy the exact curve from the same file — collapsing the translation step that quietly eats hours and introduces drift.
blocks.doIt: (1) Open a file you can edit, select the frame to animate, and switch it to Motion mode (next to Design/Draw/Dev) — the timeline appears alongside the canvas. (2) Fast start: apply a preset (fade, move, or scale); stack several on the timeline to play together, or drag to sequence. (3) Refine: move the playhead and keyframe position / scale / rotation / opacity independently, or rely on auto-keyframing; drag layers on the timeline to adjust timing. (4) Reuse easing: save a custom curve as a Motion variable and reference it everywhere. (5) Preview by scrubbing; leave a time-based comment to flag a moment for a teammate. (6) Hand off: open Dev Mode → Motion and copy the animation as CSS (or JSON / React), or export the clip — then validate the exported easing/keyframes against your real front-end before shipping. Card: card-webdesign-figma-motion.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Figma's own blog ('Introducing Figma Motion' + the Config 2026 recap, primary/vendor) plus two independent press confirmations (TechCrunch and The Next Web both on the Jun 24 Config 2026 animations + code-layers news). Honest scope: Motion is open beta, available on all plans, but Starter exports are limited and full design-system integration + agent-assisted motion generation favor paid seats — so treat it as a workflow you can start today, with export gating to check on free plans. The Dev Mode CSS/React copy is vendor-claimed fidelity; eyeball the exported curve before trusting it on the critical path. Distinct from Code Layers ('code on the canvas'), which is NOT a run-it-today play — it begins rolling out in July 2026 via a waitlist. Joins the design-tooling/handoff branch (related to the Figma Dev Mode MCP design↔code card and the scroll-triggered-animations CSS card — the same 'design→production motion' story). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships an on-canvas animation timeline that exports production motion code, OR Figma Motion exits open beta to GA). Sources: Figma Blog ×2, TechCrunch, The Next Web.

sections.h: Social (organic) — run it today: ask Facebook's free Creator Assistant why your best Reel won, then have it pitch 3 angles off live trends

blocks.sub: Meta's Creator Assistant — a free conversational AI built into the Facebook creator dashboard (live since Jun 4 2026; Meta doubled down Jun 24 with a standalone Creator Studio app built on the same assistant) — answers questions against your OWN account data (why a post popped, when to post, how your audience shifted, what comments say) and brainstorms content ideas off live Facebook trends
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Meta's Creator Assistant, a free conversational AI for organic creators built into the Facebook creator dashboard, went live on Jun 4 2026 for creators in the US, Canada and India — and on Jun 24 2026 Meta doubled down, announcing a standalone Creator Studio companion app built around the same assistant (the app itself is in limited testing / waitlist; the assistant is the part you can use now). The assistant answers questions against your own account performance — why a specific Reel outperformed, when your audience is most active, how your audience has shifted over time, what people are saying in your comments — and brainstorms fresh content angles off live Facebook trends (trending audio, cultural moments, top-performing content styles), tailored to your niche, with conversational follow-ups. Why it matters for us: most 'AI for social' advice is a blank ChatGPT prompt that knows nothing about your account, or a generic best-time-to-post chart. A first-party coach that sees your real numbers turns 'I think the hook worked' into a diagnosed, repeatable variable — and one diagnosed win, drilled into hook/length/angle variations, becomes a week of posts. It's free, organic (no ad spend), and lives next to where the content already posts.
blocks.doIt: (1) Check eligibility: you need a creator account in the US, Canada or India; open the Facebook mobile app → creator dashboard and look for the assistant (no separate download). (2) Diagnose a winner: ask 'Why did [this specific Reel] outperform the rest?' and extract the repeatable variable (hook, length, audio, topic). (3) Read your audience: ask 'How has my audience changed over time?' and 'What are people saying in my comments?' to find a new segment or sentiment to lean into. (4) Time it: ask 'When should I post?' — it answers from your own engagement patterns, not a generic chart. (5) Brainstorm off trends: ask for fresh angles tied to what's trending for your niche; capture 3. (6) Iterate: follow up ('give me 3 hook variations for that angle') to spin one insight into a week of posts; A/B the cuts and export a brand PDF in Edits (card-social-edits-creator-analytics). Card: card-social-facebook-creator-assistant.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Meta Newsroom (about.fb.com, primary, Jun 4) + TechCrunch (Jun 4 launch and Jun 24 companion-app coverage) + Social Media Today (Jun 24 Creator Studio relaunch). Honest scope: geo-gated — live only in US/Canada/India right now (more markets 'over the coming months'), so verify region before relying on it; it lives in the Facebook creator dashboard (NOT inside Instagram, despite some headlines conflating the two); the standalone Creator Studio app is waitlist-only (the assistant is the run-it-today piece, the app is not). Meta describes the access path and example questions but doesn't publish a numbered click-path, so the exact prompts above are assembled from Meta's described capabilities — adapt to what your dashboard surfaces. Advances the Instagram/creator-tooling thread (the editor/dashboard becomes the analytics-and-ideas desk); related: card-social-edits-creator-analytics, card-social-instagram-your-algorithm, card-social-repurpose-claude. Logged a prediction (Creator Assistant expands beyond US/CA/India OR the standalone Creator Studio app exits waitlist by Q3-end). Sources: Meta Newsroom, TechCrunch ×2, Social Media Today.

sections.h: Paid — run it today: pack ONE Meta ad with up to 10 mixed image + video assets across aspect ratios, and let Advantage+ pick the winner per placement

blocks.sub: Meta's refreshed multimedia-ads guidance (reported Jun 24 2026, from Meta's own 'multi-media ads' help doc): stop splitting creative into a separate ad per placement — upload up to 10 images and videos, including multiple aspect ratios (9:16 + 1:1/4:5), into a single ad and let Advantage+ delivery optimize which variation each user sees
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Meta published refreshed best-practice guidance for its multimedia (multi-media) ads, written up Jun 24 2026 and traced to Meta's own help doc. The core instruction: upload all your assets into a single ad and let Meta handle delivery automatically — specifically up to 10 images and videos per ad, and you can include assets in different aspect ratios (e.g. 1:1 and 9:16) in that same ad. Meta's stated logic: 'the more creative options you provide, the more opportunities the delivery system has to optimize your delivery and performance,' picking the best-fitting variation per placement and user. Why it matters for us: operators still routinely build a separate ad per aspect ratio/placement, which splinters the conversion signal across many ad objects and fragments delivery. Consolidating diverse assets into one ad concentrates that signal and hands Meta's Andromeda-era delivery a richer combination space to optimize against — and it shifts the lever back to the thing you actually control: creative volume and variety, not audience micro-targeting. This is a creative-architecture move (how you pack assets inside the ad), distinct from the Advantage+ campaign-consolidation play (how you structure the campaign).
blocks.doIt: (1) In Ads Manager, create/open an ad and choose the Advantage+ / multimedia creative setup. (2) At the ad (creative) level, upload up to 10 assets into the one ad — deliberately mix at least one video with several images. (3) Include multiple aspect ratios in that single ad: 9:16 (vertical — most inventory) plus 1:1 or 4:5 for feed. (4) Make the assets conceptually distinct, not minor variants — e.g. a UGC clip, a product demo, a testimonial, a text-led explainer, a lifestyle shot (~5 genuinely different angles beats 50 near-duplicates). (5) Keep Advantage+ creative delivery ON so Meta auto-selects per placement/user; design within placement safe zones so key visuals survive cropping. (6) If any asset is AI-generated, set Meta's AI-content disclosure before launch; refresh assets every 2–4 weeks, watching CPM/reach as the fatigue signal. Card: card-paid-meta-multimedia-creative.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Meta's own 'multi-media ads' help doc (primary; the page loads and matches, though its body text wasn't machine-extractable) + Social Media Today (Jun 24, which quotes the doc for the '10 assets / mixed aspect ratios' figure); the broader 'diverse creative volume, let delivery optimize' strategy is independently echoed by agency sources (AdMove), pushing the general play toward Confirmed. Honest scope: the hard figure is up to 10 assets PER AD — don't conflate it with the larger asset-volume targets agencies recommend at the ad-set level (those are recommendations, not a Meta limit); this is creative best-practice guidance, not a dated product launch; and AI-content disclosure has been required since Mar 2026. Advances the platform-AI-defaults thread (related: card-paid-meta-advantage-plus, card-paid-ai-ad-creative, card-graphic-firefly-brand-batch-creatives). Logged a prediction (Meta raises the per-ad asset cap above 10 OR makes multimedia/multi-aspect-ratio the default creative setup). Sources: Meta for Business Help, Social Media Today, AdMove. Scan-line (not a card): Google began rolling out the June 2026 Spam Update globally Jun 24 (organic SEO — watch Search Console for ranking shifts), and is testing US-only auto-applied 'Strongest match' labels on Search ads (no advertiser controls yet — a watch-line, not runnable).

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, premarket/intraday vs close vary by source)

blocks.sub: 'AI capex is paying off' rebound flips the Jun 22–23 fear: Micron blew out fiscal Q3 (rev ~$41.5B vs ~$35.7B est, record 84.9% gross margin, Q4 guide ~$50B; HBM sold out through 2027) and jumped ~+16% premarket Jun 25 — and Morgan Stanley reframed the selloff as a 'crowded dip, not broken ROI,' RESOLVING our Jun 23 prediction ✅
blocks.p: What moved: this is decisively an 'AI-capex-is-paying-off' rebound week, flipping the Jun 22–23 global tech rout. Micron reported fiscal Q3 2026 revenue of ~$41.5B (vs ~$35.7B expected, ~16% beat), adjusted EPS ~$25.1 (vs ~$20.5), a record 84.9% gross margin, and guided Q4 to ~$50B revenue / ~86% margin — with data-center revenue ~$25B in the quarter, HBM4 shipments already >$1B, and HBM (high-bandwidth memory, the chips that feed AI accelerators) sold out through 2027 into 2028; the stock jumped ~+16% premarket Jun 25 (directional; after-hours prints ranged +13–15%). On the chip side, OpenAI + Broadcom unveiled 'Jalapeño' (Jun 24), OpenAI's first custom AI accelerator — a reticle-sized ASIC purpose-built for LLM inference (not training), TSMC 3nm, systolic-array design with eight HBM stacks, engineering samples already running workloads, initial deployment targeted for end-2026. And Qualcomm announced an all-stock acquisition of Modular (~$3.9B; the Mojo/MAX AI-compiler company founded by LLVM/Swift creator Chris Lattner — an explicit play at Nvidia's CUDA software moat, NOT a chip startup as some feeds framed it). The repricing question itself got answered: Morgan Stanley (Andrew Slimmon, Jun 24) framed the Jun 22–23 drop as AI beneficiaries being 'not expensive, but crowded' with the 'earnings-revision story validated' — a major sell-side note defending the AI thesis as a crowded dip, not broken ROI. Why it matters for us: this is a climate signal, not a trade. Two weeks ago the market punished AI on 'is the spend justified?'; this week the supply chain is visibly monetizing it. Client scrutiny shifts accordingly — from 'why are we spending on AI?' to 'show me the ROI, the unit economics (cost per outcome / perf-per-watt), and the IP provenance.' Prediction resolved ✅ [news, made 2026-06-23, due ~Jul 7]: 'within ~2 weeks, ≥1 US AI-megacap team or major sell-side note explicitly defends AI-capex ROI in response to the Jun 22–23 selloff' — the Morgan Stanley note (with Micron's call as supporting evidence) clears it. Corroboration: Micron beat + guide Confirmed (≥3 — Investing.com earnings-call transcripts, Futurum, CNBC); Jalapeño Confirmed (≥5 — OpenAI, Broadcom investor release, TechCrunch, CNBC, SiliconANGLE); Qualcomm–Modular Confirmed (≥3 — Yahoo Finance, Network World, Wired); Morgan Stanley note (Yahoo Finance). All percentages directional. Sources: Micron transcript (Investing.com), Broadcom/OpenAI, CNBC, Yahoo Finance.

blocks.sub: Counter-notes keep it honest: Cerebras sank on a soft margin guide (CEO says investors 'misunderstood' it), Oracle shed ~21,000 roles over the year (its 10-K ties cuts to AI), and Anthropic accused Alibaba's Qwen lab of an 'illicit' model-distillation campaign — sending Alibaba's HK shares ~-4.5% and surfacing AI-IP provenance as a live risk
blocks.p: The rebound is selective, not blanket. Cerebras (Nasdaq: CBRS, IPO'd May 2026) posted first-as-public earnings — revenue $193M (+94% YoY), loss narrowed — but the stock fell ~17–20% (directional) because FY gross-margin guidance of 38–41% undershot its ~47% Q1 core margin; CEO Andrew Feldman told CNBC investors 'misunderstood' the guide, attributing it to temporarily renting back its own systems from a big customer while it builds capacity, not pricing pressure. Oracle's annual 10-K shows headcount down to 141,000 from ~162,000 — roughly 21,000 roles (~13%) cut over fiscal 2026 — with the filing explicitly tying reductions to 'adoption and deployment of AI technologies,' even as capex surged ~162% to $55.7B for AI data-center buildout (the 'AI pays off for owners, not always for workers' edge of the same story). And on AI-IP: Anthropic accused operators linked to Alibaba's Qwen lab of the largest known 'distillation' campaign against it — ~28.8M interactions via ~25,000 fraudulent accounts (Apr 22–Jun 5) to 'illicitly' extract Claude's coding/agentic-reasoning capability — in a letter to US senators and the White House; Alibaba's HK shares (9988.HK) fell ~4.5% (directional) to a ~16-month low (YTD ~-33%), and Alibaba had not commented as of Jun 25 (these are Anthropic's allegations, untested). Why it matters for us: 'distillation' (training a cheaper model on a frontier model's outputs) becoming a public, geopolitical fight is the IP-provenance lesson going mainstream — expect clients (and procurement) to start asking where a model's capability came from and whether your AI deliverables are built on a defensible base. Thread note: the V-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20] is now leaning held — the Asian AI basket sits well above its Jun 8 reference (Samsung ~+21%, SK Hynix ~+53% to a record; SoftBank the lone laggard ~-7%); still open. Apple [due ~Jul 21] is still below its Jun 8 pre-WWDC level (~-3%) — open, currently trailing. Corroboration: Cerebras Confirmed (≥3 — TechCrunch, CNBC, Investing.com); Oracle Confirmed (≥3 — Yahoo Finance, CNBC, 10-K); Anthropic–Alibaba Confirmed (≥3 — Reuters, Investing.com, Bloomberg/CNBC), allegations untested. Sources: TechCrunch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, June 23
tldr: Web-dev play of the day: ship realtime — live chat, presence cursors, token-by-token AI streaming — straight from your existing Vercel Functions, with no separate always-on backend. On Jun 22 2026 Vercel moved WebSocket support into public beta: a Function can now hold a two-way WebSocket open using standard libraries (ws, Socket.IO, Hono, Express) with no extra config — the connection pins to one Fluid-compute instance and bills on Active CPU, so an idle open socket costs almost nothing. This closes a years-old gap that used to force a second box (Render/Railway/a dedicated Socket.IO server) onto every realtime build. Run-it-today: add one api/ws.ts that exports an HTTP server with a WebSocketServer, move shared room/presence state into Redis (instances aren't sticky), add client reconnect-with-backoff, deploy. (Public beta; Next.js needs the experimental experimental_upgradeWebSocket() API.)

Email play (a 10-minute DNS edit): close the phantom-subdomain spoofing hole that can quietly torch your sending reputation — add DMARCbis's new np=reject tag. DMARCbis, the long-awaited DMARC upgrade, is now a real standard — RFC 9989 (2026), which obsoletes the 2015 RFC 7489. Its headline operator change is the np= tag (Non-existent-subdomain Policy): set np=reject and inboxes reject mail from subdomains that don't exist in your DNS — the exact marketing-typo.yourbrand.com phantoms spoofers ride to borrow your domain's reputation. It also retires pct, rf and ri (use t=y for testing). With Gmail/Yahoo/Microsoft now rejecting non-compliant bulk mail outright, this is prerequisite-grade hygiene before any BIMI logo play — one record edit. (Honored by the big receivers; smaller/older providers may still fall back to sp=/p=.)

AI-tooling play: stop pressing "go" — define an agent loop once and let Claude Code run it unattended. Two commands already shipping in Claude Code turn babysitting into automation: /loop re-runs a prompt on a schedule (give it 5m for a cron interval, or no interval to let Claude self-pace 1-min–1-hr between checks), and /goal keeps a session working across turns until a separate, cheap evaluator model (defaults to Haiku) confirms your condition holds — e.g. "all tests in test/auth pass and lint is clean." The technique (Claire Vo's Jun 17 "loop engineering," echoed by Addy Osmani) is just: write the loop once, walk away. Run-it-today: /goal implement this design doc until the acceptance tests pass or stop after 20 turns, or drop a .claude/loop.md with your daily PR-watch instructions and trigger a bare /loop. (Session-scoped: fires only while Claude Code is open; recurring tasks expire after 7 days — for machine-off scheduling, graduate to Routines/GitHub Actions.)

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; figures directional): a US megacap wobble Mon Jun 22 spilled into a violent Asia session Tue Jun 23 — South Korea's Kospi fell ~8–10% (tripping circuit breakers twice), with SK Hynix and Samsung down double digits and foreigners net-selling ~₩5.8T (~$3.8B); Nikkei ~-3%, Nasdaq futures ~-2.5%. The why is two AI-specific scares, not a rates-only move: SpaceX fell ~16% Mon (worst day since its Jun 12 IPO; ~$400B wiped) after confirming a $20B bond sale to repay xAI-acquisition financing — read as cash-hungry AI capex — and Alphabet dropped ~5% on two marquee AI departures (Gemini's Noam Shazeer → OpenAI; DeepMind's John Jumper → Anthropic). For us this is a climate signal: clients will scrutinize AI work on two fronts — "is the spend justified?" and "is the moat/talent real?" — so lead pitches with cost-per-outcome and durability, not scale. (Also: OpenAI's Daybreak open-source-security initiative reported 51 issues found / 19 fixed in week one, single-source; the EU's €3 small-parcel customs duty on sub-€150 non-EU consignments goes live Jul 1.)

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is delete the standing thing you babysit — a box, a hole, a key-press. You no longer need a separate server just to hold a websocket, a human in the loop to re-run the tests, or an open door for spoofers on subdomains you never created. And the market just repriced that same instinct at scale: it has stopped paying for AI spend on faith and now wants the outcome. Define the loop, close the gap, prove the payoff — then aim the reclaimed hour at the next lever.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: add realtime (chat, presence, AI streaming) to your existing Vercel Functions with WebSocket support — no separate backend

blocks.sub: Vercel moved WebSocket support for Functions into public beta (Jun 22 2026): a Function can hold a two-way WebSocket open via standard libraries (ws, Socket.IO, Hono, Express) with no extra config — the connection pins to one Fluid-compute instance and bills on Active CPU (idle is near-free) — closing the years-old gap that forced a second always-on box onto every realtime build
blocks.p: What changed: on Jun 22 2026 Vercel moved WebSocket support for Vercel Functions into public beta. A Function can now hold a bidirectional WebSocket connection open using standard Node libraries — ws, Socket.IO, Hono, Express, Nitro/Nuxt-native — with no additional configuration: a WebSocket is just an HTTP GET carrying an Upgrade header, which Vercel pins to a single Fluid-compute function instance for the life of the connection. Billing runs on Active CPU pricing, so you pay only while the function is actually processing messages, not for idle connection time. This closes a gap that stood for years: WebSockets were previously impossible on Vercel's serverless platform, which is why every realtime feature meant standing up a second service. Why it matters for us: realtime — live chat, presence cursors, collaborative editing, token-by-token AI streaming — has been the #1 reason to bolt an always-on backend (Render, Railway, a dedicated Socket.IO box) onto an otherwise-static client build. You can now keep the whole project on Vercel and add a single api/ws.ts file, deleting a moving part out of every realtime quote — and the idle-time pricing means a chat widget that sits open all day doesn't bleed compute the way a long-lived container does.
blocks.doIt: (1) Confirm Fluid compute is on: it's the default for projects created on/after Apr 23 2025; for older projects, Vercel dashboard → your Project → Settings → Functions → Fluid compute → Enable. (2) Install the lib: npm i ws (or npm i socket.io socket.io-client). (3) Create api/ws.ts that builds an HTTP server and export defaults it: const server = http.createServer(); const wss = new WebSocketServer({ server }); wss.on('connection', ws => ws.on('message', d => ws.send(d))); export default server;. (4) Add client reconnect logic — connections close when the function hits its max duration, so wrap new WebSocket('wss://your-domain.com/api/ws') in a connect() that re-fires on close with exponential backoff (cap ~30s). (5) Move shared state out of memory: instances aren't sticky and a new deploy spins fresh ones, so put rooms/presence/pub-sub in Redis from the Vercel Marketplace, never in-process variables. (6) Deploy and test with wscat -c wss://your-domain.com/api/ws; for Next.js use the documented experimental_upgradeWebSocket() from @vercel/functions inside an app/api/ws/route.ts GET handler. Card: card-webdev-vercel-websockets.
blocks.note: Grounding: primary, single-vendor — Vercel's own changelog (the public-beta announcement), the Vercel Docs WebSockets-on-Functions page (exact api/ws.ts + Next.js/Hono/Express/Socket.IO examples), and the Vercel KB guide; three first-party Vercel pages (not independent corroboration), so this is a vendor-confirmed feature, not a 3-source claim. Honest scope: this is public beta (expect rough edges + possible limit changes); it requires Fluid compute (default only for projects created on/after Apr 23 2025); Next.js needs the experimental experimental_upgradeWebSocket API; there are no sticky sessions / no guaranteed instance affinity, so externalize ALL shared state or in-memory rooms silently break across reconnects and deploys; and chatty connections still bill real bytes (Active CPU + data transfer) — idle is cheap, busy is not. Joins the Vercel-platform thread; related to card-webdev-vercel-cancelable-jobs and card-webdev-vercel-workflow-nitro. Logged a prediction (WebSocket support reaches GA + Next.js drops the experimental_ prefix by Q3-end). Sources: Vercel Changelog, Vercel Docs, Vercel KB.

sections.h: Email — run it today: close the phantom-subdomain spoofing hole with DMARCbis's new np=reject tag (RFC 9989)

blocks.sub: DMARC is now a full IETF standard — RFC 9989 (2026) obsoletes the 2015 RFC 7489 — and its headline operator change is the np= tag (Non-existent-subdomain Policy): np=reject makes inboxes reject mail from subdomains that don't exist in your DNS (the phantoms spoofers ride to borrow your reputation), while pct/rf/ri are retired (use t=y for testing) — a 10-minute DNS edit, prerequisite-grade before any BIMI play
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: DMARC is no longer an informal 2015 spec. The IETF published RFC 9989 (the long-awaited DMARCbis upgrade), which obsoletes the original RFC 7489 and RFC 9091. The headline operator-facing change is a new np= tag (Non-existent-subdomain Policy) that lets you set none/quarantine/reject specifically for subdomains that don't exist in DNS — the exact phantom subdomains (marketing-typo.yourbrand.com) that spammers spoof to ride on your domain's reputation. RFC 9989 also moves pct, rf and ri to historic (deprecated) status, replacing percentage-rollout with a cleaner t=y testing flag. Why it matters for us: Gmail, Yahoo and Microsoft already reject non-compliant bulk mail at the SMTP level in 2026, so your deliverability — and your subscriber LTV — ride on domain auth being airtight. A spoofer exploiting a subdomain you never created can torch your domain reputation and land your real campaigns in spam; adding np=reject closes that hole in one DNS edit, and it's a prerequisite-grade move before any BIMI logo play. Ten minutes, outsized protection.
blocks.doIt: (1) Pull your current record: run dig TXT _dmarc.yourbrand.com +short (or a free DMARC lookup) and copy the existing string. (2) Confirm your base policy is enforcing — np only takes effect at the organizational domain, so make sure you already have p=quarantine or p=reject (if you're still on p=none, keep monitoring first, but you can safely add np now). (3) Add the new tag: edit the _dmarc TXT record to append np=reject — e.g. v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; np=reject; rua=mailto:reports@yourbrand.com; fo=1 (this rejects non-existent-subdomain mail while leaving your real sending subdomains under p/sp). (4) Remove deprecated tags: delete any pct=, rf= or ri=; if you used pct= for a staged rollout, switch to t=y (testing mode). (5) Publish and validate the record through a DMARC checker so it parses cleanly. (6) Watch your rua aggregate reports for 3–7 days to confirm legitimate subdomain mail still authenticates before tightening p further. Card: card-email-dmarcbis-np.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (4) — RFC 9989 (primary), plus Validity, dmarcwise and Suped independently confirming the np tag, the deprecation of pct/rf/ri, and the RFC 7489/9091 obsoletion. Honest scope: this is a standards-level change, not an ESP product drop (RFC 9989 finalized in 2026) — genuinely actionable now, but it's a spec to apply at your DNS, not a button in your ESP; np is honored by the big receivers but not universally (older/smaller providers may still fall back to sp=/p=); the record still begins v=DMARC1 (there is no v=DMARCbis — don't change the version); and BIMI still requires p=quarantine/reject separately. Advances the email deliverability thread (distinct from Gmail's Postmaster verdict and the third-party IPR seed test); related: card-email-dmarc-bimi, card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis, card-email-inbox-placement-audit. Logged a prediction (a major mailbox provider references RFC 9989 / np in sender guidance by Q3-end). Sources: RFC 9989, Validity, dmarcwise, Suped.

sections.h: AI tooling — run it today: define an agent loop once and let Claude Code run it unattended with /loop and /goal

blocks.sub: Two commands already in Claude Code turn babysitting into automation: /loop re-runs a prompt on a schedule (a cron interval like 5m, or self-paced when you give none) and /goal keeps a session working across turns until a SEPARATE cheap evaluator model (defaults to Haiku) confirms your 'done when' condition holds — the runnable substrate behind the Jun 17 'loop engineering' technique (Claire Vo / ChatPRD, echoed by Addy Osmani)
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: on Jun 17 2026 Claire Vo (ChatPRD) published a "How I AI" episode and companion Lenny's Newsletter post on loop engineering — designing autonomous agent loops instead of hand-prompting an agent turn by turn. The runnable substrate is already shipping in Claude Code. /loop re-runs a prompt on a cron interval (or a Claude-chosen one), and /goal keeps the session working across turns until a fresh, separate evaluator model confirms a condition holds (e.g. "all tests in test/auth pass and lint is clean"). Addy Osmani independently codified the same pattern (Jun 7) into four loop types — heartbeat, cron, hook, goal — plus worktrees, skills and verification subagents. The net: you stop being the person who presses "go" repeatedly and instead define the loop once. Why it matters for us: the team burns real hours babysitting deploys, chasing stale PRs and re-running test/lint cycles by hand — exactly the toil these loops absorb. A /goal loop with a separate evaluator (it defaults to Haiku, so checking is cheap) turns "implement this design doc until the acceptance criteria pass" into a walk-away task — a multiplier you can wire up today with zero new tools or infra, just commands already in the CLI.
blocks.doIt: (1) Confirm you're current: claude --version — /loop needs v2.1.72+, /goal needs v2.1.139+; accept the workspace-trust dialog (/goal runs through the hooks system). (2) Quick poll loop: type /loop 5m check if the deploy finished and tell me what happened — Claude converts 5m to cron, schedules it, returns a job ID (press Esc to stop while it waits). (3) Self-paced loop: type /loop check whether CI passed and address any review comments (no interval) — Claude picks a 1-min-to-1-hr delay per iteration and can end itself once provably done. (4) Work-until-done goal: type /goal all tests in test/auth pass and the lint step is clean or stop after 20 turns — a ◎ /goal active indicator shows runtime, a small fast model checks the condition after each turn and feeds any "no" back as guidance (bare /goal shows turns/tokens; /goal clear aborts). (5) Make a reusable default loop: create .claude/loop.md with your maintenance instructions ("check the release PR; if CI is red, pull the failing log, diagnose, push a minimal fix") so a bare /loop runs it. (6) Manage jobs in plain English ("what scheduled tasks do I have?" / "cancel the deploy check"); for schedule-sensitive runs use a minute that isn't :00/:30 to dodge the jitter offset. Card: card-ai-tooling-claude-loop-goal.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (4) — the official Claude Code docs (scheduled-tasks + goal pages, primary), Lenny's Newsletter/Claire Vo, and ChatPRD (Addy Osmani's loop-engineering post corroborates independently). Honest scope: /loop and /goal are session-scoped — they fire only while Claude Code is running and idle, recurring tasks auto-expire 7 days after creation, and there's no catch-up for missed fires (a fresh conversation clears tasks; --resume restores unexpired ones). The /goal evaluator judges only what Claude surfaces in the transcript — it doesn't run commands or read files itself — so conditions must be provable from Claude's own output. For unattended, machine-off scheduling you graduate to Routines (Anthropic cloud, 1-hour minimum), Desktop scheduled tasks, or GitHub Actions; some Bedrock/Vertex behaviors differ. Advances the multi-agent-orchestration thread (now a cross-tool storyline with Codex's /goal); related: card-ai-tooling-codex-goal, card-ai-tooling-claude-workflows. Logged a prediction (Anthropic relaxes the 7-day expiry or ships an in-CLI renew by Q3-end). Sources: Claude Code Docs (scheduled-tasks), Claude Code Docs (goal), Lenny's Newsletter, ChatPRD.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; all market figures directional, intraday vs close varies by source)

blocks.sub: Global tech rout: a US megacap wobble Mon Jun 22 spilled into Asia Tue Jun 23 — Kospi ~-8–10% (circuit breakers tripped twice), SK Hynix & Samsung down double digits, foreigners net-selling ~₩5.8T (~$3.8B); the trigger was two AI-specific scares — SpaceX's $20B bond (capex cash-hunger; ~-16%, ~$400B wiped) and Alphabet's AI-talent exits (~-5%)
blocks.p: What moved: a US megacap tech wobble on Mon Jun 22 spilled into a violent Asia session on Tue Jun 23, hammering the AI/semiconductor complex. South Korea's Kospi fell on the order of 8–10% (Korea Times put the close at -9.99%, 8,203.84; Yahoo captured intraday -8.11%), tripping circuit breakers twice; SK Hynix ~-11.5 to -12.5% and Samsung ~-8.8 to -12.3%; the Nikkei ~-3% (snapping an 8-session streak) and Nasdaq futures ~-2.5%; foreign investors net-sold ~₩5.79T (~$3.8B) of Korean shares. The why is two AI-specific triggers plus a structural amplifier, not a rates-only move. (1) AI-capex confidence shock — SpaceX fell ~16.4% Mon (worst day since its Jun 12 IPO) after confirming a $20B bond sale to repay bridge financing from its xAI acquisition — read as a cash-hungry AI-infrastructure signal that wiped ~$400B in value and "rattled confidence in AI capital spending." (2) AI-talent / commoditization fear — Alphabet closed ~-5% (intraday as much as -7.2%), its worst day in over a year, after two high-profile departures: Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer → OpenAI and DeepMind VP / Nobel laureate John Jumper (AlphaFold) → Anthropic. (3) Structural amplifier: Taiwan/Korea/Japan benchmarks were each up 40%+ YTD on a concentrated semiconductor trade — the most exposed names when US megacap sentiment turned; Korean analysts (Kiwoom) framed it as profit-taking / short-term correction layered with rate-hike jitters. Net read: an AI-capex-scrutiny + talent-flight + crowded-trade unwind. Why it matters for us: this is a climate signal, not a trade — the market just punished AI on the two questions clients will now mirror, "is the spend justified?" (SpaceX/capex) and "is the talent/moat real?" (Alphabet exits). Frame AI pitches around defensible, cost-per-outcome results and durability, and expect "is this a bubble?" pushback in the room. Thread impact: this pressures but does not resolve our open "dip not regime change" V-shape call — the basket (SoftBank, Samsung, SK Hynix) now sits well below its Jun 8 reference, so the bar to clear it by the ~Jul 20 due date just rose; still open. Corroboration: selloff size/scope Confirmed (≥3 — Yahoo Finance, Korea Times, Bloomberg, CNBC); SpaceX $20B bond / ~-16% / ~$400B Confirmed (≥3 — Yahoo, beincrypto, techtimes, CNBC); Alphabet ~-5% + the two exits Confirmed (≥3 — Bloomberg, Yahoo, CNBC). All percentages directional, intraday vs close vary by source. Sources: Yahoo Finance, Korea Times, CNBC.

blocks.sub: Also: OpenAI's Daybreak open-source-security initiative (week one: 51 issues found, 19 fixed across 19 projects — single-source) and the EU's €3 small-parcel customs duty on sub-€150 non-EU consignments goes live Jul 1 2026 (Confirmed)
blocks.p: Two smaller landscape markers. OpenAI Daybreak — a cyber-defense program run with Trail of Bits — reported its first week of "Patch the Planet": across 19 open-source projects it found 51 issues and had 19 already fixed, pitching AI that bakes defense into software from the start and cuts analysis time "hours to minutes" (Engadget, Jun 23 — single-source on the specifics, treat the counts as Reported/directional). Separately, the EU's €3 small-parcel customs duty goes live Jul 1 2026: non-EU consignments under €150 carry a fixed €3 customs duty (applied per tariff heading, collected via IOSS), with a separate ~€2 handling fee still under negotiation — directly relevant to any client running cross-border DTC/ecommerce into the EU (EU Council/Consilium + KPMG + VAT trade press, Confirmed). Why it matters for us: the Daybreak data reinforces the "treat your toolchain/dependencies as an attack surface" lesson we keep teaching on the dev side; the EU duty is a concrete July cost change to flag in any cross-border commerce build or pitch. Sources: Engadget, EU Council (Consilium).
