reports.title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, June 17
tldr: Web-design play of the day: stop letting AI guess your UI from a screenshot — hand it your real Figma data with the Dev Mode MCP server. A Jun 16 Figma post shows the workflow maturing both directions. Design→code: enable the Dev Mode MCP server (Figma → Preferences → 'Enable Dev Mode MCP Server'), connect Claude Code / Cursor / VS Code, select ONE frame, and prompt 'implement the selected frame, reuse my existing components and variables.' The agent gets the real node tree, design tokens/variables, layout constraints and assets — not a flat image — so the code uses your design system instead of one-off CSS. Code→canvas: via Figma Make, prompt 'bring this preview back into Figma as design layers' to round-trip a built screen into editable frames — killing the design/code drift that eats teams. (Needs a Figma plan with Dev Mode; keep selections small and review the diff — MCP gives structure, not pixel-perfection.)

Graphic play: kill 'random off-brand AI art' at the source — connect your Canva Brand Kit to your AI assistant. Canva now plugs into the major assistants: the new Perplexity Computer connector (~Jun 4) turns research/briefs into editable Canva decks, social sets, infographics and brand kits across 11 languages, joining the Claude (Apr) and ChatGPT/Gemini Brand Kit connectors. Set your Brand Kit once (hex colors, fonts, logo, voice), then prompt 'turn this into an on-brand deck using my Canva Brand Kit' — the first draft already uses your palette/type/voice and lands as editable Canva layers, not a dead PNG. Bonus: Magic Layers (now in Gemini & ChatGPT) converts a flat AI image into a layered, editable Canva design. (Brand Kit needs Pro/Teams.)

Social play: finally prove your X presence drives sales — wire up no-code conversion tracking. X rebuilt its Ads Manager (announced Jun 16) with three measurement wins: a Google Tag Manager integration that deploys the X Pixel + Conversion API (CAPI) through a guided no-code flow, all CAPI resources consolidated in Events Manager, and a real-time conversion diagnostics dashboard to spot and fix broken events. The dev-time barrier that left most teams' X tracking unset is gone — connect your GTM container, define purchase/signup/lead events, fire a test, confirm Pixel + CAPI show healthy, then judge X on conversions instead of likes.

Also moved (scan, not card-worthy): the G7 wrapped with OpenAI, Anthropic and Google at the table on AI safeguards (China pushed a parallel safety track, outside the summit); SpaceX ticked up after passing Amazon in market cap post-IPO; Pinterest opened an experimental 'Ask Pinterest' AI shopping app and Figma shipped its own AI agent. Climate, not a play — every figure here is one to cross-check before you repeat it to a client.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is connect your source of truth — don't let the AI guess. Hand the coder your Figma tokens, not a screenshot; hand the assistant your Brand Kit, not a vibe; hand your analytics the real conversion event, not a like. The unglamorous plumbing — the MCP server, the Brand Kit connector, the GTM/CAPI hookup — is the half that makes AI output structured, on-brand and measured enough to ship to a client.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: turn a Figma frame into code (and code back into canvas) with the Dev Mode MCP server

blocks.sub: Figma's Dev Mode MCP server hands Claude Code / Cursor your real design data — node tree, variables, layout, assets — instead of a screenshot, so generated code uses your components; a Jun 16 Figma post shows the code↔canvas round-trip maturing
blocks.p: What changed / why surface it now: Figma published '4 ways we're using our MCP server at Figma' (Jun 16, 2026), and the useful, replicable throughline for our builds is that the Dev Mode MCP server now works cleanly both directions. The core capability: instead of pasting a screenshot into an AI coding tool and hoping it guesses spacing and colors, the MCP server passes the structured design — the layer/node tree, design tokens and variables, layout constraints, and asset references — straight to Claude Code, Cursor or VS Code. Because the agent sees your actual components and tokens, the code it writes reuses your design system rather than inventing one-off CSS. The Jun 16 post adds the return trip: Figma staff move fluidly code→canvas via Figma Make ('bring this preview back into Figma as design layers'; 'push this dashboard into Figma, reuse my existing components and variables') — the MCP server reads the design library and the codebase together and reuses existing components instead of duplicating them. Why it matters for us: the expensive, recurring failure on client builds is design/code drift — the coded UI slowly diverges from the Figma source until neither is trustworthy. A structured design→code handoff plus a code→canvas round-trip closes that loop: a designer's frame becomes real, on-system code in one prompt, and a dev's change can come back as editable layers. This is a different web-design lever from the recent native-CSS-primitives thread (squircles, carousels, anchor positioning) — it's the design-system/handoff half of frontend craft.
blocks.doIt: (1) On a Figma plan that includes Dev Mode, open a design file → Figma menu → Preferences → check 'Enable Dev Mode MCP Server' (starts a local MCP server). (2) Connect your AI tool: in Claude Code add the Figma MCP server (Figma Learn's 'Claude Code and Figma: Set up the MCP server' has the exact steps); in Cursor/VS Code add it under MCP settings — confirm the figma tools appear. (3) Design→code: select ONE frame or component in Figma, then prompt 'implement the selected frame; reuse my existing components, variables and tokens' (small selections = accurate output). (4) Code→canvas: in Figma Make, prompt 'bring this preview back into Figma as design layers' or 'push this screen into Figma, reuse existing components and variables' to round-trip a built UI into editable frames. (5) Review the diff — MCP gives structure, not pixel-perfection; spot-check spacing, tokens and accessibility before committing. (6) Try it on one real client component first to feel the speed-up, then fold it into the design-system workflow. Card: card-webdesign-figma-mcp-design-to-code.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: the official Dev Mode MCP server requires a paid Figma plan with Dev Mode; output quality drops if you select a huge or messy frame, so go component-by-component and treat the result as a strong first pass to review, not final code. The Jun 16 post describes Figma's internal uses (some via custom in-house skills), but the design→code and Figma-Make code↔canvas moves are generally available capabilities you can run today. Joins the design-to-code/design-system handoff story (related: card-webdesign-ai-ready-design-system, card-graphic-figma-capture-layers). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships a comparable bidirectional code↔canvas sync). Sources: Figma Blog (×2), Figma Learn.

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: make AI design on-brand from the first draft by connecting your Canva Brand Kit to your AI assistant

blocks.sub: Canva now plugs into Claude, ChatGPT/Gemini and (new, ~Jun 4) Perplexity Computer — pin generation to your Brand Kit so AI assets arrive on-brand and editable, not as generic art you have to redo
blocks.p: What changed: Canva keeps wiring its design engine into the AI assistants teams already work in, and the freshest hook is the Canva connector for Perplexity Computer (announced ~Jun 4, 2026) — paid Perplexity users (Pro/Max/Enterprise) turn AI-generated research and briefs into editable Canva presentations, social campaigns, infographics, pitch docs, brand kits and templates, across 11 languages. It joins the existing Claude connector (Apr) and the ChatGPT/Gemini Brand Kit integrations, plus Magic Layers (Jun 7) which now turns a flat AI image in Gemini/ChatGPT into a layered, editable Canva design. The common thread that matters for brand work: all of these let you apply your Brand Kit — colors, fonts, logo, voice — from the first draft, and the output stays editable inside Canva rather than arriving as a flat export. Why it matters for us: the standing complaint about AI design is that it produces random, off-brand outputs that take as long to fix as to make from scratch — exactly the 'demand editable, on-brand assets' lesson this domain keeps teaching. Pinning generation to a Brand Kit fixes it at the source: the assistant does the research/brief, Canva renders it in your palette and type, and your team finishes editable layers — so AI speeds up on-brand production instead of creating cleanup work. It's the brand-discipline counterpart to the Recraft (editable SVG) and Ideogram (structured JSON briefs) plays.
blocks.doIt: (1) In Canva, build/confirm your Brand Kit — brand colors (exact hex), fonts, logo, and (where available) brand voice: Brand Hub → Brand Kit (needs Canva Pro/Teams). (2) Connect Canva to your assistant: in Perplexity (Pro/Max/Enterprise) add the Canva connector in Perplexity Computer; in Claude or ChatGPT enable the Canva app/connector. (3) Do the research/brief in the assistant (meeting notes, performance data, live web context), then prompt: 'Turn this into an on-brand [deck / 5 social posts / infographic] using my Canva Brand Kit.' (4) Open the result in Canva — it lands as editable assets; refine copy/layout, swap images, finalize. (5) Got a flat AI image to edit? Use Magic Layers (Gemini/ChatGPT) to convert it into a layered, editable Canva design. (6) Save winners as reusable Canva templates so the next on-brand asset is one prompt away. Card: card-graphic-canva-brand-kit-ai.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — 9to5Mac, Storyboard18 and Social Samosa independently report the Perplexity Computer connector (Canva's own on-brand-in-AI-assistants announcement backs the Brand Kit capability). Honest notes: the connector requires a paid Perplexity tier and Brand Kit features sit on Canva Pro/Teams; on-brand still means review — the model applies your kit, it doesn't guarantee good art direction, so treat outputs as strong first drafts. Advances the on-brand/editable-AI-asset story (related: card-graphic-color-palette [team-rated 5★], card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major design tool ships a Brand-Kit-aware connector into a major AI assistant). Sources: 9to5Mac, Storyboard18, Social Samosa, Canva.

sections.h: Social — run it today: prove your X presence drives sales with no-code conversion tracking (GTM + CAPI + live diagnostics)

blocks.sub: X rebuilt its Ads Manager (Jun 16) with a guided no-code Google Tag Manager setup for the Pixel + Conversion API, consolidated CAPI tools in Events Manager, and a real-time conversion-diagnostics dashboard — remove the dev-time barrier and finally attribute conversions to X
blocks.p: What changed: on Jun 16, 2026 X announced a rebuilt Ads Manager with three measurement-focused updates. (1) A Google Tag Manager integration that lets you set up the X Pixel + Conversion API (CAPI) directly in Ads Manager through a guided, no-code experience — 'minimizing the need for in-depth developer support.' (2) Consolidated developer tools: all CAPI resources now live together in Events Manager, a single place to manage integrations. (3) A real-time conversion diagnostics dashboard to monitor Pixel and CAPI event health and 'quickly spot and resolve issues.' All three are rolling out this month. Why it matters for the social team: the reason most brands can't say whether X is worth the effort is that conversion tracking never got set up — it historically needed a developer to wire the pixel and a server-side CAPI feed, so the work got deprioritized and X ran 'blind.' A no-code GTM path plus a live health dashboard removes that excuse: you can attribute signups, leads and purchases to X activity, see at a glance when an event breaks, and make the keep-or-cut call on X with data instead of vanity likes. It's the measurement half of the same discipline this domain keeps teaching — design for the signal that actually proves the channel.
blocks.doIt: (1) Open X Ads Manager → Events Manager (the rebuilt one) and start the Google Tag Manager integration. (2) Connect your GTM container through the guided flow — it deploys the X Pixel + Conversion API (CAPI) without hand-coding tags. (3) Define your key conversion events (purchase, signup, lead) and map them to the right pages/actions. (4) Open the real-time conversion diagnostics dashboard, fire a test event, and confirm both Pixel and CAPI events show as healthy. (5) Fix anything flagged (event deduplication, missing parameters) using the consolidated CAPI tools in Events Manager. (6) Once events are clean, judge your X presence on conversions — not likes — and reallocate effort accordingly. Card: card-social-x-conversion-tracking.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Social Media Today, Social Samosa and PPC Land independently report the Jun 16 GTM integration, consolidated CAPI tools and live diagnostics (X Business docs back the underlying conversion-tracking setup). Honest scope: this lives in Ads Manager and is measurement-first — it serves organic-social teams who need to prove X drives conversions as much as paid; a full server-side CAPI setup may still want a server-side GTM container, but the guided flow covers the standard Pixel+CAPI case with no code. Cross-check the rollout reaching your account (phased this month). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major social platform ships an equivalent no-code GTM/CAPI conversion-setup flow). Sources: Social Media Today, Social Samosa, PPC Land, X Business.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, June 16
tldr: Web-dev play of the day: run long AI/agent jobs on Vercel — and now kill them mid-flight. Two changes land back-to-back: Vercel Functions can run up to 30 minutes (1,800s, up from 800s — Jun 15, Fluid Compute, Pro/Enterprise, beta over 800s), and today (Jun 16) the Workflow SDK adds inflight cancellation via the standard AbortController — the cancel signal survives suspensions and replays and crosses step boundaries. Together that's the missing half of durable execution: a report build, a multi-model pipeline, a scrape can now run for half an hour serverless and be aborted the instant the user cancels — so a runaway step can't quietly burn compute. (Cancellation is cooperative: the step must watch the signal or pass it to fetch.)

Email play: stop trusting your ESP's 'delivery rate' — measure inbox PLACEMENT. A 98% delivery rate can hide a ~60% inbox rate: 'delivered' only means the mailbox provider accepted the message, not that it reached the inbox. With Gmail/Yahoo/Microsoft now permanently rejecting (not just spam-foldering) senders who miss the rules — spam-complaint ceiling 0.30%, target under 0.10% — an unmeasured placement gap is lost revenue your client's dashboard says is fine. Run a seed/spam-list test on one live campaign and read folder placement per provider before you send. (Litmus.)

Paid play: Google Ads is recalibrating how budget-limited Smart Bidding hits your targets — re-baseline before Aug 17. Three changes: (1) a backend bidding-target optimization for budget-constrained tCPA/tROAS campaigns (account notices ~Jul 6, rollout starts Aug 17, expect a short calibration wobble); (2) Promotion Mode (beta) to schedule a temporary ROAS-tolerance loosen + extra daily budget around sales spikes; (3) Smart Bidding Exploration now global for Search + feed-less PMax, with a new Shopping beta. Audit your limited-by-budget campaigns and pull stale targets toward real trailing actuals now. (Dates per the announcement — directional until the in-account notice lands.)

AI landscape (news, cross-checked): ChatGPT dropped below 50% assistant share for the first time. Sensor Tower's app-usage data (end-May) puts ChatGPT at 46.4%, Gemini 27.7% (the gainer), Claude 10.3% — note this is app/assistant usage, not web traffic (StatCounter still shows ChatGPT ~80% of chatbot web visits; cite the right metric). Alongside: SpaceX agreed to buy Cursor (Anysphere) for ~$60B, close ~Q3 2026 (Reported; deal structure not primary-confirmed), and the SpaceX IPO total grew to $85.7B after the greenshoe. The read for us: client AI work gets judged in a multi-model world — design for portability, lead with cost-per-outcome.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is add the off-switch, then scale — a 30-minute job needs a cancel signal, a billing-default needs a re-baselined target, a 'delivered' number needs a placement check, a single-model build needs a fallback. The capability is the easy half; the guardrail that stops the silent failure — the abort, the threshold, the seed-test, the second model — is the half that makes it safe to ship to a client.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: run a 30-minute AI/agent job on Vercel and cancel it mid-flight (Functions 30-min + Workflow SDK inflight cancellation)

blocks.sub: Vercel Functions now run up to 30 min (Jun 15) and the Workflow SDK adds standard-AbortController cancellation that survives suspensions/replays (Jun 16) — together, cancelable long-running jobs with no extra infra
blocks.p: What changed (two changelog entries, back to back): (1) Jun 15 — Vercel Functions' max duration rose to 30 minutes (1,800s, up from 800s) for Node.js and Python on Pro/Enterprise, on Fluid Compute (which bills CPU only during active execution and pauses during I/O waits); anything over 800s is in beta. (2) Jun 16 (today) — the Workflow SDK (workflow@beta) added inflight cancellation built on the standard Web AbortController/AbortSignal: the cancel propagates across workflow and step boundaries and stays durable across suspensions and deterministic replay, so a step running as a separate function invocation still sees the abort. Cancellation is cooperative — the step has to inspect the signal (or hand it to a signal-aware API like fetch) to actually stop. Why it matters for our builds: the long client jobs we used to push onto a queue/worker — PDF or video generation, a multi-model AI pipeline, a scrape, a nightly report build — previously hit the 800s wall. Half an hour serverless clears most of them with no infra to stand up. And cancellation is the piece that makes it safe: race a durable timeout against a slow model, cancel the siblings after the first success, or kill the whole pipeline the moment the user navigates away — without leaking compute spend, because Fluid only bills active CPU. It's the same 'never ship an AI feature without a cost ceiling' discipline, now native to the runtime. This is a fresh advance on the durable-execution thread (distinct from the Jun 13 Nitro-v3 integration), not a repeat.
blocks.doIt: (1) On a Pro/Enterprise team, turn on Fluid Compute (Project → Settings → Functions). (2) Install the beta SDK: npm i workflow@beta. (3) Raise the limit on the long route — Next.js App Router: add export const maxDuration = 1800; to the route file; other frameworks: { "functions": { "api/long-task.py": { "maxDuration": 1800 } } } in vercel.json. (4) Make it cancelable — create an AbortController, thread controller.signal into every step/fetch you want killable, and call controller.abort() on the losing branch of a Promise.race (e.g. real work vs a sleep('30s') timeout). (5) Deploy, trigger a run, and confirm the cancel propagates in Dashboard → Observability → Workflows. (6) Put a hard dollar cap in front of any model calls inside the job (card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits) so even a 30-minute window can't run away. Card: card-webdev-vercel-cancelable-jobs.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: both the >800s/30-min tier and the Workflow SDK (incl. cancellation) are beta — pin workflow@beta and expect API churn before GA. Cancellation is cooperative, not preemptive: a CPU-bound loop that never checks the signal won't stop, so pass signal into your awaits. Same-day bonus: the Workflow SDK also added TanStack Start support via the workflow/vite plugin — relevant if a client app is on TanStack. Logged a prediction (the duration tier + cancellation reach GA by ~Sep 16). Sources: Vercel Changelog (×2), Vercel Docs.

sections.h: Email — run it today: audit inbox PLACEMENT, not 'delivery rate' (the metric that's quietly costing your client revenue)

blocks.sub: A 98% delivery rate can sit on a ~60% inbox-placement rate — and now that Gmail/Yahoo/Microsoft permanently reject rule-breakers, the gap is lost revenue, not a spam-folder slap. Measure it with a seed/spam test before you send.
blocks.p: What's the play: the number every ESP shows you — delivery rate — only means the mailbox provider accepted the message; it says nothing about whether it landed in the inbox or the spam folder. So a campaign can report 98% delivered while its real inbox-placement rate (IPR) is ~60% — a third of the audience never sees it, and the dashboard looks healthy. Why this is timely, not evergreen filler: through late 2025 into 2026 Gmail, Yahoo and Microsoft moved enforcement from soft-fail to permanent rejection (e.g. SPF 5.7.27, Microsoft 550 5.7.515). The bar is now hard: spam-complaint rate must stay under 0.30% (target under 0.10%), bounces under ~2%, with working one-click unsubscribe (RFC 8058). Miss it and you're blocked at the gateway, not filtered. Why it matters for our work: clients pay for revenue, and revenue tracks inbox placement — so a client whose IPR has quietly slid to 70% is losing 3-in-10 emails of pipeline while their ESP report says everything's fine. Turning that invisible leak into a measured number is a billable win and the seed of a recurring deliverability retainer.
blocks.doIt: (1) Pick one live, high-revenue campaign (a top automation or the next broadcast) and use the real final creative — not a stripped test. (2) Run a seed/spam-list test: in Litmus, Test tab → 'View and create spam tests' (or ESP Sync the draft) — it checks placement across ~11 providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Office 365, Outlook, AOL, GMX…). (3) Read folder placement per provider: Primary = pass, Promotions/Tabs = partial, Spam/Junk = reputation problem, Missing = blocked at the gateway. (4) Compute IPR = (inbox ÷ delivered) × 100; flag any major provider under ~85%. (5) Check the authentication row (SPF, DKIM, DMARC, one-click List-Unsubscribe, TLS, BIMI) — any red here is the first fix, because it's what triggers permanent rejection (card-email-dmarc-bimi). (6) Cross-check live reputation in Google Postmaster Tools (spam rate under 0.10%) and Microsoft SNDS. (7) Fix failing auth, prune low-engagement/invalid addresses, re-test until IPR clears the threshold, then ship. (8) Productize it as a one-page 'Inbox Placement Scorecard' for the client. Card: card-email-inbox-placement-audit.
blocks.note: Grounded in Litmus's deliverability guidance (the delivery-vs-placement reframing + the spam-test method) and the 2026 Gmail/Yahoo sender thresholds (0.10%/0.30%, permanent-rejection codes). The exact IPR figures are illustrative of the gap, not a promise for your list — the point is to measure yours. Fresh angle vs recent email beats (not the Omnisend MCP, not the micro-animation GIF). Logged a prediction (a major ESP surfaces IPR as a default report metric by end-Q3). Sources: Litmus (×2), Chronos Agency (2026 sender requirements).

sections.h: Paid — run it today: re-baseline your budget-limited Smart Bidding targets before Google's Aug 17 recalibration (+ two more Google Ads bidding changes)

blocks.sub: Google is changing how budget-constrained tCPA/tROAS campaigns optimize (rollout Aug 17), shipping Promotion Mode (beta) to schedule a temporary ROAS-loosen + extra budget for sales spikes, and taking Smart Bidding Exploration global — audit your limited-by-budget campaigns now
blocks.p: What changed — three Google Ads bidding/budgeting updates: (1) Bidding-target optimization for budget-limited campaigns (a backend change): Google is recalibrating how budget-constrained tCPA/tROAS campaigns optimize toward their targets so performance stays more predictable as budgets scale. Timeline (cross-checked across Search Engine Journal and Search Engine Roundtable): account notifications begin ~Jul 6, rollout starts Aug 17 and takes a few weeks, with a brief calibration window of 'minor performance fluctuations.' (2) Promotion Mode (beta) for Search + Performance Max: schedule a temporary change to ROAS tolerance plus extra daily budget around a known demand spike (launch, seasonal event, flash sale) — a native lever that replaces the manual budget/tROAS hacks teams do during sales. (3) Smart Bidding Exploration expanded globally across all languages for Search and feed-less PMax (finds incremental converting queries beyond your normal targeting without forcing you to loosen ROAS much), with a new Shopping beta for standard Shopping + PMax with feeds. Why it matters for us: update (1) will quietly move spend and CPA/ROAS on every budget-capped campaign during calibration — if a client's targets are stale or aspirational, you'll see efficiency swing and could misread a normal wobble as a bug, or let a too-loose target overspend. (3) is close to free incremental volume for accounts that feel query-starved. Get ahead of it on purpose.
blocks.doIt: (1) Find the exposed campaigns: Campaigns table → add the 'Budget' status column or filter Status: Limited by budget; flag every Search/PMax/Shopping campaign showing it. (2) For each, open Settings → Bidding and record the current Target CPA / Target ROAS, then pull trailing 30–90-day actual CPA/ROAS (Columns → add Conv., Cost/conv., Conv. value/cost). (3) Re-baseline to reality before Aug 17 — if your set target is far from trailing actuals, move it toward an achievable number so the recalibration optimizes against a sane goal. (4) Set a notifications watch (bell icon) for the bidding-target notice ~Jul 6, and screenshot it for the account log. (5) Enable Smart Bidding Exploration where you want volume (Settings → Bidding on Search / feed-less PMax); check eligibility for the Shopping beta. (6) Pre-stage Promotion Mode on your top promo campaigns now so it's ready to schedule for the next sale instead of a manual override. (7) Diary an Aug 17–Sep 7 'calibration watch': annotate accounts, hold big manual bid edits for the first ~2 weeks, judge on a rolling 14-day window, not day-over-day. Card: card-paid-google-bidding-recalibration.
blocks.note: Cross-checked: SEJ and Search Engine Roundtable agree on the Aug 17 rollout, the ~Jul 6 notifications, Promotion Mode's scheduled ROAS-tolerance + extra-budget mechanic, and the Smart Bidding Exploration global expansion + Shopping beta. Treat the Jul 6 / Aug 17 dates as the announced schedule — directional until the in-account notice lands. Secondary (worth a look, not a deadline): Microsoft Advertising launched Product Explorer — a Merchant Center tool to search/filter your catalog by feed attributes + performance, surface rejected/underperforming SKUs, and export lists (US advertisers, under 100k SKUs). Reuses the durable paid lesson: keep a guarded manual baseline to prove the AI's lift on YOUR account. Logged a prediction (Promotion Mode reaches GA for Search + PMax by end-2026). Sources: Search Engine Journal (×2), Search Engine Roundtable.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape, cross-checked: ChatGPT slips below 50% assistant share; SpaceX buys Cursor for ~$60B; SpaceX IPO total hits $85.7B
sections.intro: Markets/landscape = intake, not a craft card — but it's the climate every client conversation happens in. Every figure here is cross-checked against ≥2 independent sources; fast-moving prints are flagged directional.

blocks.sub: ChatGPT fell below 50% of AI-assistant usage for the first time — Gemini is the gainer (be precise about which metric)
blocks.p: What the data says: per Sensor Tower's end-of-May app-usage reading, ChatGPT is at 46.4% of AI-assistant usage (above 50% until January 2026), Google Gemini 27.7% (the clear gainer, riding Google-ecosystem integration), and Anthropic Claude 10.3%; Grok/Perplexity/DeepSeek/Meta AI together under 5%. The critical caveat — and the thing to get right when a client cites it: this is app/assistant usage share. A different metric, StatCounter web traffic, still puts ChatGPT near ~80% of chatbot web visits. So 'below 50%' is true and newsworthy for app usage, not a claim that ChatGPT lost the web. Why it matters for us: the assistant market is genuinely multi-model now, which is exactly why we keep building AI features for portability (a gateway with a fallback model, card-ai-tooling-model-portability) rather than hard-wiring one vendor — and why client pitches should lead with cost-per-outcome over brand allegiance.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — TechCrunch + Yahoo Finance, both attributing the Sensor Tower dataset. The 46.4/27.7/10.3 split is one tracker's app-usage estimate — directional, and explicitly NOT web-traffic share. Advances the AI-landscape thread (open-vs-closed / assistant market).

blocks.sub: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor (Anysphere) for ~$60B; SpaceX IPO total grew to $85.7B after the greenshoe
blocks.p: Two deal items extend the SpaceX arc we covered on Jun 12–13. (1) SpaceX → Cursor: SpaceX signed a definitive agreement to acquire AI-coding startup Cursor/Anysphere at ~$60B equity value, expected to close Q3 2026 — feeding xAI (which SpaceX merged with in February) a foothold in AI coding; Cursor is reportedly at ~$2.6B annualized revenue, and SpaceX exercised an April option to buy rather than partner. (2) SpaceX IPO: underwriters exercised the overallotment/greenshoe, lifting total proceeds from the Jun 12 debut to $85.7B (~83M extra shares; priced $135, closed ~$161, valuation past $2T). Why it matters for us: AI coding is consolidating into a SpaceX/xAI mega-entity alongside Anthropic and OpenAI — another reason a team's toolchain shouldn't single-point on one vendor's roadmap (keep it swap-ready). The market read stays risk-on for marquee AI/frontier bets even as chip names reprice on spend-scrutiny — a climate signal (lead with cost-per-outcome), not a trade.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Cursor deal = Reported (Reuters/Investing.com + Yahoo/Reuters + Bloomberg headline) — the ~$60B value and Q3-2026 close are confirmed, but the 'all-stock'/merger-sub structure is NOT primary-confirmed, so treat structure as reported. IPO $85.7B greenshoe = Reported (CNBC + Reuters/Investing.com); single-day prints directional. Advances the megacap-risk-on + agentic-consolidation threads.

blocks.sub: Reported sweep (2 sources each): Nvidia's $25B bond, Salesforce buys Fin for $3.6B, Threads hits 500M, SoftBank+OpenAI patching service
blocks.p: Fast scan, each cross-checked: Nvidia launched its first investment-grade bond deal since 2021, upsized to ~$25B on heavy demand (Confirmed ≥3: Bloomberg, Euronews, Reuters/NST) — raising debt instead of diluting equity as its capital commitments mount. Salesforce agreed to acquire AI customer-service platform Fin (formerly Intercom) for $3.6B, folding it into Agentforce; close expected ~early 2027 (Confirmed ≥3 incl. Salesforce's own release). Threads hit 500M monthly users and shipped a 'Your Algo' feed-control plus Communities out of beta (Meta newsroom + TechCrunch/Engadget) — a maturing channel for the social team to watch. SoftBank + OpenAI launched a 'Patching as a Service' security offering for Japan's top 3,000 critical-infrastructure firms (SoftBank release + AP). Why it matters: the through-line is AI moving from demos into balance-sheet-scale infrastructure and consolidation — the backdrop against which clients now scrutinize every AI line item.
blocks.note: Corroboration as labeled inline (Nvidia/Salesforce = Confirmed ≥3; Threads/SoftBank = Reported 2 incl. a primary newsroom). Figures cross-checked; order-book/oversubscription details on the Nvidia bond are from secondary writeups — directional. France's reported €655M AI plan is held back here: the government described the capability, not the vendor — it is NOT confirmed as Mistral-built, so we're not publishing that attribution.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Monday, June 15
tldr: Web design play of the day: delete your tooltip/dropdown/menu JavaScript — the trio that replaces it is now Baseline in every browser. Three native features finally line up across Chrome, Safari AND Firefox: the Popover API (show/hide an overlay with one HTML attribute), invoker commands (command/commandfor on a button — open/close with zero JS), and CSS anchor positioning (tether the overlay to its trigger and flip it when it would overflow, in pure CSS). That's the whole job a library like Floating UI/Popper used to do — now with no dependency, and it works for all users, not just Chrome. (Honest: the last piece reached Baseline early 2026, so this is a cross-browser status upgrade, not a 24-hour scoop.)

CRO & AOV — the team asked 'what's the ONE thing to focus on?' Here it is, grounded. For conversions: kill the surprise. Unexpected extra costs at checkout are the #1 reason ready buyers walk — ~48% of those who abandon say so (Baymard) — so show shipping/tax in the cart, not the final screen. For order value: one free-shipping threshold set ~15–25% above your current AOV, with a live 'you're $12 away' bar — shoppers add items to qualify, and orders with free delivery run ~30% larger. Do those two before anything fancier.

CRO timing twist (2026): re-peg that threshold THIS year — it's a reset year. Deloitte's 2026 retail survey found 67% of retail execs plan to raise their free-shipping threshold to defend margins against carrier and tariff cost shocks. If yours is still last year's number, it's probably too low (giving away margin) or stale vs your real AOV. Pull your AOV, re-peg, and re-check the shipping-cost margin.

AI tooling — claim your Agent SDK credit TODAY (Jun 15) or your automations can stop. As of today, Claude Agent SDK, claude -p, and Claude Code GitHub Actions no longer draw on your normal Claude plan — they move to a separate monthly credit ($20 Pro / $100 Max 5x / $200 Max 20x), metered at full API rates, and it's a one-time opt-in you must claim. Past the credit, jobs only continue if you've enabled usage credits — otherwise they halt until the credit refreshes. Claim it, then put a hard dollar cap in front of it.
Also today (Monday): Weekly Issue #4 is live — the week of Jun 8–14 in one idea (subtract the middle layer), the keepers worth adopting, and an assignable action list per discipline.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is own less, but keep one guard — let the browser run the menu instead of a JS library, let a threshold do your upselling instead of a popup, let the platform default run your agents — but keep the guardrail that stops a silent failure: a graceful fallback, a margin check, a spend cap. The subtraction is the win; the guard is what makes it safe to ship.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: build tooltips, dropdowns & menus with zero JS (Popover API + invoker commands + anchor positioning — now Baseline)

blocks.sub: The native trio that replaces Floating UI/Popper is finally Baseline across Chrome, Safari AND Firefox — so you can ship it to every user, not just Chromium
blocks.p: What's the play: the overlay UI every site needs — a tooltip, a dropdown, a context menu, a popover card — is normally shipped with a positioning library (Floating UI / Popper) plus a chunk of toggle JavaScript, and it's a recurring source of bugs (it opens off-screen, traps focus, ignores the keyboard). Three native web-platform pieces now do the whole job, and — unlike the Chromium-only CSS primitives we covered last week (the carousel, sibling-index) — all three are Baseline, meaning Chrome, Edge, Safari and Firefox all support them. (1) The Popover API: add the popover attribute to any element and the browser gives you a top-layer overlay with light-dismiss (click outside / Esc), correct stacking, and the accessibility wiring for free (Baseline since 2025). (2) Invoker commands: put commandfor="menu" command="toggle-popover" on a plain <button> and it opens/closes the popover with no JavaScript at all (Baseline once Safari 26.2 shipped, early 2026). (3) CSS anchor positioning: name the trigger as an anchor and pin the overlay to it in pure CSS — and have it automatically flip to the other side when it would overflow the screen (Baseline once Firefox 147 shipped, Jan 2026). Why it matters for our builds: this deletes a JS dependency, its hydration cost, and a whole class of a11y/positioning bugs — and because it's Baseline, you're not gambling on browser support or writing a fallback. It's the cross-browser counterpoint to last week's Chromium-only primitives: this one you can put on a client's production site today, for everyone.
blocks.doIt: (1) Markup, no JS: <button commandfor='menu' command='toggle-popover'>Menu</button> then <div id='menu' popover>…</div> — that alone gives you a working, dismissible menu. (2) Tether it in CSS: on the button set anchor-name: --menu-btn; and on the popover set position-anchor: --menu-btn; position-area: bottom span-right; (drop it below, aligned to the trigger). (3) Stop it going off-screen: add position-try-fallbacks: flip-block, flip-inline; so the browser flips it above/left when there's no room — the thing Floating UI existed to do. (4) For a tooltip, swap the button command for the new interesttarget/hover-and-focus pattern, or keep command for click-to-open. (5) Delete the old Floating-UI/Popper init and the toggle handler. (6) Sanity-check keyboard + screen-reader behaviour — the browser supplies the basics, but still tab through it.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: this is a Baseline status upgrade (the pieces landed across 2025–early 2026), surfaced today because it's the highest-value web-design play that's now safe cross-browser — not a 24-hour scoop. Anchor positioning's overflow-fallback property is still settling in syntax across engines, so test the flip behaviour. Very old browsers (pre-2025) won't support it — if you must serve those, keep a minimal fallback, but for an evergreen modern audience you can drop the library outright. Card: card-webdesign-anchor-positioning-menus (joins thread-modern-css-primitives). Logged a prediction (a major UI library documents/ships a pure-CSS anchor mode). Sources: MDN, web.dev, InfoQ.

sections.h: CRO & AOV — run it today: the one highest-leverage fix for each (answering the team's question)
sections.intro: Closing an open team gap (Ask-anything: 'the best one thing to focus on for CRO and AOV'). The honest answer: find YOUR biggest leak first — but across thousands of stores the data points to the same two levers, so start here while you measure.

blocks.sub: CRO — the #1 lever: kill the surprise cost. Unexpected extra fees at checkout are the single biggest reason ready buyers abandon (~48%, Baymard)
blocks.p: What the data says: cart abandonment averages ~70% across 50 studies (Baymard), and among shoppers who abandon for a stated reason, the #1 reason — at ~48% — is 'extra costs too high' (shipping, tax, fees) revealed too late. That makes it the highest-leverage conversion fix there is: if the first time someone sees the real total is the final screen, a big chunk of people who meant to buy bail. Showing the true cost early loses only the few who'd never have paid it — and keeps everyone else. Why it matters for our work: teams burn weeks A/B-testing button colours (no universal winner) while leaving the biggest, best-documented leak wide open. Fix the surprise first; it's the cheapest large win in the funnel.
blocks.doIt: (1) Add a shipping + tax estimate to the cart (a postcode field, or a clear 'from' price) — never a blank 'calculated at checkout'. (2) Surface any handling/surcharge the moment it applies, not at the payment step. (3) If you have a free-shipping threshold, show it in the cart with the gap ('add $12 for free shipping'). (4) Use AI to find your own leak fast: paste your checkout's field list / a screen recording into ChatGPT or Claude and ask it to audit against Baymard's checkout principles and rank the friction; then watch your analytics for the step with the biggest drop — it's usually right after a surprise cost appears. Card: card-cro-show-total-cost-early.
blocks.note: Grounded in Baymard's cart-abandonment dataset (the ~48% / ~70% figures are theirs). The 'best one thing' is genuinely store-specific — these are the odds-on favourites, not a substitute for looking at your own funnel.

blocks.sub: AOV — the #1 lever: one free-shipping threshold pegged ~15–25% above your current AOV, with a live progress bar
blocks.p: What the data says: free shipping is the incentive shoppers want most — ~81% say it makes them more likely to buy, ~51% say they add items specifically to qualify, and orders that include free delivery run roughly 30% larger (Capital One Shopping). A threshold turns that into an AOV lever via the 'goal-gradient' effect (people push harder as a reward gets closer) — but only if the bar is reachable: set it ~15–25% above your current AOV (e.g. AOV $65 → threshold ~$75–$85). Too high and it deflates; too low and you give away margin. The mechanism is well-supported; the exact number is yours to test. Why it matters: it's the cheapest extra revenue there is — from customers already buying — and it pairs directly with the CRO fix above (the same progress bar that nudges basket size also removes the shipping objection).
blocks.doIt: (1) Pull your real AOV (last 90 days). (2) Set the threshold ~15–25% above it. (3) Show a live progress bar in the cart/mini-cart: 'You're $12 away from free shipping', with one relevant low-cost add-on suggested right beside it. (4) AI step: export your order-value distribution and ask Claude/ChatGPT to find the threshold that captures the largest cluster of orders 'one nudge' away, and to draft the progress-bar + cart copy. (5) Watch AOV, conversion AND shipping-cost margin together for 3–4 weeks before locking it. Card: card-cro-free-shipping-threshold.
blocks.note: 2026 timing twist — re-peg this NOW. Deloitte's 2026 Retail Outlook (survey of 330 execs) found 67% plan to raise their free-shipping threshold this year to defend margins against carrier-cost and trade-policy shocks; the average threshold sits ~$64 and many are quietly pushing it 10–20% higher. So if your number is a year old, re-peg it to current AOV and re-run the margin math — don't leave it where it was. Treat the 15–25% / ~30% figures as well-supported starting points to validate on your store, not guarantees.

sections.h: AI tooling — run it today: claim your Agent SDK credit before today's billing split, then cap it

blocks.sub: As of Jun 15, Agent SDK / claude -p / Claude Code GitHub Actions stop drawing on your Claude plan and move to a separate, opt-in monthly credit metered at full API rates — claim it or programmatic runs can halt
blocks.p: What changed (today, Jun 15, 2026): Anthropic split Claude billing into two pools. Interactive use — the Claude Code terminal/IDE, Coworking, and chat on web/desktop/mobile — keeps drawing on your normal plan as before. But programmatic use — the Agent SDK, the claude -p headless command, third-party apps built on the Agent SDK, and the Claude Code GitHub Actions integration — no longer counts toward your plan limits. It now draws on a separate monthly Agent SDK credit ($20 on Pro, $100 on Max 5x, $200 on Max 20x), metered at standard API rates. Two gotchas: (1) it's a one-time opt-in you have to claim in your Claude account — it isn't switched on automatically; (2) once the monthly credit runs out, extra usage only continues if you've enabled usage credits (pay-as-you-go at API rates) — otherwise the requests stop until the credit refreshes. Why it matters for us: anything we run on automation — a scheduled agent, a CI job, a GitHub Action that calls Claude (this very briefing pipeline is the shape) — could silently stop on a teammate's account today if the credit isn't claimed or the budget isn't set. It's the same 'govern the AI default' discipline we keep teaching, now with a real deadline.
blocks.doIt: (1) Today: open your Claude account billing and claim the Agent SDK credit (one-time; then it refreshes each cycle). (2) Decide whether to enable usage credits so jobs don't hard-stop when the monthly credit is gone — and if you do, treat it as real API spend. (3) Put a hard dollar cap in front of any agent/CI workload so a runaway loop can't quietly bill at full API rates — route through a gateway with a spend limit (card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits) and set per-model/per-day budgets. (4) Audit which of your automations call the Agent SDK / claude -p / Claude Code Actions and confirm each runs on an account that's claimed the credit. (5) Keep your toolchain swap-ready (card-ai-tooling-model-portability) so a billing change never single-points your pipeline.
blocks.note: Grounded in Anthropic's official Help Center article ('Use the Claude Agent SDK with your Claude plan'), corroborated across The New Stack and XDA. The interactive TUI and claude.ai are explicitly unaffected — this is about programmatic/headless usage only. Card: card-ai-tooling-agent-sdk-credit. Logged a prediction (an in-product Agent SDK spend dashboard/cap follows). Sources: Claude Help Center, The New Stack, XDA.

reports.title: Weekly Briefing — Issue #4
tldr: The week in one idea: subtract the middle layer. Almost every play this week removed something you used to need — a JS library, a backend server, a desktop tool, an export-to-spreadsheet loop, a runaway AI bill. The browser, the platform default, and the user's own machine quietly absorbed the work. The operator move: take the subtraction, but keep one guard.

Design + Dev — the browser ate the JavaScript (and the backend). In one week: dividers in grid/flex (row-rule), staggered motion (sibling-index()), page transitions (View Transitions), an accessible carousel (CSS Overflow 5), and real Python in the browser with no server (Pyodide + WASM wheels on PyPI). Most are Chromium-only and degrade cleanly — ship as progressive enhancement.

AI tooling — the bill came under the microscope. Cloudflare shipped dollar spend caps for AI calls; Claude Fable 5 went GA but with a 30-day retention catch (ZDR doesn't apply); Claude Code /workflows made multi-agent orchestration a built-in; and the Agent SDK billing split lands Jun 15. Theme: govern AI spend and provenance, don't just consume it.

Marketing — answer engines are now a measurable channel. AEO got its missing half: Semrush's free AI Search Visibility Checker scores whether ChatGPT/Gemini cite you; new research pinned the formats that win citations (and FAQ schema as an under-used edge); LinkedIn is the #2 most-cited domain in AI answers (original 500–2,000-word articles win). Paid: Google's DSA→AI Max migration slipped to Feb 2027 (a window to test), Meta's Opportunity Score went universal.

News — the AI trade got graded, then bounced. A Broadcom guidance miss rotated money out of AI chips (Jun 3–8), then names snapped back (Jun 11) on reaffirmed ~$750B hyperscaler capex; SpaceX ran the largest IPO in history (Musk → first trillionaire); and the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 government suspension hardened. The durable read: capital is grading AI spend, not AI ambition — and power/compute is still the moat.

Sharpen your edge: the connective thread to last week's 'controls collapse' — this week you also got to remove cost and dependencies, not just hand work to a default. Every subtraction below ships with a guard: a graceful CSS fallback, a spend cap, a data-retention check, a sandbox account. Take the simplification; keep the guardrail.

sections.h: The week in one idea: subtract the middle layer

blocks.sub: The wins this week were removals, not additions
blocks.p: Line the week's plays up and they rhyme: a CSS rule replaced a JS carousel library and a tooltip library; the browser itself ran Python so there was no backend to host (Pyodide + WASM wheels on PyPI); an MCP connector replaced the export-to-spreadsheet loop in your ESP; a single threshold replaced a manual upsell; a spend cap replaced the fear of a runaway bill. The middle layer — the dependency, the server, the manual workflow, the agency step — kept getting deleted because the browser, the platform default, or the user's own device absorbed it.
blocks.note: Synthesis, not recap: five-plus dailies this week told one story from different crafts — the cheapest, most durable win right now is a subtraction.

blocks.sub: But every subtraction shipped with a guard
blocks.p: The discipline is the twin of the simplification. Push Python to the browser — but lazy-load it so you don't tax the page. Drop the tooltip library — but only because the native trio is now Baseline (test the keyboard). Let the platform default run your ads/agents — but keep a manual control, a spend cap, and a data-provenance check. The same week the market punished loose AI spend and Anthropic split Agent SDK billing, the move that wins is the one we keep repeating: take the default, keep one guardrail that catches a silent failure.
blocks.doIt: This week's homework, one line each: ship one Baseline native overlay and delete its JS (design); lazy-load one in-browser tool instead of a backend (dev); claim + cap your Agent SDK credit before Jun 15 (AI); run the free AI-visibility check and fix the top gap page (growth); re-peg one free-shipping threshold to current AOV (cro/marketing).

sections.h: What changed, and what to do — by domain
sections.intro: One tight synthesis per domain that moved this week. The throughline (subtract + guard) shows up differently in each.

blocks.sub: Web design — the browser replaced the JS library, five times
blocks.p: A remarkable run of native primitives landed or matured: Gap Decorations (column-rule/row-rule in grid & flex, Chrome 149), sibling-index()/sibling-count() for staggered motion and math layouts, the View Transitions API for app-like page changes, and CSS Overflow 5 carousel primitives — plus the Popover + invoker-commands + anchor-positioning trio that is now Baseline cross-browser. Each deletes a library or a hack.
blocks.doIt: Adopt the gracefully-degrading ones now as progressive enhancement (most are Chromium-only and fall back cleanly). Lead with the Baseline trio for production overlays (tooltips/menus) since it's safe for all users; keep the Chromium-only ones (carousel, sibling-index) decorative until a 2nd engine ships.
blocks.note: Keeper principle reaffirmed: a gracefully-degrading CSS primitive can ship the day it hits one engine; a Baseline one can replace a JS dependency outright.

blocks.sub: Web dev — own less server: push compute to the edge and the browser
blocks.p: The dev plays all moved work off your infrastructure: Pyodide 314 + WASM wheels on PyPI run real Python (and its libraries) in a browser tab with no backend and no per-call API bill; Copilot CLI + LSP gave the terminal agent real code intelligence so it stops guessing; Vercel's Workflow SDK runs durably on Nitro v3. The security flip side: 'opening' a poisoned repo inside an AI IDE is now code execution — patch kernels, rotate tokens.
blocks.doIt: Reach for an in-browser tool (Pyodide) before standing up a backend for client-side calculators/explorers — lazy-load it on click. Wire LSP into your terminal agent. And treat your AI coding toolchain as attack surface: rotate any tokens exposed to untrusted repos.
blocks.note: Two independent dev cards this week (browser-Python, Copilot LSP) share one root: remove the layer that was only there to compensate for a missing capability.

blocks.sub: AI tooling — govern the spend and the provenance
blocks.p: The AI plays were about control, not capability: Cloudflare AI Gateway spend limits (a hard dollar cap), Claude Code /workflows (planner→worker→verifier as a built-in primitive), and the Fable 5 GA with a 30-day retention catch (existing ZDR agreements don't apply; Microsoft restricted it internally). The Agent SDK billing split (Jun 15) makes cost governance a dated, required action.
blocks.doIt: Stand up the two-gate habit: a spend cap in front of every AI feature, and a data-provenance check before routing client-confidential data to any new model/default. Claim + cap the Agent SDK credit today. Keep a model router so a billing or retention change never single-points your pipeline.
blocks.note: This is last week's 'controls collapse → govern the system' continuing — but the week's sharpest edge was specifically about money and data terms, the two things that age your stack overnight.

blocks.sub: Growth — answer engines became a measurable channel
blocks.p: AEO stopped being vibes: Semrush's free AI Search Visibility Checker (no login) scores whether ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity cite you and lists the prompts where you're invisible; HubSpot/Wix research pinned which formats win citations; FAQ/structured-data schema emerged as an under-used legibility edge (~2.8× citation rate for well-structured pages). This resolved our open call that AEO would go mainstream-measurable (✅).
blocks.doIt: Run the free check on a client domain, take the top 'invisible' prompt, and apply the formatting pass (intent title → one-sentence answer → list/table → original stat → FAQ schema + last-updated). Re-measure in 2–4 weeks.
blocks.note: Measure → fix → re-measure is now a real loop, not a leap of faith — the half that was missing in earlier AEO advice.

blocks.sub: Social + Paid — design your inputs for the signal the AI now ranks on
blocks.p: Social: LinkedIn is the #2 most-cited domain in AI answers (original 500–2,000-word articles, posted consistently, beat viral feed posts), and Instagram's 'Your Algorithm' topic controls reached the main feed — so a clean, consistent topic signal is the new reach lever. Paid: Meta's Opportunity Score went universal (a 0–100 setup-health grade) and the Advantage+ learning bar halved to ~25 conv/week; Google delayed the DSA→AI Max forced migration to Feb 2027 — a window to test AI Max as a guarded experiment, not get auto-migrated blind.
blocks.doIt: Social: publish one 500–2,000-word LinkedIn article answering a buyer's AI question; name one clear topic per Instagram post. Paid: triage accounts by Opportunity Score (fix anything under ~60), and run AI Max 50/50 against your live DSA in Experiments with brand exclusions pre-set before Feb 2027.
blocks.note: Same move as the AEO story: the platform automates the optimization; your edge is setting the inputs and the guardrails so its automation lands on your terms.

blocks.sub: Graphic — make AI output editable and on-brand by declaring it as data
blocks.p: Two plays killed the worst failure modes of AI design assets: Ideogram 4.0 takes a structured JSON brief (typed text layers, per-element bounding boxes, hex palette) so in-image text comes out spelled right and laid out where you put it; Recraft V4 Vector outputs true editable SVG (not a flat PNG) you drop into Figma and recolour to brand tokens.
blocks.doIt: For any AI logo/icon/layout headed for client work, prompt as a brief (declare copy, position, brand hexes) and demand a vector/editable output — then do a designer's pass for path cleanliness and licensing.
blocks.note: The graphic version of the week's theme: stop fighting the tool's defaults — hand it structured intent and get back something you can actually edit.

blocks.sub: Email — query your live program in plain English, and own the inbox
blocks.p: Omnisend shipped a hosted MCP server — connect it to Claude and audit campaigns/deliverability against your real account without exports; and the durable DMARC + BIMI setup (one afternoon of DNS) improves inbox placement and puts your verified logo beside every send.
blocks.doIt: Connect the Omnisend MCP and ask for a 30-day revenue + deliverability audit with one fix each; separately, publish DMARC (move toward p=reject) and a BIMI record with a VMC.
blocks.note: MCP is the 'subtract the middle layer' move for marketers — the spreadsheet export loop disappears.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, June 14
tldr: Web dev play of the day: run real Python in the browser with zero backend — and now install almost any package straight from PyPI. Pyodide 314.0 (Jun 13) lets package authors publish WebAssembly wheels to PyPI, so micropip.install('pandas') pulls real libraries into a browser tab at runtime — no server, no API bill, and the user's data never leaves their machine. Perfect for a client-side calculator, CSV explorer, or PDF/image tool. One CDN script tag and you're running.

Graphic play: stop generating logos and icons you can't edit — get native, editable SVG from AI. Every other AI image tool hands you a flat PNG; Recraft V4's Vector models output true SVG with clean, editable paths you drop straight into Figma or Illustrator, recolour to your brand, and scale from favicon to billboard. The fix for the #1 problem with AI brand assets: they usually arrive as uneditable pixels.

Growth play: add FAQ schema to your top money pages so AI answer engines can quote you — structured pages get ~2.8× the AI-citation rate. Schema markup is invisible JSON-LD code that spells out, in machine-readable form, exactly what your page says. FAQ schema sits on only ~10.5% of AI-cited pages, so it's an open lane: paste a FAQPage block into a high-intent page, validate it, and you've made yourself legible to ChatGPT/Perplexity/Google AI.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is own less server, own more of the edge — push the computation to the user's browser (Pyodide), ship assets that stay editable instead of baked (SVG), and make your pages machine-legible so the AI does the distribution (schema). The cheapest, most durable wins right now are the ones that need no backend and no re-work later.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: real Python in the browser, no backend (Pyodide 314.0 + WASM wheels on PyPI)

blocks.sub: Pyodide 314.0 (Jun 13, 2026) lets any package ship a WebAssembly wheel to PyPI — so micropip pulls real Python libraries into a browser tab at runtime, no server to host or pay for
blocks.p: What changed: Pyodide is CPython compiled to WebAssembly (Wasm) — the standard that lets non-JavaScript code run at near-native speed inside any modern browser tab — so Python runs client-side with no server. The 314.0 release (Jun 13, 2026, which tracks Python 3.14) closes the ecosystem's biggest gap: combined with a PyPI change (PEP 783, landed Apr 21), package authors can now publish Wasm 'wheels' (pre-built installable packages) straight to PyPI under the pyemscripten_2026_0 tag — 28 packages already do — so micropip.install('somepkg') fetches them at runtime. Before this, Pyodide's maintainers had to hand-build and host ~300 packages themselves, a bottleneck; now any C/Rust-extension library can ship its own browser build like it ships Linux/Mac/Windows ones. Why it matters for our builds: you can ship an interactive tool — a pricing/ROI calculator, a CSV or data explorer, a client-side image/PDF processor — that runs entirely in the browser. No backend to stand up, host, or pay for; no per-call API cost; and the user's data never leaves their machine, which is a genuine selling point for client tools that touch sensitive inputs (financials, uploads, PII). It's the 'free/no-build by default' move: reach for Python's mature libraries instead of re-implementing them in JavaScript.
blocks.doIt: (1) Drop one CDN script tag in your page: <script src='https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/pyodide/v314.0.0/full/pyodide.js'></script>. (2) Boot it once: const pyodide = await loadPyodide();. (3) Install a package at runtime: await pyodide.loadPackage('micropip'); const micropip = pyodide.pyimport('micropip'); await micropip.install('snowballstemmer'); (swap in any PyPI package that ships a pure-Python or Wasm wheel). (4) Run Python and read the result back into JS: const out = await pyodide.runPythonAsync("import statistics; statistics.mean([2,4,6])");. (5) Lazy-load it — don't boot Pyodide on page load; trigger loadPyodide() on the click that opens the tool, so the runtime download only hits users who actually use it. (6) Verify your exact package installs in the Pyodide REPL at pyodide.org before you wire up the UI.
blocks.note: Honest caveat: the first load downloads the Python runtime (several MB) plus any packages — fine for an interactive tool the user opted into, wrong for your marketing homepage's critical path; lazy-load on click and show a 'loading…' state. The runtime works across modern browsers, not Chromium-only. The PyPI-Wasm-wheel ecosystem is young (28 packages on Jun 13) — the long-bundled scientific stack (numpy, pandas) already works via micropip; check your specific extension package first. Card: card-webdev-pyodide-browser-python. Logged a prediction (Wasm-wheel package count grows). Sources: Pyodide release blog, Pyodide docs, Simon Willison.

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: generate editable SVG logos & icons with AI (Recraft V4 Vector → Figma)

blocks.sub: A durable play resurfaced for a quiet graphic day: Recraft V4's Vector models output true, editable SVG (not a flat PNG) — the only major AI image model doing native SVG rather than raster-to-vector tracing
blocks.p: The play: the #1 problem with AI-generated logos and icons is that they arrive as pixels — a flat PNG you can't recolour cleanly, can't resize without blur, and can't hand to a developer. Recraft V4's Vector models fix that: they generate native SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics — math-defined shapes that stay razor-sharp at any size and stay editable), with clean Bézier paths you open directly in Figma, Illustrator or Sketch and edit path-by-path. Recraft is currently the only major AI image tool producing production-ready SVG natively, instead of auto-tracing a raster image into a messy approximation. Why it matters for our craft: this is the difference between a one-off picture and a real brand asset — an icon set that scales from a 16px favicon to a billboard, recolours to the client's exact brand tokens, and drops into the design system without a redraw. It pairs with the structured-prompt and palette plays we already use (give it your hex palette, prompt as a brief).
blocks.doIt: (1) Open recraft.ai and pick a V4 Vector model (Vector / Vector Pro). (2) Prompt like a brief, not a sentence: minimalist single-line icon of a paper plane, flat, one accent colour #2563EB on transparent, consistent 2px stroke. (3) Generate, then download as SVG. (4) Drag the SVG into Figma — it lands as editable vector layers; recolour to your brand tokens and tweak stroke/shape. (5) Build a consistent set by reusing the exact same style clause across prompts (same stroke weight, fill rule, corner style) so 12 icons read as one family. (6) Keep a human in the loop — pick the 2–3 that fit, fix kinks in the paths, and confirm the result is on-brand before shipping.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: confirm commercial-use rights on your Recraft plan before using output in client work; AI vector output still needs a designer's pass for true brand fit and path cleanliness; for typographic/text-in-image pieces, Ideogram's JSON layout play is the better tool (card-graphic-ideogram-json-layout). This is a durable technique surfaced because the graphic beat was quiet today — not breaking news. Card: card-graphic-recraft-svg-vector. Logged a prediction (a 2nd major model ships native vector). Sources: Recraft, abduzeedo, MindStudio.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: add FAQ schema so AI answer engines can quote your pages

blocks.sub: Structured pages (clean headings + schema markup) earn ~2.8× the AI-citation rate of poorly-structured ones — and FAQ schema sits on only ~10.5% of AI-cited pages, so it's an open lane
blocks.p: What changed: as buyers shift to asking AI assistants instead of scrolling search results, the question is whether ChatGPT/Perplexity/Google AI can cleanly lift an answer from your page. Schema markup — invisible JSON-LD code (a small structured block in your page's HTML) that states, in machine-readable form, exactly what the page is and what each Q&A says — is how you remove the ambiguity. 2026 research (AirOps' State of AI Search) found pages with clean structure plus schema earn ~2.8× higher AI-citation rates than poorly-structured ones, and pages using three or more schema types are ~13% more likely to be cited. Crucially, FAQ schema appears on only ~10.5% of AI-cited pages — under-used, so it's a low-effort edge. Why it matters for our craft: this is the third leg of the answer-engine-optimization play we've been building — we covered the content-format pass (card-growth-aeo-content-formats) and the free visibility check (card-growth-ai-visibility-check); this is the machine-legibility leg. FAQPage and HowTo schema map directly to how an LLM extracts and attributes an answer.
blocks.doIt: (1) Pick one high-intent money page that already answers real buyer questions (pricing, comparison, 'how does X work'). (2) Generate a FAQPage JSON-LD block — use Google's Structured Data Markup Helper, or on WordPress the Rank Math / All in One SEO FAQ block (no code). (3) Paste the JSON-LD into the page <head> (JSON-LD is Google's recommended format because it sits apart from your layout and won't break it). (4) Add Organization + Author schema too for trust/E-E-A-T signals. (5) Validate with Google's Rich Results Test and the Schema.org Validator — fix any errors before shipping. (6) Re-run your AI-visibility check (card-growth-ai-visibility-check) in ~2–4 weeks to confirm new mentions, and templatise the winning markup across similar pages.
blocks.note: Honest caveats: schema makes you legible and eligible, not guaranteed — the page still needs a genuinely good, attributable first-party answer (the content-format pass). Note Google scaled back FAQ rich results in classic blue-link Search back in 2023, so the payoff here is AEO / AI-answer legibility, not a guaranteed search-snippet; don't promise clients a rich snippet. Card: card-growth-schema-aeo (advances the answer-engine-optimization thread). Logged a prediction. Sources: AirOps, HubSpot, Google Search Central.
