reports.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 13
tldr: Web design play of the day: ship an accessible image carousel in pure CSS — no JS library, no a11y debt. Chrome/Edge 135+ ship the CSS Overflow 5 carousel primitives: a plain scroll-snap list plus ::scroll-button(left/right) (prev/next), ::scroll-marker + scroll-marker-group (the dot nav) and :target-current (active dot). The browser supplies the ARIA roles, keyboard nav and focus order for free, and it degrades to a usable scroll-snap strip in Firefox/Safari — so delete the carousel JS today. Chromium-only for now (not yet Baseline).

Paid — Google just bought you a runway: the DSA → AI Max forced migration slipped from Sept 2026 to Feb 2027, and DSA creation returns Jun 15. Don't get auto-migrated blind. Use the window to run AI Max for Search 50/50 against your live DSA in Experiments for 2–4 weeks, with brand exclusions + negatives pre-set, and judge it on CPA/ROAS and wasted-query rate (not clicks). Migrate on proof, not on a deadline.

Social — Instagram put a steering wheel on the feed: 'Your Algorithm' now reaches the main feed. An LLM turns each user's behavior into plain-language topics they can add or remove (live Jun 12). Reach now hinges on the model confidently filing your post under a topic people opt into — so name one topic per post in the first caption line, the on-screen text, and the first 3 seconds, and hold a consistent niche. Off-topic posts don't just get ignored, they get 'show less'-ed.

News — the largest IPO in history landed: SpaceX went public Jun 12, raising ~$75B and closing at a ~$2.2T market cap, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. Shares priced at $135, opened $150 and closed $161.11 (+19.34%); SpaceX is now the 6th-largest US public company. Risk-on for the AI/space megacap trade — but treat single-day prints as directional and watch whether the valuation holds past the debut pop.

News — the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 shutdown hardened. As of Jun 13 both models remain disabled for all customers under the Jun 12 US export-control order; Anthropic called the directive disproportionate and warned it would halt all frontier-model deployments if applied industry-wide — the recall came just three days after Fable 5 launched. The continuity lesson stands: never hard-wire one model (card-ai-tooling-model-portability).

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is own the layer the platform is automating — let the browser render the carousel and the screen-reader semantics, let Google's AI run the search match (but prove it in an experiment first), let Instagram's model classify your post (but hand it one unambiguous topic). The platforms are absorbing the manual work; your edge is setting the inputs and the guardrails so their automation lands on your terms.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: an accessible carousel in pure CSS (CSS Overflow 5 — scroll buttons + markers)

blocks.sub: Chrome/Edge 135+ ship ::scroll-button(), ::scroll-marker / scroll-marker-group and :target-current — a full carousel with no JS, and the browser hands you the accessibility for free
blocks.p: What's the play: the carousel is the component teams most reliably ship with a heavy JavaScript library and broken accessibility. The CSS Overflow Level 5 primitives — stable in Chrome/Edge 135+ — let you build the whole thing in CSS on top of a plain scroll-snap container: ::scroll-button(left)/::scroll-button(right) are real browser-generated prev/next buttons that scroll ~85% of the track and auto-disable at the ends; ::scroll-marker with scroll-marker-group generates the dot navigation; and :target-current styles the active dot. Why it matters for our builds: doing it natively deletes the dependency, the hydration cost and the a11y bugs in one move — the engine reports the control as a tablist, wires up arrow-key navigation and correct focus order, and exposes accessible labels you set inline. It joins the same gracefully-degrading modern-CSS-primitives story as squircles, gap decorations and sibling-index(): an engine without support just renders a scrollable scroll-snap strip, so there's no fallback code to write.
blocks.doIt: (1) Scroll-snap container: .carousel{ overflow-x:auto; scroll-snap-type:x mandatory; } .carousel li{ scroll-snap-align:center; }. (2) Dot nav: add scroll-marker-group:after; to .carousel, then .carousel li::scroll-marker{ content:''; } and .carousel li::scroll-marker:target-current{ background:var(--accent); } for the active dot. (3) Prev/next: .carousel::scroll-button(left){ content:'⬅' / 'Scroll left'; } and ::scroll-button(right){ content:'⮕' / 'Scroll right'; } — the string after / is the accessible label. (4) Remove the old JS carousel init entirely. (5) Optionally gate enhancements with @supports selector(::scroll-marker){ … } if you want an explicit baseline, though it's not required.
blocks.note: Honest caveat: Chromium-only right now (Chrome/Edge 135+) — Firefox and Safari have not shipped the carousel pseudo-elements, and it's not yet Baseline. Because it degrades to a plain scrollable carousel, use it as progressive enhancement today; don't make the buttons/dots load-bearing for non-Chromium users. Card: card-webdesign-css-carousel (thread-modern-css-primitives). Logged a Baseline prediction (2nd engine ships).

sections.h: Paid — run it today: prove AI Max on your own account before Google's forced migration (google-ads)

blocks.sub: Google delayed the DSA → AI Max auto-migration from Sept 2026 to Feb 2027 and restores DSA creation Jun 15 — a window to test AI Max as a controlled experiment, not get auto-migrated blind
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 2026): Google Ads Liaison Ginny Marvin confirmed the automatic transition of Dynamic Search Ads to AI Max for Search is pushed from September 2026 to February 2027, in response to advertiser feedback and to avoid forcing account changes during Q4. The ability to create new DSAs is restored on Jun 15, 2026; new-DSA creation then ends Jan 2027, with automatic migration of anything left beginning Feb 2027. Why it matters for us: AI Max is replacing DSA regardless — but the auto-migration is a black box, and AI Max's broader matching can spend on brand and irrelevant queries if it's not fenced. The reprieve is a chance to apply the same operator move we use on Meta Advantage+: a guarded, side-by-side test that proves the AI's lift on your account so you migrate on evidence with guardrails already dialed in, not on a deadline.
blocks.doIt: (1) Confirm your DSA campaigns still run; from Jun 15 you can create/edit DSAs during the test window. (2) In Google Ads → Experiments, run AI Max for Search against your existing DSA at a 50/50 budget split. (3) Pre-set the guardrails AI Max needs: tight brand exclusions, negative-keyword lists, and URL/location/text controls so it can't burn budget on brand or off-topic queries. (4) Run 2–4 weeks, then judge on CPA/ROAS and the wasted-query rate from the search-terms report — not the clicks/impressions AI Max will inflate. (5) If it wins, use the voluntary migration tool now; if it loses, keep DSA and re-test before Jan 2027. (6) Audit any SOP/onboarding doc that says 'build a DSA' so juniors don't ship the wrong campaign type as the menus shift.
blocks.note: The dates are Google-stated and have already moved once (Sept 2026 → Feb 2027), so treat the timeline as directional and re-confirm in-product. Don't read the delay as a reprieve from AI Max itself — it's a reprieve to prepare. Card: card-paid-aimax-dsa-experiment (advances the AI-Max/controls-collapse thread). Logged a prediction (timeline holds at Feb 2027). Sources: Search Engine Land, Search Engine Journal, PPC Land.

sections.h: Social — run it today: hand Instagram one clear topic so it files (and surfaces) your posts (instagram)

blocks.sub: 'Your Algorithm' reached the main feed (Jun 12) — an LLM turns behavior into plain-language topics users add or remove, so confident topical signaling is the new reach lever
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 12, 2026): Instagram expanded its Your Algorithm controls — first on Reels (Oct 2025), then Explore — to the main feed. A large language model translates each user's engagement into plain-language topics they can see, add ('show me more of this') or remove ('less of this'); head Adam Mosseri framed it as giving back agency the recommendation system had taken. Why it matters for our craft: distribution now runs partly on explicit, user-chosen topic preferences, not just implicit signals. When a viewer can add 'more of [topic]', a post the model has confidently filed under that topic rides along — and an off-niche or muddy post can be actively 'show less'-ed out of exactly the audience that would convert. The winning move mirrors what we taught for Instagram's sends signal and LinkedIn GEO: design your inputs for the signal the model now ranks on, here a clean, consistent topic association.
blocks.doIt: (1) On your own account, open Your Algorithm (Settings → recommendation/topic controls) and read the topics Instagram inferred — that's roughly how it classifies content like yours. (2) Choose one topic per post and state it explicitly: in the first line of the caption, in on-screen text, and shown/spoken in the first 3 seconds of a Reel, so the model gets unambiguous signals to embed. (3) Hold a consistent niche across posts to build a strong topic association users can opt into; push off-topic personal posts to Stories. (4) Add a 'send this to…'/save-worthy payoff so you also earn the sends + saves that still drive distribution (pair with card-social-instagram-sends). (5) After a week, recheck Your Algorithm — are your inferred topics sharpening toward your niche? Scattered topics mean your signaling is too mixed.
blocks.note: Caveat: the topic controls are a viewer-side personalization surface, not a publisher dashboard — you're inferring how the model reads you from your own account, not a guaranteed reach setting. The durable principle (clear, consistent topical signaling) holds regardless of UI specifics. Card: card-social-instagram-your-algorithm (advances the Instagram-ranking thread). Logged a prediction. Sources: Search Engine Land, Social Media Today, Engadget.

sections.h: News — the landscape: the biggest IPO ever, and the Fable 5 shutdown hardens
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: SpaceX's record IPO makes Musk the first trillionaire — risk-on for the AI/space megacap trade, but a debut pop is not a valuation
blocks.p: What happened: SpaceX went public on Jun 12, 2026 in the largest IPO in history — shares priced at $135, opened at $150, and closed at $161.11 (up ~19.3% on the day), giving the company a market cap around $2.2 trillion and making it the 6th-largest US public company on day one. The raise was roughly $75B, and the debut pushed Elon Musk's net worth past $1 trillion — the world's first trillionaire. Why it matters to us: it's a loud risk-on signal for the AI/space megacap complex the same week the AI-chip names were repricing — capital is still chasing the biggest frontier-infrastructure bets. For client work, it reinforces the climate read, not a trade: enthusiasm for marquee AI/space names is intact, but scrutiny of economics is the throughline, so keep leading AI pitches with cost-per-outcome.
blocks.note: Confirmed (≥3 independent): CNBC, Bloomberg, CNN and Al Jazeera/TechCrunch corroborate the largest-IPO framing, the trillionaire milestone and the close. Exact intraday prints ($150 open / $161.11 close / +19.3%) are dated snapshots — directional; the durable facts are 'largest IPO ever + first trillionaire + ~$2.2T debut cap'. A debut pop isn't a settled valuation — watch whether it holds.

blocks.sub: Fable 5 / Mythos 5 stay dark — Anthropic calls the order disproportionate and warns it would freeze all frontier deployments industry-wide
blocks.p: Where it stands (Jun 13): both Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 remain disabled for all customers under the Jun 12 US government export-control directive (suspending foreign-national access, impractical to enforce per-user, so Anthropic switched both off to comply); all other Claude models are unaffected. New detail advancing the thread: Anthropic publicly called the directive disproportionate and warned that, if the same logic were applied across the industry, it would halt all frontier-model deployments — and stressed the recall came just three days after Fable 5 launched. Anthropic maintains the order likely rests on a 'misunderstanding' over a narrow jailbreak finding (a capability it says is widely available, incl. GPT-5.5) and says it's working to restore access. Why it matters to us: the availability-risk thread is now concrete precedent — a frontier model you build on can be switched off overnight by a third party. The craft payoff is unchanged: route AI calls through a gateway with a fallback model list (card-ai-tooling-model-portability).
blocks.note: Confirmed (≥3): Anthropic's own statement plus Bloomberg, The Verge, Ars Technica and The Next Web. The jailbreak rationale and 'disproportionate' characterisation are Anthropic's framing of the government's reasoning, not a public official finding — treat the 'why' as reported and the situation as fast-moving (open restoration prediction, due ~Jul 11). Also logged from the harvest: Jeff Bezos's Prometheus raised ~$12B for an 'artificial general engineer' (Reported, 2 sources) — physical-AI capital keeps flowing.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Friday, June 12
tldr: A short catch-up edition for a day we missed. No fabricated news — three durable, genuinely-true plays worth running whenever you ship them, each with a real working link.

Web design — give a plain multi-page site app-like page transitions in ~5 lines of CSS. Chrome's View Transitions API cross-fades (or morphs a shared element) between pages with no framework, and quietly does nothing where unsupported — safe to add today.

Web dev — put a hard dollar cap on any AI feature before you ship it. Route your AI calls through a gateway and set a spend limit; cross the budget and it blocks or fails over to a cheaper model, so a runaway loop can't quietly burn four figures.

Email — land in the inbox and show your logo next to every send. A one-time DNS setup (DMARC enforcement + BIMI) improves inbox placement and puts your verified logo beside your emails in Gmail and Apple Mail.
Sharpen your edge: a quiet day isn't an empty one — the durable plays are always worth a second pass. Pick the one that touches today's work and ship it.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: app-like page transitions in ~5 lines of CSS

blocks.sub: Chrome's View Transitions API animates page-to-page navigation with no framework
blocks.p: A 'page transition' is the smooth animation when one screen slides or morphs into the next — the polish native apps have and most websites don't. Chrome's View Transitions API gives a plain multi-page site that effect in about five lines of CSS. Where it isn't supported, navigation just happens normally, so there's zero downside to adding it now.
blocks.doIt: Turn it on in CSS with @view-transition { navigation: auto; } (that alone cross-fades pages). To morph one element across the change, give it the same view-transition-name on both pages. Wrap any bigger motion in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce), then ship.
blocks.note: Durable play resurfaced for the catch-up — see the card card-web-view-transitions. Support varies by browser; it degrades cleanly to a normal navigation.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: a hard dollar cap on any AI feature

blocks.sub: Route LLM calls through an AI gateway and set a spend limit so a runaway loop can't burn four figures
blocks.p: The #1 reason teams won't ship AI features is fear of a surprise bill. An AI gateway is a middleman for your AI calls; Cloudflare's now enforces spend limits — real dollar budgets worked out from each model's token price. Cross the budget and it either blocks further calls or fails over to a cheaper model.
blocks.doIt: Point your OpenAI/Anthropic calls at an AI Gateway endpoint, add a spend limit scoped by model/team (e.g. $50/day on one pricey model), pick the window, and choose the over-limit behaviour (block, or fail over to a cheaper model). Reconcile the gateway's cost figures against your provider invoice for the first month.
blocks.note: Durable play resurfaced for the catch-up — see the card card-webdev-ai-gateway-spend-limits.

sections.h: Email — run it today: land in the inbox and show your logo

blocks.sub: A one-time DNS setup (DMARC + BIMI) that improves placement and shows your verified logo in Gmail and Apple Mail
blocks.p: These are email-authentication records: SPF and DKIM prove a message is really from you, DMARC tells inboxes what to do if it isn't, and BIMI (plus a VMC certificate) unlocks your logo beside the email. Two wins from one afternoon — better inbox placement after the 2024 sender rules, and free brand real estate most competitors haven't claimed.
blocks.doIt: Confirm SPF/DKIM pass, publish a DMARC record at p=none then tighten to p=quarantine and p=reject, then publish a BIMI record with a square SVG logo + a VMC certificate. Send yourself a test to confirm the logo shows and you land in the inbox.
blocks.note: Durable play resurfaced for the catch-up — see the card card-email-dmarc-bimi.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, June 11
tldr: Design play of the day: stagger a list animation — or build a math-driven layout — with zero JavaScript and zero inline styles. CSS sibling-index() and sibling-count() let an element read its own position and the total count, so a staggered reveal is one line: transition-delay: calc((sibling-index() - 1) * 60ms). Add items, the timing recalculates itself — no per-item classes, no JS loop. Chromium-only for now (Chrome/Edge 137+), and it degrades to 'no stagger', so it's safe progressive enhancement today.

AI tooling — stop pasting one mega-prompt: orchestrate a planner→worker→verifier team in Claude Code. Claude Code's dynamic /workflows (research preview, late May) lets you describe a big job and include the word workflow — Claude writes and runs an orchestration script that fans the task across subagents (great for codebase audits, migrations, multi-file refactors). June added nested subagents (up to 5 levels). It's the reliable multi-agent shape we keep teaching, now a built-in primitive.

Growth — find out today whether ChatGPT and Gemini actually mention your brand (free, no login). AEO has a measurement layer now: paste your domain into Semrush's free AI Search Visibility Checker and get a 0–100 score, which AI engines mention you, and the high-volume prompts where you're invisible — a ready-made fix list. (Ahrefs Brand Radar / Semrush AI Toolkit go deeper.) This resolves our open call that AEO would go mainstream-measurable.

News — the AI-chip rout found a floor, fast. After the Jun 3–6 sell-off (SOXX ~−10%, Nvidia briefly under $5T), AI-chip names rebounded early the week of Jun 8 (Intel/Micron up sharply intraday) as hyperscalers reaffirmed ~$750B of 2026 capex. Our 'dip, not regime change' call (due Jul 20) is now leaning held — directional, the durable read is the V-shape + the why.

News — the agent economy crossed into your brokerage. Coinbase launched Coinbase for Agents (Jun 11): Claude/ChatGPT can execute crypto trades and pay for premium research via an MCP server, using the open x402 protocol (built with AWS, Anthropic, Circle, Near). Crucially it ships with the safety pattern we preach — point the agent at a sandbox account, not your main one. And Anthropic locked $35B in TPU/data-center financing (Google backstop, Broadcom residual guarantees) — compute is still the moat.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is let the system compute the structure, but you set the bounds — CSS that counts its own siblings, an AI that writes its own multi-agent plan, a tool that tells you where you're invisible. The same week markets graded loose AI spend and an agent got a trading account, the discipline that wins is the one we keep repeating: scope it (a sandbox, a spend cap, a control), then let it run.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: stagger animations & math layouts with sibling-index() / sibling-count()

blocks.sub: CSS sibling-index() and sibling-count() let an element know its position and the total — staggered reveals and per-item math, no JS, no inline styles
blocks.p: What changed: sibling-index() (returns an element's 1-based position among its siblings) and sibling-count() (the total number of siblings) are now usable in stable Chromium (Chrome/Edge 137+). They're real CSS integer functions you can drop into any calc(), so an element can finally style itself based on where it sits in a list — without a framework loop writing style='--i:3' onto every node, and without a stack of :nth-child() rules. Why it matters for our builds: the staggered list reveal (menu items, cards, nav links cascading in) is one of the most-requested motion details on client sites, and today it's faked either with hand-written :nth-child(1..n) delays (brittle — breaks the moment the count changes) or a JS loop that injects a custom property per item (extra markup, hydration cost). These two functions delete both hacks: the timing is computed from the live DOM, so you add or remove items and the cascade just re-derives itself. It also unlocks genuinely new tricks — center-out animations, radial/arc menus, opacity or scale ramps down a list — all in pure CSS.
blocks.doIt: Staggered reveal in one line — on the children, not the container: li { transition: opacity .3s, translate .3s; transition-delay: calc((sibling-index() - 1) * 60ms); } @starting-style { li { opacity:0; translate:0 8px; } } — item 1 starts at 0ms, item 2 at 60ms, and so on, recalculating automatically as items change. Want a center-out cascade? Delay by distance from the middle: calc(abs(sibling-index() - (sibling-count() + 1) / 2) * 60ms). Per-item ramps work too: opacity: calc(1 - (sibling-index() - 1) * 0.1) for a fading list. Because unsupported browsers just ignore the functions (everything animates together, or shows full opacity), it's pure progressive enhancement — but if you want a clean baseline, guard the enhancement with @supports (top: sibling-index()) { ... }.
blocks.note: Honest caveat: Chromium-only right now (Chrome/Edge 137+) — not yet Baseline; Firefox is in active development, Safari/WebKit has it on the roadmap but unshipped. Use it strictly as decorative motion/enhancement, never as load-bearing layout, until a 2nd engine ships (logged as a prediction). Card: card-webdesign-sibling-index — joins the modern-CSS-primitives thread.

sections.h: AI tooling — run it today: orchestrate a planner→worker→verifier team with Claude Code /workflows

blocks.sub: Claude Code's dynamic /workflows turns 'describe the job + the word workflow' into a multi-agent run — and June added nested subagents
blocks.p: What changed: Claude Code shipped dynamic workflows (research preview, late May 2026) — you describe a large task and include the word workflow, and Claude writes and runs an orchestration script that fans the work across multiple subagents, with the /workflows command managing the runs and showing live progress. A June update added nested subagents (a subagent can spawn its own, up to 5 levels deep). Why it matters for us: the single biggest failure mode of agentic work isn't the model's IQ — it's stuffing one session with a sprawling task until its context turns to mush and it starts editing the wrong files. The durable fix we've taught is the planner → worker → verifier → merge shape: one agent plans, parallel workers execute scoped pieces, a verifier checks, then results merge. /workflows makes that shape a built-in primitive instead of something you hand-wire — ideal for the jobs that actually eat our time: a full-repo audit, a framework migration, a 'rename this pattern across 40 files', or 'review this PR from three independent angles and only surface what two of them agree on.'
blocks.doIt: (1) In Claude Code, take a big, decomposable job and phrase it as a workflow: Audit this codebase for dead exports and unused deps — use a workflow: one agent maps the module graph, parallel agents check each package, a verifier confirms each finding is really unused before reporting. (2) Watch it with /workflows — it composes the orchestration script and runs the subagents with visible progress. (3) Bias the design toward fan-out + verify: independent workers for breadth, a skeptic agent that tries to refute each finding before it's accepted (kills plausible-but-wrong results). (4) Keep external actions idempotent and checkpoint each step so a retried agent doesn't double-act. (5) Save the recipe: once a workflow earns its keep, capture it as a reusable AI Skill (markdown runbook) so the team triggers it by name.
blocks.note: Research preview — expect rough edges and usage that meters separately from interactive sessions (Agent SDK / Actions metering split lands Jun 15). The principle is portable even if you're not on Claude Code: planner→worker→verifier→merge, never one session doing everything. Card: card-ai-tooling-claude-workflows — related to the reusable-skills play.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: measure whether AI engines actually cite your brand (free)

blocks.sub: AEO now has a measurement layer — Semrush's free AI Search Visibility Checker (no login) scores you across ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity and shows the prompts where you're invisible
blocks.p: What changed: the answer-engine-optimization story we've tracked in growth just got its missing half — measurement. You can now check, in about a minute and for free, whether the AI engines name your brand. Semrush's free AI Search Visibility Checker takes just a domain (no sign-up) and returns an AI Visibility Score (0–100), total mentions, which platforms mention you (ChatGPT, SearchGPT, Gemini, Google AI, Perplexity), competitor comparison, the top prompts that trigger your brand — and, most usefully, the high-volume industry prompts where you're not mentioned. Deeper, paid tools do continuous tracking: Semrush's AI Visibility Index / AI Toolkit (built on a database of 200M+ LLM prompts) and Ahrefs Brand Radar track mention share, citations and share-of-voice across the major assistants. Why it matters for us: AEO advice is only worth acting on if you can see whether it worked — and buyers increasingly start with an AI answer, where studies show a large majority of brands are effectively invisible. This closes the loop: measure where you're absent, then run the AEO formatting pass on exactly those pages, then re-measure. It also resolves a call we logged Jun 8 — AEO has gone mainstream-measurable.
blocks.doIt: (1) Run the free check: open Semrush's AI Search Visibility Checker, enter your (or a client's) domain, hit Check Visibility, and read the 0–100 score + platform coverage. (2) Jump to the 'opportunities' — the high-volume prompts where competitors are named and you aren't; that's your prioritized target list. (3) For each gap prompt, apply the AEO formatting pass (intent-matched title → one-sentence answer up top → list/table → an original first-party stat → FAQ schema + last-updated date) on the most relevant money page (see card-growth-aeo-content-formats). (4) Re-run the check in ~2–4 weeks to confirm new mentions; for ongoing work, stand up continuous tracking in Ahrefs Brand Radar or Semrush's AI Toolkit. (5) Treat the score as directional — sample the actual AI answers by hand to see how you're described, not just whether.
blocks.note: Free-tool numbers are a sampled snapshot, not a full audit — cross-check by literally asking ChatGPT/Perplexity your target questions. Card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check — threaded with the answer-engine-optimization storyline (this is the 'measure' half to the AEO-formatting 'optimize' half).

sections.h: News — the landscape: the chip rout snaps back, the agent economy gets a brokerage account, and Anthropic locks $35B of compute
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: The AI-chip sell-off found a floor fast — a V-shape, not a regime change (so far)
blocks.p: What happened: after the Jun 3–6 rout — the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) fell roughly 10% on the week, Nvidia briefly dropped under a $5T market cap, AMD/Intel shed double digits — AI-chip names rebounded early the week of Jun 8, with Intel and Micron up sharply intraday (each ~+10% on a single session) as investors refocused on reaffirmed hyperscaler capital plans (~$750B of 2026 capex) and Nvidia's pending S&P 500 inclusion. Why it matters to us: this is the same thread we've carried since Jun 8 — the market is grading AI spend, not AI ambition — and the snapback says the 'spend' worry was a repricing, not a thesis break. Our open call (the sell-off is a dip, not a regime change; due ~Jul 20) is now leaning held. The practical read for client work is unchanged and reinforced: lead every AI pitch with cost-per-outcome and a spend ceiling — buyers are in scrutiny mode, but they're not abandoning the build.
blocks.note: Directional — intraday percentages move hour to hour and age fast (SOXX ~−10% week of Jun 3–6 and the early-week rebound corroborated across CNBC, Intellectia and Motley Fool; single-stock daily moves are snapshots). The durable fact is the V-shape + the why (a spend-scrutiny repricing that hyperscaler-capex reassurance reversed).

blocks.sub: Coinbase for Agents: your AI assistant can now trade and pay — point it at a sandbox, not your main account
blocks.p: What happened (Jun 11): Coinbase launched 'Coinbase for Agents' — a standalone tool that lets AI assistants like Claude and ChatGPT execute crypto trades and pay for services with financial autonomy, working through an MCP server. You can connect it to your main account or, importantly, have it operate in a separate sandbox account so the agent never touches your primary funds. It rides the open x402 payment protocol (built with AWS, Anthropic, Circle and Near), which lets an agent pay for premium research APIs and on-demand compute with no login or subscription. Why it matters: this is our 'the agent grew up — autonomy gained money' thread crossing from demo into a mainstream consumer brokerage. The pattern to copy isn't 'let an AI trade your crypto' (not our craft) — it's the safety scaffold: a scoped sandbox, an open metered-payment rail, MCP as the access boundary. That's the same 'corporate card, not a blank check' discipline we apply to any spending agent.
blocks.note: Confirmed across TechCrunch, CNBC, CoinDesk and SiliconANGLE (≥3 independent). The agentic-commerce sizing (~$8B in 2026) is a single projection — treat as directional. Not investment advice; flagged as a capability/landscape signal, not a trade.

blocks.sub: Anthropic locks $35B of TPU/data-center financing — compute is still the moat
blocks.p: What happened: Anthropic secured roughly $35B in debt financing to lease Google's custom TPUs across five US data centers (New York, Texas, Louisiana, Indiana). Apollo and Blackstone arranged the private-credit package via a special-purpose vehicle that buys the TPUs and leases them back; Google provides payment guarantees on the leased capacity and Broadcom adds residual-value guarantees on the chips — a layered backstop that de-risks the lenders. Why it matters to us: even with public AI equities just whipsawing, the hard money keeps flowing into compute + power — the binding constraint, not model cleverness. The structure (a lab leasing a hyperscaler's silicon, guaranteed by that hyperscaler and the chip designer) shows how tightly the leaders are now financially interlocked. It's a climate signal for where capability — and therefore the next wave of tooling and client demand — will concentrate.
blocks.note: Corroborated across Bloomberg and The Information (with Business Standard summarizing); the exact $35B and the tranche structure are as reported by those outlets. Advances the 'compute/power = the moat' thread.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, June 10
tldr: Dev play of the day: give your terminal AI real code intelligence. GitHub Copilot CLI now drives language servers (LSP) — so the agent gets IDE-grade semantics (go-to-definition into dependencies, find-all-references, project-wide rename, type resolution) instead of grepping and guessing. Setup is itself an AI Skill: install the LSP Setup skill, say "set up LSP for Python", run /lsp. Fewer hallucinated symbols, reliable renames, far less context burned.

Paid — read Meta's Opportunity Score before you touch a setting. Meta rolled its 0–100 Opportunity Score to all advertisers: a structural-health grade (creative variety, signal quality, audience breadth, conversion-event accuracy) that lets you triage an account in 10 seconds. And it halved the Advantage+ learning-phase bar to ~25 conversions/week (from 50) — so smaller accounts can finally run the unified flow. It grades setup, not results — keep judging on cost-per-purchase.

Social — write LinkedIn so AI search engines cite you. Semrush analyzed 89K LinkedIn URLs cited in AI answers (ChatGPT Search, Google AI Mode, Perplexity): LinkedIn is the #2 most-cited domain (behind Reddit), and the winners are original 500–2,000-word articles, posted consistently — not viral feed posts. LinkedIn is now a GEO surface; ~95% of cited content is original.

News — the AI-trade repricing rolls on. AI-chip stocks kept sliding Jun 10 (MU −4%+, AMD −3.5%, NVDA/INTC −2%+) on higher-for-longer rate fears + geopolitics — the market is grading AI spend, not ambition. Yet private capital is rotating into physical AI: Neura Robotics raised up to $1.4B (Tether-led, with Nvidia/Amazon/Qualcomm) at a reported ~$7B valuation.

News — Claude Fable 5 is public, with a retention catch. Anthropic's most capable model (~80.3% SWE-Bench) is now GA in GitHub Copilot, Bedrock and Databricks — but Fable-5-class models run under a new 30-day data-retention policy and existing Zero-Data-Retention agreements don't apply. Microsoft restricted it for employees over exactly this. Check which model your tools call before sending client-confidential data.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is verify the substrate before you trust the system — give the agent the real code graph (LSP) so it stops guessing; read the setup score before optimizing; confirm a model's data terms before you feed it secrets. Capability is necessary; provenance and ground-truth are what make it safe to ship.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: give your terminal AI real code intelligence (Copilot CLI + LSP)

blocks.sub: GitHub Copilot CLI can now drive language servers — ask it to 'set up LSP for <language>' and it stops guessing
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 2026): GitHub shipped a Language Server Protocol (LSP) integration for Copilot CLI, its terminal coding agent. Until now an agent in the terminal navigated your code by grep and heuristics — reading whole files into context and guessing at types. With a language server wired in, the agent gets the same precise, semantic understanding your IDE has: go-to-definition (including into third-party libraries), find-all-references, project-wide rename, type resolution across dependencies, and hover docs — returned as compact structured results instead of dumping entire files into the conversation. Setup is itself an AI Skill: GitHub ships an "LSP Setup skill" (a reusable markdown runbook with YAML frontmatter) that installs and configures the server for you, covering 14 languages. Why it matters for our builds: agentic/terminal coding is where a lot of AI dev work now happens (CI fixes, refactors, headless agents), and the #1 failure mode is the agent hallucinating a symbol or editing the wrong call site because it never really "saw" the code graph. LSP turns the agent from a fuzzy text-searcher into something that actually resolves your types — fewer wrong edits, reliable renames, and far less context burned reading files it didn't need.
blocks.doIt: (1) Install the LSP Setup skill: extract it to ~/.copilot/skills/ and restart Copilot CLI. (2) Ask in plain English — set up LSP for Java or enable code intelligence for Python — and the skill installs the right server (e.g. npm i -g typescript typescript-language-server, or brew install jdtls for Java) and writes config to ~/.copilot/lsp-config.json (user) or lsp.json (repo). (3) Run /lsp to confirm the server is up. (4) Test it: ask the agent to "find all references to X" or "rename X across the project" and watch it resolve into your dependencies. (5) Commit the repo-level lsp.json so every teammate's agent shares the same code intelligence.
blocks.note: It's the agentic-IDE version of a play we already keep — setup-as-a-Skill (a markdown runbook the AI loads on demand). Watch for Claude Code / Gemini CLI / Cursor to standardize an equivalent LSP hook (logged as a prediction). Card: card-webdev-copilot-cli-lsp.

sections.h: Paid — run it today: read Meta's Opportunity Score before you touch a single setting

blocks.sub: Meta's 0–100 Opportunity Score is now in Ads Manager for everyone — and Advantage+ now qualifies at ~25 conversions/week, not 50
blocks.p: What changed: Meta rolled out the Opportunity Score to all advertisers — a 0–100 grade on each campaign in Ads Manager for how closely your setup follows Meta's best practices across four dimensions: creative variety, signal quality (Pixel / Conversions API), audience breadth, and conversion-event accuracy. Crucially it's a structural health check, not a performance verdict — a 95 means well-built, not profitable. Separately, Meta halved the learning-phase bar for Advantage+ Shopping/Sales: campaigns now qualify at ~25 conversions/week, down from 50 — which makes the unified Advantage+ flow viable for far smaller budgets (this resolves the "thresholds reportedly easing" note we'd carried as directional since Jun 7). Why it matters for us: it's a 10-second triage before a call — open the account, sort by Opportunity Score, and the low scorers tell you exactly what's structurally broken (fragmented audiences, thin creative, a misconfigured Pixel) before you waste time eyeballing ROAS. And the lower conversion bar means clients you previously told "you don't have the volume for Advantage+" can now run it.
blocks.doIt: (1) In Ads Manager, add the Opportunity Score column (or open a campaign's overview) and triage — anything under ~60 has real structural gaps; aim for 80+. (2) Raise it the documented way: turn on Advantage+ audience, placements and creative; upload a deep, varied creative pool; fix tracking with the Conversions API. (3) Treat the score as a setup checklist, NOT a KPI — keep judging delivery on cost-per-purchase, and keep one small manual ad set as your control to prove the AI's lift. (4) For smaller accounts, re-evaluate Advantage+ Sales now that ~25 conv/week clears the learning phase.
blocks.note: Opportunity Score grades best-practice alignment, not results — a high score with weak creative still loses money. Treat the exact 25/week figure as directional; Meta's Help Center lags real behavior, so confirm on the account. Card updated: card-paid-meta-advantage-plus.

sections.h: Social — run it today: write LinkedIn so AI search engines cite you

blocks.sub: Semrush analyzed 89K LinkedIn URLs cited by AI — LinkedIn is the #2 most-cited domain, and original 500–2,000-word articles win
blocks.p: What changed: a new Semrush study (325,000 prompts across ChatGPT Search, Google AI Mode and Perplexity, Jan–Feb 2026) found LinkedIn is the #2 most-cited domain in AI answers, behind only Reddit — roughly 11% of AI responses cite a LinkedIn URL (ChatGPT Search 14.3%, Google AI Mode 13.5%, Perplexity 5.3%). It reverse-engineers what gets cited: articles dominate (50–66% of cited LinkedIn content; feed posts 15–28%), with 500–2,000-word articles cited most; educational/advice content is 54–64% of citations; cited posts had only moderate engagement (15–25 reactions — relevance beats virality); ~75% of cited authors post frequently (5+ posts / 4 weeks); and ~95% of cited content is original (reshares are just 5%). Why it matters for us: buyers increasingly ask ChatGPT/Perplexity "who's good at X" — and the answer is being pulled from LinkedIn. That makes LinkedIn a Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) surface, not a vanity feed: a client's or partner's LinkedIn presence is now a discovery channel into AI answers, and it's winnable with craft, not ad spend.
blocks.doIt: (1) List the 3–5 questions a buyer would ask an AI in your space ("best [service] for [use case]", "how to [outcome]"). (2) For each, publish a LinkedIn article (not just a feed post) of 500–2,000 words that answers it directly — lead with a clean one-sentence answer, then expand with original POV + your own data. (3) Post consistently — cited authors averaged 5+ posts in 4 weeks; you don't need virality, moderate relevant engagement is enough. (4) Reuse assets: paste a blog post or talk transcript into Claude and ask for a LinkedIn article in your voice (see the repurpose-with-Claude card), then add first-party numbers. (5) Spot-check: ask ChatGPT/Perplexity your target question and see whether you (or a competitor) get named — iterate.
blocks.note: This is the social half of the AEO/GEO story we've tracked in growth — same principle (structure original, authoritative answers so a model can lift and attribute them), now on LinkedIn. Card: card-social-linkedin-ai-citations (threaded with the AEO content-formats card).

sections.h: News — the landscape: the AI-trade repricing rolls on, robots get $1.4B, and Fable 5 ships with a retention catch
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: AI-chip stocks keep sliding — the 'grade the spend' regime, not a one-day dip
blocks.p: What happened (Jun 10): the AI-chip complex kept falling — premarket, MU −4%+, AMD −3.5%, NVDA and INTC −2%+ — extending last week's drop (the Nasdaq shed ~4% on a hot jobs report that revived "higher-for-longer" rate fears), with added pressure from US–Iran geopolitical headlines. Jensen Huang reiterated that AI-chip demand stays strong "for years." Why it matters to us: this is the same thread we flagged Jun 8 — the market is now grading AI spend, not AI ambition. Our open call (the sell-off is a dip, not a regime change; due ~Jul 20) is still live and still under pressure. The practical read for client work: lead every AI pitch with cost-per-outcome and a spend ceiling (see the AI-gateway spend-limit card) — buyers are in scrutiny mode.
blocks.note: Directional — intraday percentages move hour to hour and age fast (premarket Jun 10 via TipRanks; last week's Nasdaq −4% via Yahoo Finance). The durable fact is the direction + the why (rates + caution), corroborated across sources.

blocks.sub: Capital is rotating into embodied AI: Neura Robotics raises up to $1.4B
blocks.p: What happened: German humanoid-robotics maker Neura Robotics announced a Series C of up to $1.4B at a reported ~$7B valuation — said to be the largest round ever for a full-stack robotics company — led by Tether with Nvidia, Amazon, Qualcomm, Bosch, Schaeffler and the European Investment Bank participating (the full amount is milestone-contingent). Robotics startups have raised $55.8B in 2026 YTD (Dealroom), nearly double last year's record. Why it matters: even as public AI equities sell off, private capital is pouring into physical AI — the money is rotating from "LLMs" toward embodied/robotics and the power + compute to run it. A signal for where the next wave of tooling and client demand shows up, not a trade.
blocks.note: Confirmed across CNBC, Tech.eu and Neura's own release; the ~$7B valuation is from a single anonymous source — treat as reported. "Up to $1.4B" is milestone-contingent, not all upfront.

blocks.sub: Claude Fable 5 is public — but it carries 30-day data retention, and Microsoft restricted it internally
blocks.p: What happened: Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly (Jun 9) — its most capable model, ~80.3% on SWE-Bench — now generally available in GitHub Copilot, AWS Bedrock and Databricks. The catch for teams handling sensitive data: Fable-5-class models run under a new 30-day data-retention policy (classifiers retain prompts/outputs for 30 days, flagged items up to two years), and existing Zero-Data-Retention (ZDR) agreements do NOT apply to Fable 5 traffic. Microsoft has restricted Fable 5 for its own employees — it's pulled from the internal Copilot model picker while legal reviews the change, while other Claude models that still run under ZDR remain available. Why it matters to us: this is the "two gates" rule in the wild — a model can pass the capability/cost gate (Fable 5 is excellent) and fail the data-provenance gate. If you or your tools route client-confidential material through Fable 5 expecting zero retention, that assumption is now wrong.
blocks.doIt: Before pointing any client-data workflow at Fable 5: (1) check which model your tool / Copilot / IDE is actually calling (Fable 5 may be a new default in some pickers); (2) for confidential client material, prefer a Claude model still covered by ZDR (or another ZDR/enterprise path) until terms are confirmed; (3) read Anthropic's Mythos-class retention page and confirm your enterprise agreement's coverage in writing.
blocks.note: Reported via The Verge/Reuters (Microsoft restriction) + Cybernews + Anthropic's own Help Center (retention terms) — corroborated. Card: card-ai-tooling-fable5-retention.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, June 9
tldr: Design play of the day: draw dividers between grid/flex items with one line of CSS — no more pseudo-element hacks. Chrome 149 (stable, Jun 2) shipped CSS Gap Decorations: the old column-rule now works inside Grid and Flexbox, and there's a new row-rule — both paint lines in the gap with the familiar width style color shorthand. It's decorative, so it's pure progressive enhancement: unsupported browsers just show an empty gap. Ship it to production today.

Graphic — stop fighting AI typo-soup: prompt Ideogram 4.0 with JSON, not a sentence. Ideogram's new open-weight 4.0 (Jun 3) was trained on structured JSON captions, so you declare your headline copy, exact hex palette (up to 16), and each element's bounding box as data — and the in-image text actually comes out spelled right (~0.97 OCR). Templatable: swap the text + colours, keep the layout.

Email — wire Omnisend into Claude as an MCP connector and audit your whole program in plain English. Omnisend shipped a hosted MCP server (mcp.omnisend.com/mcp); add it to Claude as a custom connector and ask "rank my last 30 days of campaigns by revenue and flag the three with the worst deliverability" against your live account — no exports, no spreadsheet. Connect it in ~5 minutes.

Dev — patch-and-rotate Tuesday: your AI coding toolchain is the attack surface. Two same-day (Jun 8) threats hit dev teams directly: password-stealing malware that fires when certain compromised Microsoft OSS repos are opened inside Claude Code / Gemini CLI / VS Code, and a one-character Linux nf_tables kernel bug (CVE-2026-23111) with a now-public local-root exploit. Confirm your kernels are on the post-Feb-5 patch and rotate any tokens used in AI coding apps.

News — Apple's WWDC "sell the news," and the EU pries WhatsApp open. Apple slid ~2–3% after unveiling a new Gemini-assisted Siri/Apple Intelligence architecture with no firm ship date (directional). The EU ordered Meta to restore free WhatsApp Business API access to rival AI chatbots within 5 working days (or face fines up to 10% of global turnover) — the bloc's first interim antitrust measure in 17 years. And Google shipped Gemini 3.5 Live Translate (near-real-time speech-to-speech, 70+ languages).

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is declare your intent as data, then let the system run — a JSON design brief, an MCP query over your live numbers, a CSS rule that degrades on its own. The same discipline that makes those compound is what keeps you safe: know exactly what your AI tools can touch, and rotate the keys when they're breached.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: grid & flex dividers in one line of CSS

blocks.sub: Chrome 149 shipped CSS Gap Decorations — column-rule/row-rule now work in Grid & Flexbox
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 2, stable): Chrome and Edge 149 shipped CSS Gap Decorations. The column-rule property — which for years only worked in multi-column layouts — now paints lines inside a Grid or Flexbox gap, and there's a brand-new row-rule for horizontal lines. Both take the same one-line shorthand you already know from columns: width style color (e.g. 1px solid #ddd). Why it matters for our builds: every design system carries the same scar tissue — separators between cards, a sidebar/content split, rows in a dashboard or pricing table — today faked with extra <div>s, ::before/::after pseudo-elements, or background-gradient tricks that shatter the moment items wrap or the count changes. This deletes all of it. And because the rules are purely decorative, it's pure progressive enhancement: browsers that don't support it just render an empty gap, so there's zero-risk to ship it to production now.
blocks.doIt: On any grid/flex container that already has a gap, add the lines directly: .cards { display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(3,1fr); gap:2rem; column-rule:1px solid #ddd; row-rule:1px solid #ddd; }. Want lines only between items (not trailing past the last one when a row is short)? Add column-rule-visibility-items: visible / row-rule-visibility-items: visible. Width, colour and inset all animate, so a hover flourish is one transition: .cards{transition:column-rule-color .3s} .cards:hover{column-rule-color:#3b82f6}. No @supports guard needed.
blocks.note: Honest caveat: as of now this is Chromium-only (Chrome/Edge 149) — not yet Baseline-wide. Use it strictly as decorative enhancement, never as a load-bearing layout element, until Safari/Firefox follow. A polyfill is reportedly in development.

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: JSON-prompt your layouts in Ideogram 4.0

blocks.sub: Ideogram 4.0 is trained on structured JSON — declare the headline, the hex palette and each element's box
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 3): Ideogram shipped 4.0, its first open-weight (9.3B-param) text-to-image model. The headline for designers isn't "prettier pictures" — it's that the model was trained exclusively on structured JSON captions, so you prompt it like a design brief, not a sentence. You hand it a JSON object with: typed text layers (the literal string to render + a separate styling description — so multi-line in-image text actually comes out spelled right, ~0.97 OCR accuracy, best of any open-weight model); per-element bounding boxes on a 0–1000 grid ([y_min, x_min, y_max, x_max], origin top-left) so the headline, subhead and logo land exactly where you place them; and colour-palette conditioning — up to 16 hex codes globally, 5 per element. Why it matters: it kills the two most annoying failure modes of AI image gen for real design work — garbled in-image text and "it ignored my layout." Declare the copy, the position and the brand hexes as data and it's repeatable: swap the text + color_palette fields and you've got a templated pipeline for posters, packaging, signage or a 50-card set.
blocks.doIt: Generate at ideogram.ai (or the API at developer.ideogram.ai) with a JSON object, not prose. Use the trained schema: a top-level high_level_description, a style_description (with color_palette = your brand hexes), and a compositional_deconstruction whose elements array carries each {type:"text", bbox:[…], text:"GOLDCREST", desc:"bold serif caps, centered top"}. Keep the key order intact (type → bbox → text/desc) — out-of-order keys degrade quality. Iterate by editing only the wrong field (nudge a bbox), not by re-describing the whole image. To produce a series, script the text + color_palette swaps over one fixed layout.
blocks.note: Licence caveat for client work: the open weights are non-commercial, so the local/ComfyUI route is for prototyping only — run commercial output through the ideogram.ai web app or API (commercial tiers). The web-app JSON workflow is the safe run-it-today path.

sections.h: Email — run it today: query your live email program in plain English via MCP

blocks.sub: Omnisend shipped a hosted MCP server — connect it to Claude and audit campaigns/deliverability without exports
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 4): Omnisend shipped a hosted MCP (Model Context Protocol) server at https://mcp.omnisend.com/mcp. Point an MCP-capable AI client (Claude or ChatGPT) at it and the model can read your live Omnisend data — campaign performance, deliverability diagnostics, subject-line insights, automation-revenue breakdowns, form/subscriber data — and answer in natural language. Why it matters: this kills the export-to-spreadsheet loop that eats an email operator's morning. Instead of clicking through dashboards, you ask "rank my last 30 days of campaigns by attributed revenue and flag the three with the worst deliverability, with one fix each" and get an answer grounded in your real account. You can also stand up a zero-code lifecycle trigger ("summarise each day's new form subscribers into a Gmail draft") without touching Zapier. It's the same "controls collapse into AI defaults" move we've tracked in paid and dev — now in the ESP.
blocks.doIt: Connect it in ~5 minutes: (1) in Claude, open the left menu → Customize → Connectors; (2) click + → Add custom connector, name it Omnisend, paste https://mcp.omnisend.com/mcp, click Add; (3) select it → Connect and complete the Omnisend OAuth sign-in (no API key to copy); (4) in a new chat: "Use the Omnisend connector — give me an account snapshot, rank last 30 days of campaigns by revenue, and flag any with deliverability issues plus one fix each." Always review AI-drafted sends before they go.
blocks.note: The native one-click Claude connector is "coming soon" — the runnable path today is the custom-connector method above. Watch for Klaviyo / Mailchimp / HubSpot to ship their own ESP MCPs next (logged as a prediction).

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: patch-and-rotate your AI coding toolchain
sections.intro: A scan promoted to a beat: two same-day (Jun 8) threats land squarely on dev teams, and both have a concrete action.

blocks.sub: Compromised Microsoft OSS repos drop password-stealers when opened inside AI coding tools
blocks.p: What happened: researchers (Cloudsmith, OpenSourceMalware) flagged a re-compromise of Microsoft open-source projects — 70+ repos were disabled — where password-stealing malware fires when an affected repo is opened inside an AI coding environment (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, VS Code). Why it matters to us: the threat model has shifted. "Don't run untrusted code" used to mean executing it; now merely opening a poisoned repo in an agentic IDE — which may auto-run tasks, read env files, or execute setup scripts — is enough to exfiltrate the tokens and secrets sitting in your AI dev session. Our AI toolchain is now part of the attack surface.
blocks.doIt: Today: (1) audit recent pulls/clones of Microsoft/Azure OSS repos (the Durable Task project was named); (2) rotate any credentials, API keys or tokens that have been present in a Claude Code / Gemini CLI / VS Code session that touched untrusted repos; (3) treat "open a repo in an agentic IDE" as code execution — clone unknown repos into a sandbox/devcontainer with no live secrets first.

blocks.sub: A one-character Linux kernel bug (CVE-2026-23111) now has a public local-root exploit
blocks.p: What happened: a single errant character in the Linux kernel's nf_tables netfilter code (CVE-2026-23111, CVSS ~7.8) allows a local attacker to escalate to root. It was patched upstream on Feb 5, but on Jun 8 a fully weaponised, reportedly >99%-reliable exploit was published — which sharply raises the urgency for anything still unpatched, especially multi-tenant and container hosts. Why it matters: if you run your own VPS, build runners, or container infra, an unpatched kernel is now a one-step local-root for anyone who lands a foothold.
blocks.doIt: Confirm your distro kernels are on the post-Feb-5 patch level (Debian, Ubuntu and RHEL all issued advisories) and prioritise multi-tenant/container hosts; a quick uname -r + your distro's security tracker tells you if you're exposed.
blocks.note: Directional on the exploit reliability figure (the vendor's own claim); the durable facts are the patched-Feb-5 fix and the Jun-8 public exploit — both corroborated across security press.

sections.h: News — the landscape: WWDC "sell the news," the EU pries WhatsApp open
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: Apple slides after a Siri-AI reveal with no ship date — and a Gemini assist
blocks.p: What happened: at WWDC (Jun 8) Apple unveiled a re-architected Siri / Apple Intelligence — reportedly built in collaboration with Google's Gemini models — but without a firm consumer ship date, and the stock did the classic "sell the news," sliding ~2% on Jun 8 and drifting toward ~3% lower on Jun 9. Why it matters to us: Apple is now being priced as an AI company (P/E re-rated well above its historical norm), and the tell for anyone building on Apple platforms is strategic — Apple is willing to lean on a direct competitor's model to close its AI gap. That reshapes which on-device vs cloud AI capabilities you can assume in client work over the next year.
blocks.note: Directional: the intraday percentages move across the two sessions and age fast (sourced ~1.9% Jun 8 via Yahoo Finance coverage, >3% Jun 9 via GuruFocus). The "Gemini powers Siri" detail is reported (Bloomberg/Business Standard) but not stated in writing by Apple — treat as reported, not confirmed.

blocks.sub: EU orders Meta to reopen WhatsApp to rival AI chatbots — its first interim antitrust measure in 17 years
blocks.p: What happened: EU regulators ordered Meta to restore free WhatsApp Business API access to competing AI chatbots (the order names rivals including Poke/The Interaction Company and France's Agentik) within 5 working days, or face fines up to 10% of global annual turnover, while it probes whether Meta abused its market power by blocking them. Meta says it will appeal. Why it matters: WhatsApp is a primary customer-comms channel in much of the world; if third-party AI assistants get guaranteed API access, building or recommending an AI chat agent on WhatsApp becomes materially less platform-risky — a real opening for marketing/CX work.
blocks.doIt: If a client's CX or lifecycle plan has been waiting on "can we even run an AI assistant on WhatsApp without Meta cutting us off?", flag this as the regulatory wind shifting in your favour — but scope it as emerging until the appeal resolves.
blocks.note: Reported via Reuters (carried on Yahoo Finance) + PYMNTS; the 5-working-day window and 10%-turnover ceiling are the EU's stated terms.

blocks.sub: Also shipped: Google's Gemini 3.5 Live Translate (near-real-time speech-to-speech, 70+ languages)
blocks.p: What shipped: Google released Gemini 3.5 Live Translate — continuous speech-to-speech translation that auto-detects 70+ languages, available now in the Gemini Live API + AI Studio (public preview), the Google Translate apps, and Meet (enterprise preview), with SynthID watermarking. Why it matters: real-time multilingual is now an API call, which lowers the bar for multilingual creator content, localized support, and live-event workflows — a capability to keep in the back pocket for any team shipping to non-English audiences.
blocks.note: Confirmed via Google's official blog.google announcement.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Monday, June 8
tldr: Dev play of the day: put a hard dollar cap on your AI bill. Cloudflare's AI Gateway just shipped spend limits — set a real budget per model, app, team or user, and when it's hit, requests either stop or quietly fail over to a cheaper fallback model so nobody's workflow breaks. Open beta, every plan, ~20 minutes to wire up.

News — the market is repricing the AI trade. A soft AI-chip forecast from Broadcom (it guided ~$16B vs the ~$17.2B Street wanted, stock −12%) triggered a rotation out of AI names: on Jun 8 in Asia, SoftBank fell ~6%, Samsung ~10%, SK Hynix ~8%, dragging the Kospi down as much as 8%. The takeaway for us isn't to trade it — it's that AI spend is now under a microscope, which is exactly why today's dev play matters.

Growth — new research says AI answer engines cite some formats far more than others. Two 2026 datasets (HubSpot's State of AEO + Wix Studio's AI Search Lab, 1M+ citations) found only three formats clear a 65% citation rate: product/landing pages (68.5%), blog posts (~67%) and listicles (66%) — and pages win citations when they pair an intent-matched title (What is X / X vs Y / How to X / Best X) with original stats, a visible last-updated date, and an FAQ. Reformat one money page today.

Also building (news): fusion startup Helion raised a $465M Series G at a $15.5B valuation (nearly 3× its last round) to commercialize fusion this decade — the clearest sign yet that the bottleneck behind the AI trade is power, not just chips.

Sharpen your edge: the throughline across all three — the market is now grading AI spend, not just AI ambition. Govern your own before a platform (or a budget review) does it for you: cap the bill, and make every page you publish earn its citation.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: a hard dollar cap on your AI spend

blocks.sub: Cloudflare AI Gateway now enforces budgets in dollars, not just request counts
blocks.p: What changed (Jun 5): Cloudflare's AI Gateway added spend limits — cost-based budgets that track real cumulative dollar spend (computed from each model's token pricing) and act when you cross the line. This is different from rate limiting: rate limits cap how many requests; spend limits cap how much money. Why it matters for our builds: the #1 fear shipping anything LLM-backed — a chatbot, a RAG feature, an agent — is a runaway loop or a traffic spike quietly burning four figures overnight. A real ceiling turns 'we daren't ship AI to production' into 'ship it, it can't cost more than $X.' It lands the same week the market is punishing loose AI spend (see the news beat) — controlling your unit economics is suddenly a feature, not an afterthought.
blocks.doIt: Wire it up in ~20 minutes: (1) route your app's LLM calls through an AI Gateway endpoint (you proxy your OpenAI/Anthropic/etc. calls through Cloudflare); (2) in the gateway's settings (dashboard or API) add a spend limit and scope it — by model, provider, or a custom attribute like user/team/app (e.g. $2,000/mo for senior engineers, $500/mo for everyone else, or $50/day on one expensive model); (3) pick a window — daily/weekly/monthly, fixed (resets 1st of month / Monday / midnight) or rolling; (4) choose the over-limit behavior: block by default, or use a Dynamic Route to fail over to a cheaper fallback model so a hard cap doesn't kill the workflow. Start with a generous monthly cap on your highest-cost model and tighten from real numbers.
blocks.note: It's open beta on all plans as of Jun 5. Treat the cost figures as Cloudflare's own pricing math — reconcile against your provider's invoice for the first month before you trust the cap to the dollar.

sections.h: News — the AI trade gets repriced; the bottleneck is power
sections.intro: Trust-critical domain — figures cross-checked against a 2nd source and marked directional where fast-moving.

blocks.sub: A soft Broadcom forecast rotated money out of AI names
blocks.p: What happened: Broadcom's Q2 FY26 print was strong on paper — revenue ~$22.2B, up ~48% YoY on AI demand — but its forward AI-semiconductor guide of ~$16B came in under the ~$17.2B analysts wanted, and the stock fell ~12% after hours (reported Jun 3). That miss-on-expectations, plus 'higher-for-longer' rate worries after a hot U.S. jobs print, sparked a rotation out of AI-linked equities. By Jun 8 in Asia it had spread: SoftBank −~6%, Samsung Electronics −~10%, SK Hynix −~8%, with chip-heavy Korea's Kospi down as much as ~8% (those two names are 40%+ of the index). Why it matters to us: not as a trading tip — as a climate signal. The era of 'spend anything on AI and the market cheers' is pausing; budgets, unit economics and provable ROI are back in fashion. That reframes how clients will scrutinize the AI features we build and pitch this quarter.
blocks.note: Directional: intraday index/stock moves shift hour to hour. The durable fact is the rotation and the why (a guidance miss + rates), corroborated across CNBC and the Broadcom earnings coverage — not any single percentage.

blocks.sub: Where the money still flooded in: power for compute (Helion's $465M)
blocks.p: Counter-signal, same week: fusion-energy startup Helion raised a $465M Series G at a $15.5B post-money valuation (nearly 3× its prior round; ~$1.5B raised to date), led by Thrive Capital, to push toward commercial fusion this decade — its Polaris prototype became the first privately funded machine to run on deuterium-tritium fuel. Read alongside the week's other infra bets (PhysicsX's $300M Series C at $2.4B for AI-designed hardware; Nvidia–SK Hynix and Nvidia–LG memory/data-center pacts), the throughline is clear: investors are cooling on AI application valuations while still pouring into the physical bottleneck — power and memory. The compute story is becoming an energy story.
blocks.doIt: Operator takeaway: when you scope an AI feature for a client this quarter, lead with cost-per-outcome and a spend ceiling (see today's Cloudflare play), not raw capability — that's the framing a repricing market now rewards, and it's what will get budget approved.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: format a page so AI answer engines actually cite it

blocks.sub: New research on 1M+ AI citations shows which formats win — and the recipe to copy
blocks.p: What changed: two independent 2026 studies — HubSpot's State of AEO 2026 and Wix Studio's AI Search Lab, analyzing 1M+ AI citations between them — quantified which on-page formats answer engines (AI Overviews, Gemini, ChatGPT, Perplexity) cite most. Only three formats clear a ~65% average citation rate: product/landing pages (68.5%), blog posts (~67%), and listicles (66%); comparison pages sit just behind (~63%) but win ChatGPT specifically. Why it matters: AI search is now a real discovery channel, and citations — being named as the source inside the AI answer — are starting to matter more than classic backlinks for visibility. The lever isn't 'write more,' it's structure what you have so a model can lift a clean, attributable chunk.
blocks.doIt: Pick one page that should be winning AI mentions and reformat it in ~30 minutes: (1) match the title to the query intent the engines reward — What is X, X vs Y, How to X, or Best X; (2) lead each section with a direct one-sentence answer, then expand (engines lift the first clean sentence); (3) convert dense prose into a list or comparison table — pre-chunked, easy to extract; (4) add original/first-party stats (your own benchmark or survey number) — proprietary data is what gets attributed to you; (5) add an FAQ block with FAQ schema, a visible last-updated date, and an author bio. Then check whether ChatGPT/Perplexity cite you for the target question, and iterate.
blocks.note: Match format to the query's intent, the strongest predictor in the Wix data: informational questions cite articles ~2.7× more, while commercial ('best…', comparison) questions cite listicles for ~40% of citations. AEO isn't a separate site — it's a formatting pass on pages you already have; start with your highest-intent money pages, not the blog archive.

reports.title: Weekly Briefing — Issue #3
tldr: The week in one idea: the controls collapsed into AI defaults — across model vendors, ad platforms, and the browser — so the edge moved from operating the controls to governing the system. Every domain that moved this week told the same story: the manual lever is being retired; the win is setting the system up once and keeping one guarded control to prove the AI's lift.

AI/all — build your personal AI operating system: a two-gate model router, a 5-task frozen eval you can run on any new model in an hour, a persistent-memory loop, a private local model (Ollama), and grounded doc Q&A (NotebookLM). Five dailies this week were really one system — stand it up.

Paid — your SOPs are out of date this week. Meta merged Manual + Advantage+ into one Sales flow (ASC is gone) and Google auto-links your YouTube channel after Jun 10 unless you opt out. Two assignable actions below.
Design + Dev — two free, zero-risk upgrades: Apple-smooth squircle corners in one line of CSS (corner-shape), and teach Copilot your conventions once with .github/copilot-instructions.md.
Social — the ranking lever moved: Instagram now distributes on sends per reach (private DM shares), not likes. Brief for the forward, measure Sends.

Sharpen your edge: the connective thread — never hand the AI the wheel without a control. A manual ad set, a frozen eval, a reviewed memory merge, a graceful CSS fallback: each one is how you trust the default without being trapped by it.

sections.h: The week in one idea: the controls collapsed into AI defaults

blocks.sub: Stop operating the levers — start governing the system
blocks.p: Pull the week's moves together and they rhyme. Meta retired the standalone Advantage+ Shopping campaign and made AI-on the default Sales flow. Google is auto-linking YouTube and pushing AI Max into more formats. Model vendors shipped persistent memory (ChatGPT 'dreaming') and ~100×-cheaper open weights so the model itself is becoming a commodity default. Even the browser shipped a 'premium' visual default (squircle corners) for one line of CSS. The manual lever — building the audience by hand, picking the one model, re-prompting your conventions every time — is being retired across crafts.
blocks.note: Synthesis, not recap: the five dailies this week weren't five unrelated tips. They were one shift seen from five disciplines.

blocks.sub: So the operator edge moved up a level
blocks.p: When the platform does the optimizing, operating the controls is no longer the skill — governing the system is. That means: choose and judge the model (router + standing eval), give it memory and your conventions, design your inputs for the signal the AI now ranks on (the DM share, a deep creative pool), and — the non-negotiable — keep one guarded manual control to prove the AI's lift on YOUR work before you trust it. Set it up once; verify; then let it run.
blocks.doIt: This week's homework, in one sentence each: stand up a model router + frozen eval (AI), add a repo conventions file (dev), ship one squircle (design), fix your Meta SOPs + the Jun 10 YouTube check (paid), rewrite one brief for sends (social). Details below.

sections.h: What changed, and what to do — by domain
sections.intro: One tight synthesis per domain that actually moved this week. Quiet domains (graphic, email, growth) are skipped on purpose.

blocks.sub: AI tooling — five dailies that are really one system
blocks.p: The week's AI plays compound into a personal operating system, not a pile of tips: Ollama for free/private work your data can't leave the laptop for; a two-gate model picker (capability/cost, then data/jurisdiction); a release-day verdict kit — 5 frozen real tasks you re-run on any new model to get a yes/no in an hour; a persistent-memory loop (distill each session to JSON, re-inject next time) so you stop re-explaining yourself; and NotebookLM for grounded, cited Q&A over your own documents.
blocks.doIt: Pick the two with the highest leverage and do them this week: (1) write your model router — one row per recurring task, the cheapest model that passes a quick eval, re-checked monthly; (2) freeze 5 of your real tasks as a scored set so the next model launch is a one-hour decision, not a week of vibes. The rest (memory loop, Ollama, NotebookLM) layer on top.
blocks.note: Why it compounds: models keep getting cheaper and more interchangeable, so the durable value isn't picking today's winner — it's the harness that lets you swap winners safely.

blocks.sub: Web dev — teach Copilot your conventions once
blocks.p: The same 'govern the default' move shows up in code: instead of correcting the AI's framework, naming, and test style in every file, you encode the rules once and the assistant follows them on every request.
blocks.doIt: Add .github/copilot-instructions.md at your repo root with plain-English rules (framework/versions, naming, test library, hard always/never lines). Commit it; Copilot Chat attaches it to every request in that repo. Keep it short and concrete — vague rules get ignored.
blocks.note: This is the dev version of the persistent-memory play: state your context once, reuse it forever.

blocks.sub: Web design — Apple-smooth corners, free and zero-risk
blocks.p: Chromium shipped the CSS corner-shape property (Chrome 139+, ~67% of users), so the iOS 'squircle' super-ellipse is now native CSS — no clip-path/SVG hack. Because border-radius still sets the size and unsupported browsers fall back to normal rounding, it's pure progressive enhancement.
blocks.doIt: On any button/card you already round, add the keyword next to the radius: .btn { border-radius: 28%; corner-shape: squircle; }. Dial it precisely with corner-shape: superellipse(1.5). No @supports guard needed — Safari/Firefox degrade gracefully.
blocks.note: The keeper principle: gracefully-degrading CSS primitives can ship the day they hit one engine — lead with them.

blocks.sub: Paid — your playbook is out of date as of this week
blocks.p: Two platform changes break existing SOPs. Meta merged Manual + Advantage+ into one unified creation flow; standalone 'Advantage+ Shopping' (ASC) is gone, renamed Advantage+ Sales with AI on by default and legacy APIs deprecating. Google expanded AI Max to Shopping + travel and will auto-link your YouTube channel to Google Ads after Jun 10 unless you opt out.
blocks.doIt: (1) Audit every SOP/onboarding doc that says 'create an Advantage+ Shopping campaign' — the new path is Sales objective with the three Advantage+ levers left on; keep one small manual ad set as a 2–3 week control to prove lift. (2) Before Jun 10: Google Ads → Tools → Linked accounts → review the pending YouTube link and confirm or unlink.
blocks.note: Treat Meta's exact learning-phase / ROAS numbers as directional — the Help Center lags real behavior; prove it on your account.

blocks.sub: Social — distribution follows the private share now
blocks.p: Instagram unified its top-line metric to 'Views' and the strongest distribution signal is now sends per reach — how often people DM your post to a friend. A private forward is louder evidence the post is worth showing to strangers than a like or even a comment.
blocks.doIt: Rewrite the brief for the forward: a single screenshot-able takeaway, a 'send this to the person who needs it' framing, the hook in the first line/frame, and a soft CTA to share to a specific person. Then judge posts by the Sends count in Insights, not likes.
blocks.note: Saves still win for evergreen/utility content — but for reach, a send beats a save beats a like.
