reports.title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, June 7
tldr: Web play of the day: real iOS-style squircle corners in one line of CSS. Chrome's corner-shape property finally lets you replace the slightly-boxy border-radius look with Apple-smooth super-ellipse corners — and it falls back to normal rounding everywhere else, so you can ship it today with zero risk.

Paid: Meta quietly retired the standalone 'Advantage+ Shopping' campaign. Manual and Advantage+ are now one unified flow under the Sales objective with AI on by default — if your playbook still says 'create an ASC campaign,' it's out of date. Exact new steps below.

Social: Instagram's #1 ranking signal in 2026 is the DM share (sends-per-reach). Likes are noise; if people forward your Reel to a friend, Instagram treats it as proof it's worth pushing to strangers. Design the post for the send, not the like.
Also moved (paid): Google's AI Max expanded to Shopping + travel and Google Ads will auto-link your YouTube channel after Jun 10 unless you opt out — worth a 2-minute check.

Sharpen your edge: when you ship the squircle CSS, keep border-radius on the element too — corner-shape only changes the shape of the curve, not its size, so without a radius there's nothing to reshape.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: Apple-style squircle corners in one line

blocks.sub: Replace the boxy border-radius look with true super-ellipse corners
blocks.p: What changed: Chromium shipped the CSS corner-shape property (Chrome 139+, ~67% of users as of May 2026), so the iOS app-icon 'squircle' — the smooth super-ellipse Apple uses everywhere — is now native CSS instead of a clip-path/SVG hack. Why it matters for our builds: rounded rectangles are the single most-repeated shape in any UI (buttons, cards, modals, avatars), and the squircle reads as 'premium' the way Apple's do — for one extra line. Because border-radius still controls the size and unsupported browsers just render a normal rounded corner, it's pure progressive enhancement: zero risk to ship now.
blocks.doIt: On any button/card you already round, add the shape keyword next to the radius: .btn { border-radius: 28%; corner-shape: squircle; }. Want to dial the curve precisely? Use the math function — corner-shape: superellipse(1.5) is a softer squircle, higher n = squarer, lower = rounder. Keep a real border-radius on the element (the shape needs a size to act on). Test in Chrome/Edge; Safari/Firefox fall back to your normal rounding automatically — no @supports guard needed.
blocks.note: Don't strip your existing border-radius when you add this — corner-shape with no radius does nothing.

sections.h: Paid — what changed this week

blocks.sub: Meta merged manual + Advantage+ into one flow — 'create an ASC campaign' is dead
blocks.p: What changed: in its Feb 2026 Ads Manager overhaul Meta merged the separate 'Manual' and 'Advantage+ Shopping' options into a single unified campaign-creation flow, with AI-driven optimization on by default; the old standalone Advantage+ Shopping Campaign (ASC) was renamed Advantage+ Sales and its legacy APIs are being deprecated. Why it matters: any SOP, screenshot, or onboarding doc that says 'choose Advantage+ Shopping' now points at a menu that no longer exists — and juniors will silently build the wrong thing.
blocks.doIt: New cold-traffic ecommerce setup: (1) New campaign → Sales objective; (2) leave the three Advantage+ levers ON — Advantage+ budget, Advantage+ audience, Advantage+ placements (that is the old ASC, now the default); (3) feed it a deep, varied creative pool and iterate on creative, not audiences; (4) cap existing-customer spend in settings so budget skews to new buyers; (5) keep one small manual ad set as a 2–3 week control. Meta's global tests showed Advantage+ Sales at ~32% higher ROAS / ~17% lower CPA vs manual-only — but prove it on your account before reallocating.
blocks.note: Learning-phase thresholds are reportedly easing for smaller budgets, but Meta's Help Center lags real behaviour — treat any exact conversions-per-week number as directional, not gospel.

blocks.sub: Google AI Max expands + auto-links your YouTube after Jun 10
blocks.p: What changed (Google Marketing Live 2026): AI Max — the AI-driven Search add-on — is expanding to Shopping and travel formats with a new 'AI Brief' to steer messaging, and Google will auto-link your YouTube channel to your Google Ads account after Jun 10, 2026 unless you act. Why it matters: the auto-link unlocks organic video metrics and lets you build remarketing audiences from past viewers — useful, but it's an opt-out, so decide on purpose rather than by default.
blocks.doIt: Two-minute check: in Google Ads → Tools → Linked accounts, review the pending YouTube link before Jun 10 and confirm it's the right channel (or unlink). If you run Search at scale, switch on AI Max for one campaign as a test and compare CPA against your current setup before rolling it out.

sections.h: Social — the technique: design for the DM share, not the like

blocks.sub: Instagram's strongest 2026 ranking signal is sends-per-reach (private shares)
blocks.p: What changed: Instagram has unified its primary metric to 'Views' across Reels, Stories, photos and carousels — and the signal that most drives whether a post escapes your followers is now sends per reach: how often people DM your content to a friend. Likes and even comments are weaker tells; a private share is the algorithm's clearest evidence the post is worth showing to strangers. Why it matters: most of our social briefs still optimise for likes/saves. The lever moved — so should the brief.
blocks.doIt: Build the share trigger into the post on purpose: (1) make the takeaway forwardable — a single screenshot-able tip, a 'send this to the person who needs it' framing, an inside-joke or stat someone would DM to a colleague; (2) put the hook in the first line/frame so it survives the feed; (3) add a soft CTA to share to a specific person ('tag the teammate who still uses border-radius'); (4) check the Sends count in Insights, not just likes, and make more of whatever gets forwarded.
blocks.note: Saves still matter for evergreen/utility content, but for reach, a send beats a save beats a like.

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 6
tldr: Play of the day: give your AI a memory that survives across chats. ChatGPT and Claude now run background memory 'dreaming' for you — but you can run the same trick by hand in about 20 minutes and stop re-explaining your context every session.
Everything else on Jun 6 (lab funding, a policy proposal, model launches) was industry news, so it's cut. The one durable, run-it-today technique is below, with the exact prompt.

sections.h: Run it today: a memory that persists across sessions

blocks.sub: Stop re-explaining yourself to ChatGPT / Claude every time
blocks.p: Payoff: continuity without re-pasting your background each chat — answers that already know your tools, your style, and where you left off. OpenAI shipped this as built-in memory and 'dreaming' (OpenAI — ChatGPT memory); you don't have to wait for it, and you can run the same loop in any tool today.
blocks.doIt: Three steps, ~20 minutes. (1) Spin up a workspace per ongoing effort so chats and files share one memory (ChatGPT Projects, or a Claude Project). (2) At the end of a session, paste the distillation prompt below and save its JSON. (3) At the start of the next session, paste that JSON back in first. That's the whole technique.
list: The distillation prompt (copy verbatim): Curate my long-term memory. From this conversation output STRICT JSON with keys: preferences (my habits, tools, formatting, no-go's — only if repeated), projects (name + 1-line status + next step), open_questions, corrections (mistakes you made + the fixed behavior), drop (anything from earlier memory I contradicted). Rules: never invent; if a field has no evidence return []. Be terse.
Inject the JSON, not the transcript, next session — you get the continuity without burning your context window.
Merge, don't overwrite: each cycle, ask it to fold the new JSON into the old one, applying drop first. The curation loop is the point, not the storage.
Keep names / employers / health / finances out of auto-merge — review those by hand. Treat memory edits like a database change: reviewable and reversible.

reports.sources: OpenAI — ChatGPT memory ('dreaming') · OpenAI — Projects in ChatGPT · Anthropic — Effective context engineering

sections.h: Run it today: judge any new model in an hour

blocks.sub: Turn 'should we switch?' from a vibe into a same-day decision
blocks.p: Payoff: when a new model drops, you swap one config line, run your frozen tests, and know by lunch whether it beats your current default — instead of re-architecting on a rumor.
blocks.doIt: Keep 5 of your real tasks as a fixed, scored set (build it once — see May 18). The day a model ships: (1) point your harness at the new model; (2) re-run the 5 tasks; (3) score against the same rubric; (4) sanity-check live price and speed on Artificial Analysis; (5) switch only if it wins on quality-per-dollar for that task class. Promptfoo runs the whole set side-by-side and diffs the outputs for you.
blocks.note: Rule of thumb: never reprice your stack on a vendor benchmark before it reproduces on your own tasks.

reports.sources: Promptfoo (run an eval set across models) · Artificial Analysis (price / speed / quality)

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, June 4
tldr: Play: turn your own documents into a grounded, cited Q&A source with NotebookLM. Stop scrolling PDFs and reports — upload them and ask questions answered only from your sources, with citations you can click.

sections.h: Run it today: a private research assistant grounded in your docs

blocks.sub: Ask your documents questions instead of re-reading them
blocks.p: Payoff: fast, trustworthy answers pulled only from material you uploaded — no open-web hallucination — with inline citations back to the exact passage. Ideal for contracts, research bundles, meeting notes, or a competitor's whitepapers.
blocks.doIt: Open NotebookLM (free, Google account). Create a notebook and upload your sources (PDFs, Google Docs, pasted text, a website or YouTube URL). Ask in plain language — What are the renewal terms? or Summarize the risks across all three docs — and every claim links to its source. Use the Audio Overview to turn the set into a podcast-style briefing.
blocks.note: Because it answers only from your sources, it's far less likely to make things up than a general chatbot.

reports.sources: Google NotebookLM

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, June 3

sections.h: Run it today: a two-gate model picker

blocks.sub: Separate 'can it do the job' from 'should it, here'
blocks.p: Payoff: you get to use the cheapest capable model for most work without accidentally piping regulated or client-confidential data somewhere you can't defend. One decision, written down, instead of an accidental one.
blocks.doIt: Write two gates into your model-router doc. Gate 1 — capability/cost: does it pass your eval at an acceptable price? (compare on Artificial Analysis / OpenRouter). Gate 2 — data/jurisdiction: where does the request run, where do the weights live, and is this data OK to send there? A model can pass Gate 1 and fail Gate 2 for a given feature — route accordingly (cheap model for public/low-stakes, a vetted provider for sensitive).
blocks.note: Make it explicit per task class, not per app — that's the granularity that actually holds up.

reports.sources: Artificial Analysis (model comparison) · OpenRouter (providers behind one API)

sections.h: Run it today: teach Copilot your project's rules once

blocks.sub: Stop fixing the same Copilot mistakes in every file
blocks.p: Payoff: suggestions that already use your framework, naming, test style, and 'never do X' rules — fewer rewrites and more consistency across the team.
blocks.doIt: Create .github/copilot-instructions.md at your repo root. Write plain-English rules: language/framework versions, naming conventions, your testing library, formatting, and hard always/never rules (e.g. always use async/await, never callbacks; validate all inputs with zod). Commit it. Copilot Chat now attaches it to every request in that repo — expand the References on a reply to confirm it loaded. Docs: GitHub — repository custom instructions.
blocks.note: Keep it short and concrete; vague rules get ignored. Path-specific rules go in .github/instructions/.

sections.h: Run it today: a private, free LLM on your laptop

blocks.sub: Use AI on confidential text without sending it to anyone
blocks.p: Payoff: no API bill, no rate limits, and full privacy for the work you can't paste into a cloud chatbot — contracts, client data, internal docs. Good enough for summarizing, drafting, classifying, and code help on a modern laptop.
blocks.doIt: Install Ollama (Mac/Windows/Linux, free). In a terminal run ollama run llama3.2 (or qwen2.5, gemma3) — it downloads once, then runs locally. Chat in the terminal, or point any app at the built-in local API at localhost:11434. Pick a small model (1–8B) for speed; a bigger one if you have the RAM.
blocks.note: Match model size to your RAM (an 8B model needs ~8GB free). For the hardest 10% of tasks, still reach for a frontier model — see the model router (May 30).

reports.sources: Ollama — run open models locally

reports.title: Weekly Briefing — Issue #2
tldr: Two techniques from this week are worth keeping — the rest was news. Agent 'wallets', funding rounds, and org-chart moves dominated headlines; what survives as run-it-today craft is below.
Keep #1 — element-level citations: kill made-up sources in any research/RAG workflow with one prompt line (or a native citations feature).
Keep #2 — planner → worker → verifier: the reliable shape for getting an AI to check its own work before you trust it.

sections.h: Techniques worth keeping

blocks.sub: Kill hallucinated sources with element-level citations
blocks.p: Payoff: research and RAG answers you can actually trust, because every claim points at an exact span you can click — not a vague 'according to the docs'.
blocks.doIt: Two ways. In any chat, add this line to your prompt: For every claim, cite the exact source span (document + page/line). If you can't locate a span, label the claim 'unverified' instead of asserting it. For production, use a feature that returns spans natively (Anthropic — Citations) so the pointers are guaranteed valid, not model-guessed.

blocks.sub: Planner → worker → verifier (make the AI check itself)
blocks.p: Payoff: fewer confidently-wrong outputs. One model plans, specialized workers each do one job, and an explicit verifier checks the result against a hard bar (a test, a rubric) before it reaches you.
blocks.doIt: Don't let one chat do everything — that's where quality drops. Split the job: ask for a plan first, run the steps, then run a separate pass — now critique this against [rubric/tests] and list what fails. Pattern and tradeoffs: Anthropic — Building effective agents.

sections.h: This week's action list
checklist: Add the element-level citation line to your default research prompt.
On your most important AI task, add a separate verifier pass before you trust the output.
If you build RAG, evaluate a native citations feature so source pointers are guaranteed valid, not guessed.

reports.sources: Anthropic — Citations · Anthropic — Building effective agents

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, May 26
tldr: Play: generate on-brand mockups, icons, and social images with ChatGPT's image tool — and edit them in plain English. Concept art and asset drafts in minutes, no design suite.
The day's enterprise-platform and multimodal launches were news; the durable, run-it-today design move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: design assets from a sentence

blocks.sub: Draft visuals without opening Photoshop
blocks.p: Payoff: fast first-draft mockups, hero images, icons, and ad variations — with legible text in the image and transparent backgrounds on request — that you can hand off or polish.
blocks.doIt: In ChatGPT, ask for an image: A clean flat-style icon set for a budgeting app, 6 icons, consistent line weight, transparent background. To revise, just describe the change (make the palette warmer, add the headline FALL SALE) or select a region and edit only that part. Iterate, then download. How-to: OpenAI — Images in ChatGPT.
blocks.note: Spin up cheap variations for A/B options; always confirm you have rights to any referenced style before commercial use.

reports.sources: OpenAI — Images in ChatGPT

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Monday, May 25
tldr: Play: run non-urgent bulk AI jobs at ~50% off with the Batch API. Translating, classifying, or summarizing thousands of items? Submit them as one batch and pay half.
The day's price-cut, profitability, and power-merger headlines were news; the durable, run-it-today cost move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: halve the cost of bulk AI work

blocks.sub: Pay full price only when you need the answer now
blocks.p: Payoff: a 50% discount on large, non-interactive jobs — tag 10,000 reviews, translate a product catalog, generate metadata for a media library — with most batches finishing within an hour.
blocks.doIt: Instead of looping one-at-a-time API calls, submit them together via the Message Batches API (OpenAI has an equivalent Batch API). Bundle your requests into one batch, submit, poll for completion, then collect the results. Use it for anything where 'within an hour' is fine — evals, moderation, bulk content, data enrichment.
blocks.note: Keep real-time calls for user-facing work; route everything async to batch and the savings compound.

reports.sources: Anthropic — Message Batches (50% off bulk jobs)

reports.title: Weekly Briefing — Issue #1
tldr: The durable lesson of the week: learn the agent runtime, not the headline model. Three techniques still pay off today — writing Skills, curating context, and forcing a planning phase.
Highest-leverage move: turn one repetitive workflow into a reusable 'Skill' — a markdown runbook your AI follows — instead of re-prompting it every time.
Model launches and funding were news; the techniques below are what compound.

sections.h: Techniques worth keeping

blocks.sub: Write a Skill: a runbook your AI follows, so you stop re-prompting
blocks.p: Payoff: encode a procedure once; the AI loads it and follows it every time — same quality without you re-explaining. It's an onboarding doc for an agent.
blocks.doIt: Pick one recurring task. Write a short markdown file: when to use it, the exact steps, which tool to use when, when to stop, and 2–5 examples of the output you want. Drop it in as a Skill (Anthropic — Agent Skills; in Claude Code, Claude Code · Skills). Structure it with clear sections — models follow delineated structure far more reliably.

blocks.sub: Context engineering beats prompt engineering
blocks.p: Payoff: better answers from stronger models by giving them exactly what they need, not everything you have. Treat the context window as finite and precious.
blocks.doIt: Before a big task, curate the inputs: the 2–3 docs that matter, the relevant code, the goal — and leave the rest out. More context isn't better; the right context is. Primer: Anthropic — Effective context engineering.

blocks.sub: Force a planning phase before generation
blocks.p: Payoff: practitioners report complex-coding success jumping from roughly a third to two-thirds just by making the model plan first and splitting work across single-job sub-agents.
blocks.doIt: Add to your prompt: First write a step-by-step plan and wait for my approval before doing anything. For big jobs, give each sub-task its own session/sub-agent so one chat isn't polluted doing everything. Patterns: Anthropic — Building effective agents.

sections.h: This week's action list
checklist: Convert one recurring workflow into a Skill (markdown runbook + 2–5 examples).
Add a 'plan first, wait for approval' line to your agent prompts; split big jobs into sub-agents.
Wire up 1–2 MCP servers to your AI tool (see the May 19 play).
Curate context deliberately on your next big task — the right inputs, not all of them.

reports.sources: Anthropic — Agent Skills · Claude Code · Skills · Anthropic — Effective context engineering · Anthropic — Building effective agents · Model Context Protocol

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, May 24
tldr: Play: paste a screenshot and get the code — or the fix. Hand a UI mockup, an error screen, or a broken chart to a vision model and let it write the markup or diagnose the bug.
The day's price-cut and tool-availability headlines were news; the durable, run-it-today vision move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: turn a picture into working code

blocks.sub: Skip the typing — show, don't tell
blocks.p: Payoff: a screenshot of a design becomes HTML/CSS in one step; a screenshot of a stack trace or broken chart becomes a diagnosis and a fix — faster than describing it in words.
blocks.doIt: In Claude or ChatGPT, drag in the image and ask: Rebuild this screen as a responsive Tailwind component or Here's the error — what's wrong and how do I fix it?. For repeatable use, send images via the API. Capabilities and limits: Anthropic — Vision.
blocks.note: Great for converting a Figma export or a whiteboard photo into a first-pass build you then refine.

reports.sources: Anthropic — Vision (image input)

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, May 23
tldr: Play: do research with Perplexity, not a plain chatbot — every answer comes with clickable sources. Get a synthesized answer plus the citations to verify it, in one step.
The day's research-milestone and IPO headlines were news; the durable, run-it-today research move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: research that shows its work

blocks.sub: Answers you can actually check
blocks.p: Payoff: a fast, current, synthesized answer to a real question — market sizing, a how-to, a comparison — with numbered citations so you can confirm each claim instead of trusting a confident guess.
blocks.doIt: Go to Perplexity (free). Ask a specific question, then read the cited sources before you rely on it. Use Focus to scope the search (e.g. academic), and ask follow-ups to drill in. Treat the citations as the deliverable — open them.
blocks.note: For anything you'll publish or decide on, verify at least the load-bearing numbers against their linked sources.

reports.sources: Perplexity — cited answer engine

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Friday, May 22
tldr: Play: run an AI-assisted security pass on your code before you ship. A free scanner plus a focused LLM review catches the obvious holes a poisoned dependency or rushed PR introduces.
The day's supply-chain-breach and model-launch headlines were news; the durable, run-it-today security move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: a security review on your diff

blocks.sub: Catch the leak before it ships
blocks.p: Payoff: fewer hard-coded secrets, injection bugs, and risky dependency calls reaching production — a cheap safety net for solo devs and small teams without a dedicated security person.
blocks.doIt: Two layers. (1) Run Semgrep (free) on your repo to scan for known vulnerable patterns, secrets, and dependency issues. (2) Paste your diff into Claude/ChatGPT with: Review this diff only for security issues — injection, auth, secrets, unsafe deps. List concrete risks and fixes; say 'none found' if clean. Fix what either flags.
blocks.note: Especially worth it right after adding a new dependency or IDE extension — treat third-party code as untrusted.

reports.sources: Semgrep — code security scanning

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, May 21
tldr: Play: level up your AI mental models with Andrej Karpathy's free deep-dives — then use AI to study them. An hour with his explainers teaches you more than a week of headlines.
The day's talent-and-capital headlines were news; the durable, run-it-today learning move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: actually understand how LLMs work

blocks.sub: Invest an hour in the fundamentals
blocks.p: Payoff: judgment that compounds with every new model — once you understand tokens, context, and training, you stop chasing hype and start using AI well. The best free resource comes from the field's clearest teacher.
blocks.doIt: Watch Karpathy's free explainers (start with 'Intro to Large Language Models', then 'Deep Dive into LLMs') linked from karpathy.ai and his YouTube. To lock it in, paste the video transcript into a chatbot and ask it to quiz you, or to re-explain any part like I'm a marketer, not an engineer.
blocks.note: Skills that survive model upgrades — orchestration, evals, judgment — beat memorizing this week's leaderboard.

reports.sources: Andrej Karpathy — free AI explainers

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, May 20
tldr: Play: write prompts that survive a model switch. Platforms add and drop models weekly — a well-structured prompt ports across ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini so you're never locked in.
The day's predictions and platform-turbulence headlines were news; the durable, run-it-today prompting move is below.

sections.h: Run it today: a prompt that works anywhere

blocks.sub: Don't hard-couple your work to one model
blocks.p: Payoff: when a model gets removed, repriced, or beaten, you paste the same prompt into the next one and it just works — no rewrite, no lock-in.
blocks.doIt: Structure every important prompt the same way: a clear role, the task, the rules/constraints, the context (marked with headers or XML-style tags), and the exact output format — plus 1–2 examples. This structure is portable across providers. Reference: OpenAI — Prompt engineering guide (the same principles apply to Claude and Gemini).
blocks.note: Keep your best prompts in a plain-text file so you can drop them into whichever model wins this month.

reports.sources: OpenAI — Prompt engineering guide

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, May 19
tldr: Play: wire one MCP server into your AI tool so it acts on your real data. The pattern of the week was that every lab now ships a runtime, not just a model — and the durable skill is connecting your AI to your tools.
The keynote announcements were news; the run-it-today move is the MCP connection below.

sections.h: Run it today: connect your AI to your tools with MCP

blocks.sub: Stop copy-pasting between your AI and your apps
blocks.p: Payoff: your assistant can read a Google Drive doc, open a GitHub PR, or pull a Slack thread directly — instead of you ferrying text back and forth. MCP is 'a USB-C port for AI apps': connect once, use everywhere.
blocks.doIt: Pick one tool you touch daily (GitHub, Drive, Slack, a database). Add its MCP server to your AI client (Claude, Cursor, and VS Code all support MCP). Start read-only / scoped, confirm it fetches real data, then let it take one low-risk action. Directory and how-to: modelcontextprotocol.io; in Claude Code: Claude Code · MCP.
blocks.note: For sensitive data, scope permissions tightly and prefer local/VPC execution over sending data to a vendor cloud.

reports.sources: Model Context Protocol · Claude Code · MCP

reports.title: Daily Briefing — Monday, May 18
tldr: Play: build a standing eval set — a few real tasks you score by hand. Before a big launch week, the cheapest edge isn't reading every teaser; it's having a fixed test so you can judge any new model objectively the day it ships.
The day's infrastructure-deal headline was news; the durable move is the golden-task set below — the foundation the May 30 router and Jun 5 release kit build on.

sections.h: Run it today: a golden-task eval set

blocks.sub: Replace 'this new model feels better' with a number
blocks.p: Payoff: an objective, repeatable yes/no on any model — coding, writing, extraction — instead of vibes you can't defend to your team or your budget.
blocks.doIt: Pick 2–3 tasks you actually do (e.g. 'refactor this file', 'summarize this contract', 'extract these fields'). For each, write the input and what a great answer looks like (a short rubric or expected output). Save them. Now any model gets the same test and a comparable score. Promptfoo runs the set across models and diffs the outputs; sanity-check price and speed on Artificial Analysis.
blocks.note: Re-run it whenever a new model ships — that's the May 30 router and the Jun 5 release-day kit in action.

reports.sources: Promptfoo · Artificial Analysis
